text: The player pays close attention to who ends up with high or low payoffs and how these payoffs arise. They respond to others’ outcomes primarily through the lens of contribution behavior rather than via envy or profit-maximizing strategies. High payoffs that are accompanied by solid contributions draw rewards or at least no sanctions. High or protected payoffs for those who contribute very little evoke punishment, even when those targets are already incurring substantial sanctions from others. This suggests that the player cares about relative justice and not just their own payoff difference; they want chronic low contributors to end up worse off, consistent with a strong norm-enforcing disposition. They do not systematically punish players who spend heavily on punishment, nor do they visibly protect players who are victims of substantial punishment if those victims’ contributions are low or ambiguous. In contrast, they sometimes reward or leave untouched those who cooperate steadily even if those players face outside punishment, showing that their reference point is contribution, not punishment history. Across the game, their decisions imply that they interpret others’ aggregate outcomes (contributions plus sanction flows) as feedback about who respects or violates the cooperation norm and shape their own sanctions and rewards accordingly, but they remain largely insensitive to attempts by others to manipulate the social landscape through extreme punishment spending.

text: The player is repeatedly on the receiving end of deductions and occasional small rewards, despite remaining a high contributor. They do not visibly adjust their use of punishment or reward in response; they keep ignoring both tools. Their contribution level is also surprisingly robust to being sanctioned unjustly: after being targeted, they continue to contribute substantially and do not counter-defect or attempt to recoup losses via lower giving. They also observe episodes where some players burn large amounts of coins to punish or reward others, leading to dramatic payoff differences, but they do not imitate this behavior. Overall, their reaction to others’ outcomes is muted and conservative: they register that the environment is noisy and sometimes hostile, yet they stick with a straightforward cooperative strategy and do not join the escalation. This suggests a type who is resilient to provocation, reluctant to personalize sanctions, and focused on the underlying social surplus rather than on redistributive side-games.

text: They visibly experience that some members contribute nothing while others give the maximum, and that at various times some players spend substantial amounts on rewards. Despite this, they do not adjust either their contribution or reward behavior. They keep contributing fully even when free-riders reliably earn more in those rounds, and they refrain from rewarding even when others spend large sums on rewards that benefit them. There is no evidence of retaliation, conditional cooperation, or gratitude expressed through the available mechanisms or through chat. They also do not join the group in norm talk or in singling out suspected free-riders or bots. In effect, they register others’ outcomes enough to make informed decisions (since they continue to choose and confirm contributions), but they choose a fixed, internally anchored strategy rather than one that reacts to these observed payoffs and costs. Their type in new PGG environments is best characterized as a silent, principled full cooperator who ignores both exploitative behavior and prosocial reward behavior when deciding how to act.

text: They are attentive to others’ contribution histories and to patterns of free‑riding versus generosity. They publicly track who they see as non‑contributors and voice suspicion about them, sometimes aligning with others’ calls to “target” low contributors if a mechanism becomes available. Their comments show that they monitor not only contributions but also the oddity of someone giving large rewards while often not contributing, which they interpret as possibly non‑human or experimenter behavior. They interpret the overall interaction as a kind of social/behavioral experiment and respond with humor rather than frustration, even when inequality is obvious. The repeated free‑riding by some players does not lead them to reduce their own contributions, but it does affect how they talk about those players: mildly adversarial in words, cooperative in actions. They show no explicit emotional reaction to others’ payoffs as such; their focus is more on contribution patterns and “who’s playing fair” than on the raw payoff distribution. Overall, they are a stable cooperator who uses social commentary and group joking to deal with exploitation rather than adjusting their material strategy.

text: They closely track others’ behavior at the outcome level and adjust their actions accordingly, but entirely through contribution/reward choices rather than through talk. When others are consistently contributing, they are willing to join full cooperation at times, and they substantially reward those who do so, supporting a belief that high group contributions improve everyone’s earnings and are worth reinforcing. When some others defect or contribute less, they still tend to reward broadly, suggesting they prefer maintaining group cohesion over punishing or excluding low contributors. They show no direct hostility toward those who free-ride on the public good; instead of withdrawing rewards from suspected defectors, they generally keep them within the reward net. They do not react visibly when other players complain about “greedy” or “suspect” participants or when they themselves are labeled as under-contributing. This indicates a tolerance for being seen as exploitative and a focus on the aggregate payoff environment rather than on interpersonal approval. Overall, they treat others’ high contributions as something to be incentivized and sustained with rewards, while others’ criticism or speculation about their motives has no observable effect on their behavior.

text: The player monitors others’ contributions and seems attuned to who is benefiting from free-riding versus who is cooperating. Early on, they explicitly mark low contributors as greedy and talk about wanting mechanisms to remove or punish them, revealing sensitivity to payoff asymmetries and a desire to correct them via social control rather than by changing their own contribution. They notice patterns of zero contributions and join discussions about which avatars are “sus” or possibly bots, using outcome histories as evidence. Over time, they soften somewhat toward at least one low-contributing avatar once that player explains technical limitations on contributing; they subsequently stop publicly targeting that avatar and instead shift suspicion toward a consistently silent, high-earning participant, whom they hypothesize might be a researcher. They keep track of their own total coins and infer that a silent low-contributor (or low-contribution history) is likely accumulating much higher earnings; they mention this explicitly but still do not retaliate via behavior changes. Overall, they interpret others’ outcomes through a fairness and experimental-lens: they dislike inequity, want institutional tools to address it, are willing to call it out socially, but continue to cooperate and refrain from spending to either reward or punish.

text: The player pays attention to patterns in others’ contributions and implicitly to their resulting payoffs, but they do not adjust either contributions or reward behavior in response. They notice when multiple players contribute zero and note that those players are active, interpreting low contributions as intentional free-riding rather than inattention. They support the idea that low contributors are fair game for sanction if a mechanism becomes available, aligning with others who view these players as “greedy” or suspicious. They also track the group’s evolving cooperation structure, recognizing who tends to contribute and who does not, and they occasionally comment on specific avatars they believe are exploiting the system. They show some curiosity about total coin earnings and approximate duration/round structure, and they compare their own accumulated coins to others when this topic is raised. However, despite observing that defectors accumulate higher payoffs in some phases, they do not retaliate, withdraw cooperation, or attempt to counterbalance via rewards. Their reactions are primarily verbal/normative (calling out that everyone is active, remarking on who is free-riding) rather than behavioral adaptation. Overall, they respond cognitively and communicatively to others’ outcomes but keep their own action pattern fixed.

text: They show limited explicit reaction to others’ payoffs or reward behavior. They do not comment on who gains or loses, who is spending heavily on reward, or which players are systematically free-riding, even when other participants discuss this openly and target specific individuals. Their main social response to being singled out as “sus” is to restate that their non-contributions are driven by lack of available options, not choice. They neither threaten future behavior nor adjust their contributions in visible retaliation; instead, when they can contribute, they continue to cooperate fully, even after being associated with another persistent free-rider. They appear unconcerned with the fairness implications of others’ free-riding or with the heavy reward expenditures of one player, at least in terms of expressed attitudes. They also do not react to being occasionally rewarded, aside from continuing their pre-existing pattern of full contributions when possible and zero otherwise. Overall, they are behaviorally responsive to the choice set they perceive (whether they can contribute) but largely unresponsive—in terms of both communication and strategy—to others’ payoff profiles, rewards, or moral judgments.

text: The player shows no observable behavioral adjustment in response to others’ contribution patterns, reward expenditures, or payoff levels. They maintain full contributions regardless of fluctuating group cooperation or the presence of obvious free riders. They also do not start rewarding others after receiving rewards themselves; there is no visible reciprocity via the incentive mechanism. They do not increase or decrease contributions to “punish” low contributors or to reward high contributors; their contribution level is invariant. There is likewise no chat response to others’ outcomes or to discussions about who is contributing, who is “greedy,” or who might be a bot or researcher. From their action pattern, they appear largely indifferent—at least behaviorally—to the distribution of others’ payoffs, focusing instead on a fixed personal rule (“always contribute fully”) and on retaining all residual coins by never rewarding. Their learning or adaptation to observed outcomes is minimal to nonexistent: they persist with the same strategy from start to finish.

text: They pay close attention to others’ contribution patterns and inferred earnings, and they comment explicitly on who seems to be free‑riding and who is accumulating large totals. They repeatedly single out particular low contributors as “greedy” or “sus” and question statements from those players that attribute non‑contribution to technical limitations, indicating skepticism and a tendency to attribute selfish motives. They also explicitly infer that heavy rewarders who do not contribute are anomalous and possibly bots or researchers, pointing out that a “human” in their view would simply withhold contribution rather than burning coins on rewards. This reveals a mental model of typical human incentives and a willingness to reason about others’ payoff structures and hidden roles. Despite this vigilance and moral judgment, their behavioral response is non‑retaliatory at the contribution stage: they continue to fully cooperate. They express a desire for sanctioning power and a willingness to target low contributors if given the chance, but in the absence of punishment they do not try to counteract free‑riders through reduced contributions or targeted rewards. Emotionally, they mix mild frustration and suspicion with amusement and curiosity, maintaining cooperative behavior while intellectually dissecting others’ outcomes and treating the situation as both a fairness problem and an experimental puzzle.

text: They observe clear patterns of free-riding and of others spending heavily on rewards, but their observable behavioral response is muted. They continue to contribute fully through repeated cycles where certain players contribute nothing and where one player spends substantial amounts on rewards. They do not adjust their own contributions downward in a systematic way to punish defectors or exploit rewarders. In chat, they briefly align with the group’s moral framing that low contributors are “greedy” and agree that everyone should contribute, but they do not escalate into direct calling-out or targeting specific individuals, even as others do so. They also do not comment on the large costs some players incur to reward cooperators. There is no evidence that they try to exploit those rewarders intentionally; rather, they maintain cooperative contributions throughout, using none of their own payoff to either reinforce or counteract reward patterns. They show mild interest in aggregate earnings when the group starts comparing totals, and they signal that they are doing slightly better than another cooperative player, suggesting they track their own outcomes and possibly enjoy being marginally ahead. However, there is no sign they change behavior because of these comparisons. Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is to stay on a stable, cooperative path, accepting both exploitation by defectors and windfalls from generous rewarders without attempting to correct, punish, or strategically take advantage of these dynamics. Their type is best captured as a stable high contributor who upholds cooperation personally, ignores the reward tool, and reacts to others’ payoff choices more with quiet acceptance than with strategic adaptation.

text: They clearly monitor others’ contributions and outcomes, but their behavioral reaction is muted and somewhat inconsistent. They align verbally with group members who condemn low contributors and explicitly support the idea that everyone should contribute fully. They also express suspicion that some low contributors may not be genuine participants, which suggests they try to resolve free‑riding either as intentional greed or as exogenous constraint/bug. This indicates that they think in terms of “types” of players (cooperators, greedy free‑riders, inactive/bugged participants). They notice that one player distributes substantial rewards while under‑contributing, and they observe, along with others, that this pattern is unusual and hard to rationalize. However, they do not push that line of inquiry or adjust their own behavior in any clearly targeted way toward that player; they neither publicly favor them nor clearly retaliate through contributions or rewards. They are sensitive to how others interpret their own record: when other players claim to see them as a chronic non‑contributor, they immediately contest that view. This shows that others’ beliefs about their past choices matter to them, and they are attentive to how the visible history of contributions affects reputation. Despite all this, there is no strong evidence that they systematically condition their contributions or rewards on individual others’ past payoffs or reward costs. Their contribution shifts do not map cleanly onto specific partners’ actions, and their reward behavior remains mostly flat despite clear visibility of who has been generous or stingy. The main observable adaptation is reputational: when called out, they defend their image, but they do not dramatically alter their pattern of play in a traceable, contingent way. Overall, they track the social and payoff environment, care about how they are perceived, but respond with light adjustments and occasional opportunistic free‑riding rather than tightly calibrated reciprocity.

text: They track outcomes and others’ behaviors closely enough to identify persistent low contributors as “freeloaders” and to recognize when some players are getting high payoffs without contributing much. This triggers explicit expressions of frustration and talk of switching to zero, grounded in a fairness and reciprocity concern. They align with another high contributor in complaining about being exploited and briefly support the idea of punishing free-riders by removing contributions. Later, they moderate this stance, arguing that even with freeloaders, cooperating is still better than being purely selfish, reflecting a move from retaliatory fairness toward pragmatic efficiency. They do not appear to single out specific individuals for sustained criticism beyond general labels; instead, they increasingly categorize low contributors as likely bots or random number generators, which psychologically distances them and reduces the urge to respond punitively. This reframing lets them justify continuing to contribute fully while mentally separating cooperative “real humans” from exploitative or nonresponsive players. They also congratulate the group when contributions rise and use positive reinforcement (“good job,” “we’re getting there”) to respond to improved collective outcomes. Overall, they are sensitive to payoff inequities and initially reactive, but they ultimately reorient toward maintaining cooperation and morale rather than systematically adjusting their own contributions in response.

text: Direct verbal reactions to specific payoff outcomes or contribution levels are largely absent. The player does not comment on who is “losing” or “gaining” more, does not praise cooperators, and does not explicitly criticize freeloaders (including themselves). However, their behavioral responses show that they track others’ contributions and the payoff structure well: they repeatedly choose low contributions exactly in those contexts where others are giving a lot and thus making free-riding profitable. They show no behavioral attempt to punish or discipline low contributors, despite commenting lightly about bots and themselves as a “bot.” In practice, they consistently behave like the exploitative side of the cooperators–freeloaders dynamic that others complain about. When some players threaten to stop contributing because of freeloaders, this player does not adjust by contributing more to salvage the public good; instead, they continue with low or zero contributions. Their few statements referencing “bot” behavior suggest they understand that others see non-contributors as socially undesirable or non-human, but they treat this more as a humorous theme than as a reputational threat to correct. They appear insensitive or indifferent to the normative pressure that others express about fairness and reciprocity. Overall, this player type is: a socially aware but norm-indifferent free-rider who participates in group banter with ironic, bot-themed humor, tracks incentives well, rarely follows through on cooperative talk, and maintains low contributions even in the face of strong cooperative norms and end-game appeals.

text: The player sees all payoffs and contribution patterns and appears to internalize that some players are repeatedly contributing very little while others appeal for high trust and full contributions. Their own behavior shifts in ways consistent with quietly exploiting cooperative others: after periods where “all 20” norms are loudly promoted, they sometimes reduce their own contributions while others maintain high ones, gaining relatively high payoffs in those instances. This indicates that they attend to others’ outcomes enough to recognize exploitable situations, even if they do not talk about it. They do not verbally criticize freeriders or defend them, nor do they signal resentment or moral judgment based on outcome inequities. Instead, their limited chat content focuses on structure (possibility of more strategic coordination channels) and on the nature of the environment (speculating about bots). This suggests that, while they notice outcome patterns, they process them analytically rather than emotionally: others’ over- or under-earnings become information about how to adjust their own strategy, not triggers for punishment rhetoric or solidarity. Their persona, therefore, is that of a quiet, calculating participant who updates contributions based on observed payoffs but avoids explicit commentary on who “deserves” what.

text: They pay attention to outcome information and visibly connect realized payoffs and contributions to broader ideas about trust, logic, and reward. Early on they explicitly question whether contributing everything is wise after seeing that some participants contribute very little yet earn high payoffs. Later, they acknowledge that people who contribute fully might “deserve to be rewarded for their faith” and occasionally follow through by contributing high even in the presence of free-riders. At multiple points they comment that others do not follow logical incentives, and they entertain the idea that some accounts are bots or non-serious actors, especially those who either press for full contributions or refuse to contribute. text: This player passively observes others’ contributions, payoffs, and reward behaviors but does not respond through the available monetary channel. They see clear patterns: some players consistently give high amounts and also spend heavily on rewards; others contribute very little but sometimes receive substantial side-payments. Yet the focal player never joins either pattern—neither the “moralist/rewarder” camp nor any apparent reciprocal-reward circles. Their own contributions adjust over time in ways that are plausibly informed by others’ behavior, suggesting they do pay attention to outcomes, but their response is confined to adjusting their own contribution level, not to shaping others via reward. They do not appear to punish or sanction indirectly (e.g., they do not systematically drop contributions to the lowest levels when they observe free-riding or seemingly excessive reward expenditures); instead, they continue to contribute at roughly moderate-to-high levels while ignoring the side-payment layer. This combination implies a somewhat detached, payoff-focused stance: they are willing to cooperate in the public good as long as it remains beneficial, but they do not emotionally or normatively engage with who is “deserving” in terms of extra payoff or who is overspending on others. Their attitude toward others’ reward costs and outcomes is largely observational and non-interventionist. Any inferences about their internal beliefs (e.g., whether they approve or disapprove of others’ high reward spending) are unknown; all that is clear is that such observations do not translate into active monetary responses.

text: The player closely tracks who earns more via defection and who bears or avoids the cost of sanctions, but they respond selectively. Early in the game, when one or two individuals free-ride and thereby earn more than consistent contributors, the player does not immediately adjust their contributions or deploy punishment. This suggests initial tolerance or a wait-and-see strategy: they do not react to every payoff asymmetry. Over time, however, a specific peer who repeatedly benefits from lower contributions becomes the focal target of sanctions. When that peer free-rides alongside other defectors, this player singles them out for punishment while ignoring others, indicating that their response is history-dependent and personalized rather than purely based on the current round’s outcome. Later, even after that peer’s outcomes return to parity through full contributions, the player still invests heavily to reduce that peer’s payoff, showing that past behavior weighs more heavily than present contributions. The player’s own payoffs drop sharply in the punishment rounds, yet they do not subsequently compensate by free-riding more often or by punishing additional players. They accept a lower own outcome to impose losses on the designated target. This reveals a willingness to sacrifice personal earnings to rectify perceived inequities or to enforce norms against a specific “offender,” rather than a purely self-interested response to payoff patterns. Overall, this type reacts strongly to accumulated patterns of unfair advantage, forms stable impressions of particular others, and is prepared to use costly, targeted sanctions even when it no longer improves their own material outcome.

text: The player observes sizable payoff advantages accruing to free-riders and later to punished or rewarded players, yet does not alter their own behavior in response. They do not escalate contributions, withdraw cooperation, punish, reward, or re-enter the chat in reaction to others being punished, others spending heavily on punishment, or others receiving rewards. Their response to outcome information is thus behaviorally inert: they maintain the same cooperative and non-intervening pattern regardless of emerging inequalities, sanction patterns, or apparent conflicts among peers. This suggests a stable internal strategy that is relatively insensitive to observed payoff dynamics and to others’ attempts to enforce or undermine norms.

text: The player’s behavior shows that they attend to outcomes and adjust at a coarse level but remain generally passive with respect to others’ fortunes. When free‑riders earn more than cooperative players, they later choose to defect themselves, which is consistent with noticing the payoff advantage of defection or with a simple “if others do well by not contributing, I am willing to do the same” heuristic. However, they do not respond to others’ punishment or reward expenditures by changing how they use the sanction tools: even after being punished for defection and later receiving a reward while defecting, they never reciprocate or imitate sanctioning behavior. They do not visibly react in chat to others’ payoffs, punishment costs, or reward grants, nor do they call out unfairness or praise generosity. Their primary mode of response is through their own contribution choice, not through communication or sanctions. When others incur considerable costs to punish them, they do not escalate defection in protest, nor do they switch into a visibly conciliatory pattern beyond what they already do as a mostly cooperative player. This indicates emotional coolness or at least behavioral restraint in the face of negative treatment. Overall, they are outcome‑aware but not outcome‑expressive. They internalize information about what strategies produce which payoffs and occasionally exploit it, but they do not outwardly signal approval, disapproval, gratitude, or resentment through the available social tools.

text: They observe others’ outcomes, including cases where one peer spends heavily on punishment and another on reward, but they never imitate or respond in kind. text: This player pays attention to who is doing well or poorly and especially to whether those outcomes are tied to cooperation levels. They appear most reactive when non-contributors benefit from the group’s contributions; in those cases, they are more likely to spend on punishment, which reduces those players’ net payoffs. They seem comfortable letting high-contributing, high-earning peers keep their gains, showing no pattern of punishing them despite their superior outcomes. When others already receive extensive punishment from the group, this player sometimes refrains from adding more, which may indicate sensitivity to marginal impact or a preference not to “overkill” targets whose outcomes are already severely reduced (low confidence, as this is not perfectly consistent). They do not visibly escalate punishment after being punished themselves; their behavior toward others’ outcomes seems driven more by observed contribution patterns than by their own prior losses. In sum, they respond to others’ outcomes in a norm-referential way—tolerant of unequal but cooperative success, and hostile mainly to payoff advantages gained through free-riding.

text: The player appears largely indifferent, in behavioral terms, to detailed patterns of others’ earnings and punishment usage. They observe others both inflicting very heavy punishments and receiving severe deductions, but this does not induce them to join punishment cascades or to systematically protect cooperators by sanctioning defectors. Their decisions neither mirror nor clearly counteract others’ punishment behavior. They also do not seem to reward or reciprocate high contributors via their own contribution in any fine-grained way. While they sometimes contribute fully when many others do, that alignment looks more like a response to the overall cooperative climate and expected returns than to specific individuals’ payoffs or suffering. They show no evidence of “sympathy” in the form of reducing free-riding when others are already being heavily punished, nor “schadenfreude” in amplifying existing sanctions. In summary, this player tracks the general level of group cooperation enough to adjust their own contributions but does not visibly react to individual-level outcomes—who is gaining, who is losing, or who is punishing whom—beyond a very coarse, occasional adjustment. Their type is best described as payoff-focused, opportunistically cooperative, and almost entirely non-punitive, with minimal social enforcement or emotionally driven response to others’ fortunes.

text: They attend to others’ outcomes enough to adjust their contributions and occasional punishment, but they do not behave as if they are tightly tracking or emotionally reacting to every payoff or sanction. Despite some players suffering huge negative or near-zero payoffs due to massive punishment, this player does not systematically intervene against the aggressors or protect victims, suggesting limited concern for distributive justice at the individual level. Their choices indicate they focus more on the overall cooperation structure (who is contributing and how many are cooperating) than on the exact payoffs or punishment histories of specific peers. When they are punished, they do not clearly “sulk” by reducing contributions, nor do they ramp up a vendetta through counter-punishment. Instead, they sometimes continue to free-ride after being sanctioned and sometimes cooperate fully, implying that they interpret received punishment as background noise or as only a weak signal. Likewise, when others incur large punishment costs or suffer large deductions, there is no strong behavioral sign that this shifts their strategy—no obvious attempts to reward apparent cooperators through matching contributions, and no clear efforts to rescue severely punished players by changing targets or increasing cooperation solely on their behalf. In summary, this player is socially attentive at the level of group patterns (cooperative fractions, presence of chronic free-riders) but is comparatively insensitive to the fine-grained distribution of individual outcomes. They behave like a pragmatic, group-level conditional cooperator who uses outcome information to manage broad strategy rather than to engage in personalized reciprocity or justice-seeking.

text: The player’s behavior is strikingly insensitive to others’ outcomes. They continue to free‑ride and refrain from punishing, even when they are repeatedly punished. There is no observable reciprocity toward high contributors, no protective reaction toward heavily punished individuals, and no retaliatory reaction toward those who might be punishing them. Their type is best characterized as payoff-focused, non‑responsive to social sanctions, and indifferent or agnostic to the fairness or unfairness of others’ payoffs.

text: This player monitors the broader outcome environment and seems sensitive to aggregate patterns more than to individual identities. They observe a group with frequent extreme punishment and volatile payoffs. Despite seeing some players being driven to very low or even negative payoffs by massive punishment, they themselves keep cooperating and punishing for a substantial portion of the game, which implies a relatively high tolerance for the chaotic sanction environment and a willingness to continue engaging in norm-enforcing behavior even when others behave erratically. They do adjust behavior as the payoff landscape evolves. When others’ outcomes indicate rising instability—large punishment costs, large deductions, and some players ending rounds with very low or negative payoffs—they gradually reduce their own punishment and lean more on protecting their own earnings via increased free-riding and lower sanction spending. Yet they never fully abandon cooperation: they continue to contribute in several later rounds despite ongoing noise. This mix suggests that they interpret others’ extreme punishment not simply as a cue to defect, but as a sign that incentives are fragile and that they should simultaneously preserve their own income and only selectively try to enforce cooperation. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes by adjusting the intensity of their sanctioning and the frequency of their own contributions, balancing a desire for a cooperative norm with self-protection in a volatile environment.

text: This player appears highly attentive to others’ contributions and payoffs, inferred from their repeated willingness to spend on punishment and their alternating contribution strategy. When others’ outcomes reveal substantial earning from low contribution, they react by punishing, which suggests resentment toward free-riders and a concern with relative earnings and fairness. They do not systematically refrain from punishing high earners or cooperators; instead, they seem primarily triggered by the combination of low contribution and decent payoff, which they likely interpret as exploitation of the group. They also keep punishing in periods where many others are already spending large amounts on sanctions, indicating that they are not simply free-riding on others’ enforcement but are willing to join collective punishment efforts. Their own payoff losses from being punished do not visibly stop them from punishing others, implying that they accept mutual punishment as a cost of maintaining or expressing norms. There is no strong evidence that they reward success or kindness, since reward is unavailable, and they do not respond with clear “leniency” when others are heavily sanctioned; instead, they continue to act on their own assessment of who deserves sanctions. Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is that of a norm-focused, inequality-sensitive player who tracks who benefits from cooperation or defection and adjusts punishment and contribution accordingly, rather than someone solely maximizing absolute personal earnings.

text: They show high tolerance for asymmetries in payoffs and punishment outcomes. They are repeatedly punished heavily when they free-ride, yet they do not substantially change their general tendency to free-ride nor do they step into an enforcer role. This suggests they accept others’ willingness to sanction them as a cost of their own strategy, rather than interpreting it as a cue to adopt group norms. Their rare use of punishment does not track clearly with others’ payoff levels or punishment histories, so there is little evidence that they aim to correct perceived unfair payoff distributions. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes mainly by modest, situational adjustments in their own contribution level, not by engaging in payoff- or justice-driven punishment. Their learning is limited: they register that cooperation brings higher redistributed payoffs but remain comfortable living with being sanctioned as long as their broader free-riding strategy remains viable.

text: The player appears to attend closely to others’ outcomes and sanction activity. They observe who ends up with high or low payoffs and who is repeatedly deducted, and they continue to engage in punishment even when certain players are already being heavily sanctioned by others. This implies that seeing others punished does not fully satisfy their enforcement impulse; they still invest to layer additional deductions, signaling an intense response to perceived unfairness or disloyalty to the public good. Their own vulnerability to punishment does not cause them to disengage from sanctioning. Even when they themselves are deducted and end up with very low or negative payoffs, they continue to spend heavily on punishment in subsequent rounds. This suggests they do not primarily interpret being punished as a cue to change their enforcement behavior, but rather persist with their own norm system. They do not visibly switch to a purely self‑protective or payoff‑maximizing strategy in response to losing money; instead, they treat the punishment stage as an arena to assert their standards regardless of personal cost. At the same time, they do not fully abandon cooperation after being heavily punished or after observing chaotic sanction patterns in the group. They repeatedly return to full contributions, which indicates they do not become a stable antisocial type. Their behavior is better described as a “moralistic but volatile cooperator”: they care about the public good and about punishing perceived offenders, tolerate substantial payoff variation as a consequence, and do not easily update away from this orientation even when their own outcomes suffer. Their reactions to others’ outcomes thus combine strong sensitivity to fairness and discipline with limited adjustment toward payoff‑maximizing pragmatism.

text: The player repeatedly observes that many others contribute and often earn high payoffs, and that heavy punishers sometimes severely reduce their own payoffs. Despite this, the player does not imitate cooperative contributors or extreme punishers. They continue to free ride in the presence of persistent cooperation by others, implying that seeing others do well from contributing does not induce them to adopt similar behavior; they either discount the causal link between contribution and group payoff, or they value immediate private gain and risk avoidance more than long-run cooperative returns. When they themselves are hit with substantial punishment, they typically do not escalate punishment expenditures in following rounds, suggesting that they do not automatically retaliate when harmed at the group level, or at least not on a matching scale. Their occasional punitive actions do not clearly track simple patterns like “hit whoever earned the most” or “hit whoever contributed the least,” so their mapping from others’ outcomes to punishment is opaque and likely low-confidence to infer. Overall, their learning from outcomes appears limited: repeated evidence that non-cooperators are punished and that contributors can maintain good payoffs does not lead to a consistent shift in contribution strategy, and repeated evidence that extreme punishment is very costly does not lead them to mimic that behavior either. They behave as if they primarily monitor that their own payoff remains acceptably high, and as long as it does, they do not substantially revise their approach, regardless of what happens to others.

text: The player is repeatedly singled out by others for heavy deductions, often far beyond what anyone else receives in that round. They continue to defect despite this, indicating that social disapproval and financial loss do not effectively shift their core strategy. Their cooperation does not improve after being punished, and they even revert to taking everything after occasions when they briefly contribute fully. This implies either that they interpret punishment as an unavoidable cost of free‑riding, or that they reject the group’s cooperative norm and choose not to conform. They show little evidence of conditional “positive” responsiveness to others’ outcomes: they do not seem to increase contributions when others earn less or when punishment against defectors is intense, and they do not appear to align with any coalition that might be cooperating. Instead, their main visible adjustment in response to the social environment is a late‑game surge in punitive spending after a long history of being targeted, consistent with accumulated frustration. They accept repeated low or even negative payoffs without abandoning free‑riding, which suggests a personality profile that is stubborn, thick‑skinned to sanctions, and willing to sacrifice efficiency and earnings in order to maintain their preferred, non‑cooperative stance and occasionally retaliate when an opportunity to punish presents itself.

text: They observe an environment with frequent, often extreme punishment, including players driving others’ payoffs deeply negative, and yet they do not visibly re-optimize against this backdrop. This suggests that they either hold a strong internal norm for cooperation that dominates outcome-based learning, or they down-weight short-run payoff comparisons when updating their strategy. They do not condition on others’ individual trajectories in a sophisticated way; their behavior looks largely rule-based rather than finely responsive to who is gaining or losing from punishment. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes with steadfast consistency rather than adaptive payoff-sensitive shifts.

text: The player’s behavior shows a high tolerance for inequality and for others’ aggressive punishment choices. Being targeted by punishment themselves does not lead them to systematically reduce their contributions or to become a heavy punisher. Instead, their primary adjustment lever is almost absent: they persist in cooperative play with minimal reaction to observed outcome distributions. They also do not appear eager to “teach lessons” to players who blow up their own payoffs with excessive punishment; they allow those dynamics to play out without joining in. Overall, they read others’ outcomes enough to know that the environment includes antisocial and hyper-punishing players, but they largely decouple their own decisions from these outcome patterns and stay anchored in a stable, cooperative, low-punishment persona.

text: They clearly monitor others’ contributions and resulting payoffs and use that information to comment on behavior and incentives. Early on, they respond to the fact that some players earn higher payoffs by contributing nothing, and they reframe this in terms of how those players could earn even more by cooperating, indicating a concern with both efficiency and persuading potential free-riders rather than punishing or ostracizing them. When a highly vocal “cooperator” actually contributes zero, they highlight this mismatch between stated intentions and realized contributions, suggesting they track alignment between talk and outcomes and care about honesty or consistency. However, this recognition of exploitation and hypocrisy does not lead to any reduction in their own cooperation, nor to hostile reactions; they continue to fully contribute and do not engage in moralistic attacks. There is no evidence of spite, vengeance, or desire to “teach a lesson” through reducing contributions, even when others propose such strategies in chat. Instead, they appear to treat others’ deviations and higher payoffs as problems to be addressed via explanation and information, not via withdrawal of cooperation. Their type is thus a robust, efficiency-oriented cooperator who stays cooperative even in the presence of free-riding and perceived unfairness, and who responds to outcome disparities primarily through calm, reasoned commentary rather than behavioral retaliation.

text: They pay close attention to others’ contributions and resultant payoffs and react strongly to perceived unfairness. When some players free‑ride and therefore earn higher payoffs, they explicitly note who contributed nothing and frame this as greedy, selfish, or harmful to everyone. They challenge claims of cooperation by others when the contribution data show otherwise, directly labeling inconsistencies as lies. This indicates a strong concern for honesty and transparency around contributions and outcomes. Despite this, they do not retaliate materially. They do not reduce their own contributions in response to others’ higher payoffs from free‑riding. Instead, they respond with social sanctions: criticism, shaming, and moral appeals. When cooperation improves, they quickly pivot from frustration to enthusiastic praise, celebrating the group’s joint gains and reinforcing the idea that everyone is doing “awesome” and that they are proud of the group. As earnings accumulate, they highlight success and encourage continued adherence to the cooperative pattern. They also show some cognitive engagement with others’ incentives, questioning why someone who understands the mechanism would still defect and expressing confusion that they do not act in their own long‑term monetary interest. They are willing, briefly, to consider using collective defection as a social signal to “teach them a lesson,” but they abandon this at the behavioral level and revert to full cooperation. In summary, they are highly outcome‑sensitive in perception and emotion but behaviorally steadfastly cooperative. Their primary tools for responding to others’ payoffs and choices are verbal norm enforcement and social approval or disapproval, not strategic adjustment of contributions.

text: They closely monitor others’ contributions and implied payoffs and react to deviations from the cooperative norm. When some players contribute nothing or little and thereby earn higher payoffs, they highlight that this behavior is unfair and socially harmful, and they complain that such greed actually reduces those players’ potential earnings given the multiplier. They sometimes reframe the situation as “their greed is costing them money,” suggesting a belief that low contributors are also making a mistake, not just exploiting others. They become indignant when someone falsely claims to have contributed more than reported, siding with the public feedback and calling out inconsistency. They support and praise high contributions indirectly by celebrating the group’s growing total earnings and announcing milestones in dollars, reinforcing cooperative behavior without any explicit reward mechanism. When talk of “teaching a lesson” by jointly contributing zero arises, they oppose it, prioritizing sustained high group payoffs over retaliatory strategies. In summary, they interpret others’ outcomes through a moralized efficiency lens: high contributors are teammates to coordinate with; low contributors are either confused, selfish, or non‑human, and the correct response is to keep pushing for full cooperation rather than to reciprocate defection.

text: They clearly attend to others’ payoffs and the structure of who benefits from defection versus cooperation. They do not openly criticize specific low contributors, nor do they join in the more moralizing or shaming responses from others; instead, they remain relatively detached in chat from the blame directed at repeat defectors. At the same time, their own later behavior mirrors the initial observation: once they see that a stable cooperative regime exists and that enforcement is limited to social talk, they selectively under-contribute while benefiting from others’ high contributions, suggesting that they internalize the incentive pattern and act on it. They do not express sympathy for exploited cooperators, nor anger at free-riders; their reactions are more observational than emotional. When the group returns to full cooperation after partial breakdowns, they also return to full contributions without comment, indicating pragmatism rather than ideological commitment. Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is cognitively engaged—tracking who gains from what—but socially muted, with a focus on quietly adapting their own strategy rather than trying to reshape others’ behavior.

text: The player clearly monitors others’ contributions and the resulting payoffs, referencing who is contributing, whether contributions are recorded correctly, and how much “cash” the group is accumulating. They accept the public outcome information as accurate and use it to interpret others’ behavior (for example, noting when another player’s claim to have contributed conflicts with the recorded zero). They celebrate high collective payoffs and express excitement about accumulating money, treating cooperative success as both financially and emotionally rewarding. When others free‑ride or contribute less, they do not press for sanctions (none are available) and do not attempt social punishment beyond mild skepticism or labeling nonresponsive players as likely fake. Their main adjustment to persistent low contributors is psychological and strategic rather than behavioral: they mentally separate “us” (the chat‑active cooperators) from “them” (silent or bot‑like low contributors) and advocate ignoring the latter while maintaining cooperation within the engaged group. Their own contribution pattern does not systematically track others’ deviations; they continue to contribute maximally even in the presence of visible free‑riding. The isolated instances of zero input are not explicitly tied to anger at others’ outcomes or to teaching a lesson, and no such motive is verbally stated, so the connection is unknown. Overall, they respond to outcome information by reinforcing cooperative norms, celebrating gains, and adjusting beliefs about who is “really” in the game, rather than by reducing their contributions in response to exploitation.

text: They do not comment explicitly on punishment or reward flows, either those they receive or those others receive. Being punished once after defecting does not lead them to complain, retaliate, or alter their cooperative stance; instead, they resume maximum contribution, which suggests they accept the social feedback or, at minimum, are not destabilized by it. They do not join discussions about who deserves deductions or who is sabotaging, even when others label specific players as bots or moles and pour large amounts into sanctions. Their behavior implies a degree of independence from the punishment/reward meta‑game that others are playing: they keep contributing and, in earlier rounds, keep rewarding, without visibly conditioning on how much others are spending on sanctions or how much they themselves are being targeted. They do, however, read and relay task instructions about timing and acceptance of submissions, suggesting attention to structural conditions of payoffs, but there is no sign they adjust contributions or sanctions based on observed individual payoff trajectories. Overall, they appear resilient to noise and sanction volatility, focusing on cooperation and occasional positive reinforcement rather than reacting to others’ specific outcomes.

text: They pay close attention to outcome information and use it to interpret others’ behavior and the experiment’s design. When someone free-rides early or contributes zero by “mistake,” they react with humor and mild teasing, but they do not escalate or withdraw cooperation. When they and another full cooperator are heavily punished, they question the source but are quick to accept alternative explanations (e.g., “the computer” or experimental manipulation) rather than turning on each other. They notice patterns of extreme punishment and reward by certain players and integrate this into their mental model, labeling one as likely non-human or a plant. This attribution then informs their later support for maintaining cooperation regardless of such anomalies and eventually their late punishment of that actor. They also notice when everyone contributes fully and celebrate high joint payoffs, using phrases of collective pride (“squad,” “full house”) as positive reinforcement. They show concern for fairness and earnings over the longer run. They track their own cumulative coins and relate them to expected monetary bonus, mention unexpected bonuses in other studies, and worry about whether the promised conversion will be honored. They also worry about timing out and losing payment, urging quick responses to maximize both rounds played and security of submission. When others express confusion or frustration about being deducted despite cooperating, they validate those feelings, share that they too were deducted, and align with the interpretation that the environment or non-cooperative agents—not the cooperating chat group—are responsible. This helps preserve trust within the communicative subgroup and sustain full contributions. Overall, their responses to outcomes are interpretive and community-protective: they look for explanations that keep the group united and cooperative rather than fragmented or vindictive.

text: They show little explicit emotional or strategic reaction to the punishment/reward distributions visible each round. They occasionally acknowledge system-level or procedural events (such as getting an idle warning or noting that someone likely left due to inactivity), but they do not comment on who is being punished or rewarded, nor on fairness of specific payoffs. Even when their own payoff is reduced by deductions, they neither protest nor demand explanations in chat. They also do not adjust contributions or start punishing in response, suggesting that being targeted does not shift their cooperative baseline. When other players puzzle over deductions, they let others (who attribute some effects to “the computer”) handle the narrative; they do not contradict or endorse those interpretations. Their primary behavioral response to others’ actions is to maintain stable cooperation and friendly conversation, effectively decoupling their first-order contribution strategy from the noise and volatility of second-order outcomes. Overall they interpret the game as something where the best response is “keep maxing and stay chill,” regardless of the detailed pattern of punishments and rewards around them.

text: The player observes that others routinely contribute much more while earning lower payoffs than they do, yet they do not adjust toward greater generosity. This stable gap between their own payoff and that of most others indicates that they are comfortable benefiting disproportionately from the group’s effort. There is no visible attempt to sacrifice some of this advantage to move closer to an equitable outcome. When others maintain or even increase high contribution levels, the player does not reward this implicitly by raising their own contribution. Their responses to strong cooperation are to stay low or revert to zero, rather than to reciprocate. This suggests they interpret others’ cooperative behavior as an opportunity to continue free-riding rather than as a signal to join a cooperative norm. They also do not appear to be deterred by the fact that some low contributors in the group obtain similar or higher payoffs. The presence of other low contributors does not push them toward “fair sharing” within the subgroup; they simply maintain their own advantage as long as the environment permits it. Because punishment and reward mechanisms are disabled, there is no direct evidence of how they would spend resources to sanction or reward others, and their willingness to incur personal cost for norm enforcement is unknown. However, given their consistent payoff-maximizing choices and indifference to being better off than others, it is low-confidence but plausible that they would be reluctant to expend resources to punish free-riders or reward cooperators in similar settings. In summary, when faced with others’ outcomes—both high cooperative effort and lower relative payoffs for those others—the player responds by maintaining a self-benefiting, low-contribution strategy rather than adjusting in the direction of reciprocity, fairness, or group welfare.

text: This player responds to others’ outcomes by adjusting contributions rather than by any form of direct sanction or reward (none is available). Their main reaction mechanism is to modulate how much they give based on whether the group environment looks “cooperative enough” to sustain high returns without their full support. When others generate strong total contributions and thus high payoffs, the player often responds in subsequent decisions by lowering their own contribution while staying within the broad cooperative range. This suggests that seeing others earn well from the public good does not discourage them from the institution itself; instead, it encourages selective free-riding, as they infer that group cooperation is robust enough to withstand their partial withdrawal. Conversely, when overall outcomes worsen and group payoffs fall, they show willingness to return to high contributions, indicating a concern with avoiding a collapse of the public good. They appear to interpret poor outcomes as a signal that cooperation needs to be shored up rather than as a reason to defect further. This reflects a belief that their own contribution can meaningfully influence group efficiency, even in a large group. They do not display clear evidence of being payoff-envious or strongly payoff-equalizing. They sometimes accept being behind better-earning free-riders and sometimes position themselves as one of the higher earners by under-contributing relative to generous peers. There is no consistent movement to match the best earners or to avoid being at the top; they primarily track whether the outcome level for the group is satisfactory. Their learning is incremental: they do not lock into a single pattern but experiment across a range of contributions while monitoring overall efficiency. Over time, this yields a persona that is responsive to the general success or failure of the public good, but not specifically reactive to individual others’ gains or losses. Their “sanction” for others’ behavior is implicit and collective: they withhold or restore contributions based on the perceived stability of group cooperation, aiming to keep payoffs high while improving their own relative position when opportunities arise.

text: The player monitors others’ outcomes indirectly through how they adjust their own contributions in response to the pattern of others’ contributions and the resulting payoffs. They appear attentive to whether generous contributors are being “rewarded” in the sense of achieving acceptable payoffs relative to more selfish players. When they see that low contributors can still secure high or even superior payoffs in a cooperative group, they are willing to shift toward more self-serving behavior, indicating sensitivity to payoff differentials rather than to fairness or group welfare alone. At the same time, they do not consistently target the maximum possible exploitation when free riding is profitable; they intermittently return to higher contributions even when the environment remains generally cooperative. This suggests that they value sustaining a cooperative climate to some degree, likely believing that persistent, extreme free riding could undermine the group returns that currently benefit them. Since explicit punishment and reward are absent, their only “response” channel is to adjust their own contributions. They use this channel both to test what others will tolerate and to track whether cooperative norms remain intact. They do not appear driven by punitive motives or moral outrage; instead, their adjustments align with a learning process focused on maintaining good personal payoffs in a mostly cooperative but imperfectly enforced environment. In summary, the player responds to others’ outcomes in a calculated, self-interested, yet not purely exploitative manner: they adapt their contributions based on observed group cooperativeness and relative payoff opportunities, with low evidence of strong fairness-enforcing or altruistic motives.

text: This player sees that low contributors and free riders often earn equal or even higher payoffs than they do, yet they do not adjust their own behavior in response. There is no evidence of retaliatory withdrawal of cooperation when others profit from contributing far less, nor of competitive escalation when others’ payoffs surpass theirs. Given that no punishment or reward actions are available, their only possible response channel is adjusting their own contributions. Their refusal to substantially lower their contributions after witnessing repeated payoff advantages for low contributors indicates low concern with relative standing and limited sensitivity to outcome-based resentment. They accept that others may do better while contributing less and continue to follow their own internal rule. Their responses imply that they do not strongly believe that their behavior will discipline or guide others’ contributions via payoff feedback; they continue to cooperate at roughly the same level whether the group outcome is relatively good or relatively poor. Any learning from outcomes is minimal: even sizeable shifts in the group’s total contributions and corresponding changes in payoffs do not lead to a systematic change in their pattern. This outcome-insensitivity suggests a personality profile that is principled or habit-driven rather than opportunistic: they act in line with a stable contribution norm they set for themselves, and observed inequalities in payoffs—both in their favor and against them—have little observable impact on their decisions. Their main “response” to others’ outcomes is, effectively, to stay the course.

text: The player observes others’ outcomes but does not change behavior in response, indicating either low responsiveness or a deliberate choice to ignore payoff comparisons. Despite visible evidence that some low contributors systematically earn more than them in several periods, they do not reduce their contribution, retaliate, or attempt to protect themselves from free-riding. This implies low concern for relative payoff standing and no behavioral expression of envy or competitive motives. With no punishment or reward mechanisms available, their only way to “respond” to others’ outcomes is via their own future contributions. Their unwavering full contribution in the face of varied peer payoffs suggests they neither use others’ high payoffs as encouragement to free-ride, nor use others’ free-riding payoffs as a trigger for withdrawal. They effectively decouple their decision from others’ immediate gains or losses. They likely interpret others’ outcomes through a lens that accepts some exploitation as an acceptable cost of upholding cooperation, or they treat returns as secondary to the act of contributing. There is no sign of adaptive learning from payoff patterns; instead, their response to changing group-level earnings remains constant. Because they continue fully contributing even when many low contributors are clearly better off in that period, they appear tolerant of advantageous inequality for others at their own expense, as long as the public good is maintained at a high level. Their behavioral response to others’ outcomes is thus characterized by persistence in cooperation, indifference to payoff asymmetries, and the absence of retaliatory or strategic adjustments.

text: This player shows *non-responsive altruism* toward others’ outcomes. They accept repeatedly ending up with lower payoffs than many peers when others free-ride more, yet they never reduce their own cooperation. Their contribution rule remains unchanged across situations where others’ payoffs are higher thanks to low contributions, and across situations where the group becomes more cooperative and payoffs rise. There is no evidence they attempt to discipline free-riders, reward cooperators, or align with the emerging contribution norm; they neither converge toward the group average nor distance themselves from it when it is low. Because formal punishment and reward options are unavailable, their only lever is contribution choice, and they do not use it strategically in response to others’ earnings or behaviors. The most consistent interpretation is that they prioritize contributing to the group over managing payoff inequalities, and do not treat others’ advantageous outcomes (relative to their own) as something to correct. Their emotional or evaluative stance toward others’ outcomes is unknown, but their behavioral response is strictly invariant.

text: This player pays attention to others’ outcomes and appears to use them as information about how cooperative, stable, and profitable the group environment is. When others obtain strong payoffs that are consistent with high contributions and a well‑functioning public good, they respond by either maintaining or increasing their own contributions, effectively supporting and reinforcing that cooperative state despite personal cost. This suggests they interpret high group payoffs as a signal that cooperation is collectively worthwhile and, to some extent, trustworthy. When others’ contributions and associated payoffs become more uneven, with evidence of some players contributing very little while still benefiting, the player adjusts by pulling their own contribution down. They do not retaliate directly (no punishment is available), but they implicitly “punish” freeriding by lowering their own investment in the public good, thereby reducing the benefit free‑riders can extract. Nonetheless, they do not mirror the lowest contributors, indicating that they are not purely payoff‑maximizing in a narrow, self‑interested sense; they retain a cooperative baseline even when others’ outcomes would justify stronger self‑protection. The player appears more responsive to the overall pattern of group outcomes than to any specific individual’s payoff. Their adjustments are smooth and group‑level rather than targeted or sharply reactive, which suggests a more analytical and aggregate‑focused reasoning style: they update their strategy based on the perceived health of the group outcome rather than on moralistic resentment toward particular high earners or low contributors. They tolerate some inequality in outcomes as long as the general level of payoffs for the group remains high; once group efficiency declines, they prioritize securing acceptable personal returns over sustaining maximal public benefits. In sum, the player is a conditional cooperator with a moderate tolerance for being slightly exploited, who uses others’ payoff patterns as a guide to how much trust and generosity is justified. They adapt gradually, nudging their behavior in response to shifts in group outcomes rather than making drastic moves, which reflects both a learning orientation and a preference for maintaining some level of cooperation even in imperfect environments.

text: The player appears attentive to the relationship between others’ contributions and payoffs, and adapts in a way consistent with payoff-based learning. When cooperative behavior appears rewarded with higher group returns, they become more willing to contribute generously. When they observe that low contributors can still earn high or even higher payoffs than cooperators, they are willing to copy that pattern and free-ride themselves. They tolerate some payoff inequality but respond when inequality looks persistent or norm-violating (for example, when very low contributors repeatedly earn strong payoffs). In those cases, they reduce their own contribution substantially, indicating sensitivity to being exploited and a desire not to be the “sucker.” There is no direct punishment or reward mechanism available in this game, so all such responses occur purely through adjusting their own contribution levels. Because individual punishing and rewarding are impossible here, their only lever is their own contribution. They use that lever in a targeted way—sometimes moving toward the cooperative majority, sometimes toward low contributors—depending on how the payoff structure seems to treat different behaviors. This implies they believe others are also responsive to incentives and that changing their own behavior might shift norms or at least protect their own earnings. In sum, they: - Track how cooperative and uncooperative players fare in terms of payoffs. - Adjust their contributions to avoid being consistently outperformed by free-riders. - Still show willingness to re-engage in cooperation after a free-riding episode if the group’s contributions and resulting payoffs support that choice. Any deeper beliefs about motives (e.g., trust, altruism, spite) are unknown beyond these payoff-contingent adjustments.

text: This player pays more attention to how others’ behavior affects their own payoff than to others’ payoffs in themselves. They show no behavioral sign of being upset by unfair distributions or of trying to correct inequality when they personally gain from it. They continue to benefit from situations in which high contributors earn less than low contributors, without adjusting upward to make the outcome more balanced. When they see that free-riders or low contributors obtain especially high payoffs, they do not move to punish this pattern (no punishment is available) but do sometimes imitate the payoff-maximizing stance by keeping their own contributions low. This suggests payoff-based learning rather than norm-enforcing preferences. Conversely, when widespread cooperation leads to high overall redistribution, they are willing at times to join or approximate the cooperative pattern, but they do not “reward” cooperative others with sustained high contributions. Their cooperation is short-lived or partial and seems more like exploiting a temporarily favorable environment than building a long-term reciprocal relationship. Because there is no punishment or reward mechanism, their only observable response channel is adjusting their own contribution. They use this channel mainly to track what appears most profitable for themselves, not to signal approval or disapproval of others’ choices. They do not stabilize at a level that would suggest they internalize a norm like “everyone should give a lot” or “match what others do.” Overall, the player responds to others’ outcomes instrumentally: they infer what strategies yield good personal returns and adjust their own giving accordingly. Their social preference profile appears weak or secondary; efficiency and fairness concerns are present only insofar as they coincide with high personal earnings. Their type in new PGG environments is best described as an adaptable, payoff-oriented participant who watches the pattern of group outcomes and then oscillates between cooperation and free-riding to stay on the personally advantageous side of emerging norms.

text: Because punishment and reward are unavailable and chat is disabled, this player has no direct action channel to respond to others’ payoffs except through their own contribution choices. Their main “response” to the outcome pattern—where some low contributors regularly achieve high or even higher payoffs than themselves—is to largely maintain or return to full cooperation rather than to disengage. They repeatedly observe situations where players who contribute little earn substantial payoffs via the public pool. Despite this, they do not generalize into sustained self‑protective under‑contribution. Their single notable deviation toward a lower contribution is brief and not repeated after subsequent payoff experiences that again reveal free‑riding. This indicates that others’ advantageous outcomes, even when arguably unfair from a contribution standpoint, do not strongly shift their cooperative stance. There is no observable evidence that this player conditions their behavior on specific individuals’ payoffs or on the distribution of earnings across the group. They do not track or mirror particular peers; instead, their reaction to earnings patterns is coarse: occasional testing of a smaller contribution followed by reversion to a high‑contribution baseline. This suggests low sensitivity to inequity in outcomes and low inclination toward payoff‑based retaliation. Beliefs about others’ motives and future behavior are not directly observable, but their persistence in high contributions despite mixed group cooperation suggests that they either expect cooperation to remain worthwhile overall or accept that some exploitation is inevitable yet tolerable. Unknown whether they feel frustration or resignation internally; behaviorally, they remain cooperative and do not escalate any form of response. Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is characterized by resilience and a strong bias toward continuing to support the public good, with only minimal and transient behavioral adjustment in reaction to unfair payoff structures.

text: This player is highly sensitive to the payoff structure and others’ outcomes, and uses that information to fine-tune their free-riding rather than to reciprocate. When others earn good payoffs while contributing much more, the player does not appear bothered by the inequity in their own favor; they continue or intensify low contributions that preserve their advantage. They observe that large contributions by others generate high payoffs for everyone, including themselves, even when they contribute very little. Instead of reciprocating this positive externality, they respond by cutting their own contributions further, suggesting that they interpret others’ cooperative behavior as an opportunity to extract more surplus rather than as a norm to uphold. There is no evidence of concern for punishing or rewarding others in response to their outcomes, both because such mechanisms are absent and because the player’s own behavior never moves in a direction that would “discipline” low contributors or “support” high contributors through imitation. They do not seem to adjust upward when others’ payoffs fall, nor downward out of resentment when others outperform them; rather, their main adjustment axis is: “If others are still contributing a lot and overall payoffs remain high, I can safely contribute even less.” In personality terms (inferred with low to moderate confidence), this suggests: - A pragmatic, outcome-focused orientation rather than norm-driven fairness. - Low guilt or discomfort about benefiting disproportionately from others’ efforts. - A learning style that relies on observing group-level payoffs and then optimizing personal gain within that environment, even if it erodes the collective contribution level.

text: The player clearly observes others’ payoffs and adjusts behavior, but their adjustments are restrained rather than punitive or reactive. In the absence of direct punishment or reward mechanisms, their only way to respond to observed outcomes is via their own future contribution levels. They uses this channel, but not aggressively: when others with very low contributions receive high payoffs, they does not retaliate by slashing their own contributions to similarly low levels. Instead, they slightly trim or modulate their contributions while staying cooperative. They does not display a strong drive to equalize payoffs. Even when clear free-riders obtain higher payoffs than they do, there is no evidence of a dramatic behavioral shift aimed at protecting their own payoff at the expense of the group. This suggests that envy or inequity aversion is present, if at all, only in a mild form. Group efficiency and maintaining a high public good seem to matter more to them than eliminating payoff disparities. Their responses to others’ outcomes suggest a learning process oriented toward maintaining cooperation while testing the group’s reliability. When group outcomes are collectively strong and many peers contribute generously, they tends to sustain or increase their own contributions, reinforcing cooperation despite some free-riding. When the public good shrinks and more peers reduce contributions, they cautiously lower their own contribution, indicating a belief that cooperation is fragile and dependent on mutual support. Because no explicit punishment or reward is available, the absence of harsh downward shifts is informative: this player does not use the only indirect “sanction” at their disposal (withdrawing cooperation) in a drastic way. Instead, they practices “soft conditionality”: gently adjusting in response to others’ gains but not abandoning the cooperative norm. This points to a personality that is tolerant of some unfairness, more focused on sustaining beneficial group outcomes than on disciplining defectors, and willing to bear some personal cost to keep the public good at a comparatively high level.

text: The player is observant of payoff patterns and responsive to the overall outcome environment rather than to individual counterparts. As they see that some participants reliably obtain higher payoffs by contributing less, they do not retaliate (no punishment is possible) and do not mirror those low-contribution strategies. Instead, they make a moderate downward adjustment while remaining on the cooperative side. The absence of any sharp drop to very low contributions, even after observing repeated payoff advantages for low contributors, suggests limited spite and low payoff-based envy. They accept that others sometimes earn more despite contributing less and do not strongly adjust behavior to erase this disadvantage. Their concern appears to be more about not being excessively exploited than about eliminating all unfairness. They seem to update their expectations about group behavior over time: as it becomes evident that the group will not consistently maintain very high contribution levels, they stop sustaining full contributions. This shows learning from aggregate outcomes: they treat repeated weaker public-good outcomes as information that justifies dialing back, but not abandoning, cooperation. With no reward or punishment tools available, their “response” to others’ outcomes is encoded solely through their own contribution choices. These choices indicate a preference for maintaining a cooperative equilibrium as long as it is plausible, and then settling into a tempered-cooperation stance when the realized payoffs reveal persistent free-riding and variability. They do not experiment widely or oscillate dramatically, implying a relatively calm, measured style rather than reactive or volatile behavior.
