text: The player observes substantial variation in others’ contributions and payoffs, including repeated instances where low contributors realize higher earnings than them, yet they never alter their own cooperative stance. They do not reduce contributions in response to others’ high payoffs from apparent free-riding, nor do they increase beyond their already maximal contribution when cooperators do well. In the absence of formal punishment or reward tools, their only available “response” is to change their own contribution; their refusal to do so indicates that they do not use payoff comparisons as a trigger for negative reciprocity or retaliation. They appear relatively insensitive to disadvantageous inequality: others sometimes earn more while contributing less, but this does not provoke any observable backlash. This supports the view that they prioritize the existence and size of the public good itself over matching or beating others’ earnings. Their pattern is consistent with someone who either does not heavily weight relative payoff concerns or deliberately brackets them in favor of a cooperative norm. Because they never test defection even when others’ outcomes signal that defection can be profitable, their learning from others’ outcomes is limited or deliberately overridden. They may notice that lower contributors can do better, but they do not translate this into strategic adaptation. Instead, their response to others’ outcomes is to continue implementing a stable, high-cooperation policy. Overall, their type is best described as a steadfast, efficiency-oriented cooperator who tolerates free-riding and inequality without using available behavioral adjustments as informal “punishment.”

text: This player tracks and responds to others’ outcomes in a primarily self-interested, payoff-focused way rather than a norm-enforcing way. They observe that many others often contribute more than they do, while those others sometimes earn lower payoffs. Their subsequent reductions in contribution, especially moves toward free-riding, align with exploiting this pattern rather than correcting it. This implies that seeing high contributors earn less does not trigger compensating generosity or an attempt to restore fairness; instead, it encourages them to capitalize on the situation. There is no punishment or reward mechanism available, so their “responses” to others’ outcomes are expressed only via their own contribution choices. Given that, they do not appear to sacrifice income in order to stabilize cooperation or protect high contributors. When they notice that personal payoffs remain strong despite contributing much less than most peers, they continue or deepen this strategy, indicating low concern for others’ relative earnings and low aversion to payoff inequality that favors them. When group contributions are high and collective payoffs are strong, they occasionally increase their own contributions, but the pattern suggests this is more about exploring whether sharing in the cost still keeps them well-positioned rather than about reciprocating out of obligation or gratitude. They do not show a consistent tendency to “reward” cooperative groups with sustained high giving. Their learning across rounds shows that others’ outcomes mainly serve as information about the environment’s exploitable structure: who is likely to keep contributing, how much they can earn by free-riding, and how safe it is to lower their own contributions without collapsing the group return. Emotional or moral reactions (such as guilt, resentment, or desire to equalize payoffs) are not evident from the pattern; if they exist, they are too weak to override their drive for individually favorable outcomes. In summary, the player uses others’ payoffs as strategic input, not as a fairness reference point.

text: This player observes that free-riders and low contributors can earn higher payoffs than strong contributors, yet they continue to invest heavily after seeing such patterns. They do not have access to direct punishment or reward, and there is no evidence of indirect retaliation through sharply reduced contributions after observing others’ outcomes; their one full-defection instance does not lead into a sustained low-contribution regime. Thus, they do not systematically use their own giving level as a form of “soft punishment” or exit in response to others’ higher earnings. They appear relatively tolerant of payoff inequality: they accept that some players who contribute very little can earn more, and they do not lock into a defensive, low-contribution strategy afterward. This suggests that they prioritize the potential for high collective gains and future cooperation over strictly equalizing payoffs in the short term. At the same time, their single free-riding choice following exposure to mixed cooperation indicates they are not entirely indifferent to the profitability of self-interested strategies; they briefly verify that defecting can be privately lucrative before reverting to generous giving. Because they cannot identify specific punishers or rewarders and there is no peer-targeted mechanism, their main “response channel” is their own contribution path. They use this primarily to maintain or restore cooperation rather than to discipline others. Their reaction to the observed outcome environment is thus best characterized as **resilient and efficiency-focused, with mild opportunistic probing but no sustained retaliatory pattern**.

text: This player pays close implicit attention to others’ outcomes, as inferred from how their own contributions adjust in ways that track which strategies are most lucrative. When they see that some low-contributing players achieve higher or comparable payoffs to more cooperative players, they shift toward that lower-contribution region, indicating that they benchmark themselves against observed earnings rather than against a strict fairness ideal. They do not respond in an all-or-nothing retaliatory way to low contributors. Instead of matching the lowest contributors, they settle on intermediate levels: they tolerate the presence of clear free-riders while still contributing something themselves. This suggests limited spite or moralistic punishment motivation in the absence of formal punishment tools, and a willingness to coexist with inequality if their own payoff remains high. Their response to cooperative peers is also mixed: they do not escalate to the most cooperative levels simply to align with high contributors, even when such alignment could sustain very high group payoffs. Instead, they appear content to let others “lead” in cooperation while they position themselves somewhat below that level. This indicates that they value relative advantage and personal gain more than strict equality of outcomes. Since no explicit punishment or reward mechanisms are available, there is no direct evidence of willingness to bear costs to change others’ outcomes; their only lever is their own contribution. They use this lever primarily to optimize their own payoff rather than to enforce norms. Norm-enforcement motivation is therefore unknown but likely low, given that their behavioral adjustments consistently favor profit even when this increases payoff disparities. In summary, they are observant and responsive to the payoff structure and others’ behavior, but they channel this responsiveness into self-interested conditional cooperation rather than overt attempts to discipline or reward others through their own choices.

text: They observe that free-riders and low contributors sometimes earn substantially higher payoffs than they do, yet they largely keep their own contribution rule intact. When specific players swing between high and very low contributions, the focal player does not clearly retaliate or mirror these changes over time. Their one brief lower contribution happens when several others reduce sharply, which increases their own payoff, but they do not lock into this more selfish level afterward. Likewise, when others around them contribute less and gain more in a later episode, they respond once with a costly over-contribution, then revert. Overall, they accept that others’ outcomes may at times be more favorable, even when those others contribute less. They do not escalate defection in response, nor do they implement tit-for-tat adjustments in any sustained way. This suggests relatively low spite and low vengefulness, combined with a preference for maintaining their own internally consistent standard over closely policing fairness. Whether they feel resentment is unknown; behaviorally, they remain steady, mildly prosocial, and non-retaliatory.

text: This player observes that low contributors can achieve very high round payoffs, and that high contributors sometimes end up with comparatively lower payoffs. Despite this, they rarely imitate the most selfish patterns. Their contribution rule remains largely stable across episodes where others free-ride and earn more, indicating relatively low envy or, at least, limited behavioral response to payoff inequality. Occasional downward shifts in their contribution suggest some sensitivity to perceived exploitation or group under-provision, but these shifts are brief and not tightly synchronized with any single peer’s payoff spike. They do not clearly reward high contributors with matching generosity, nor systematically punish low contributors by drastically reducing their own contributions; instead, they drift slightly down or up around their baseline, as if acknowledging the incentives but preferring not to engage in strong reactive strategies. Without punishment or reward tools, their only lever is their own contribution. They appear reluctant to weaponize it to “discipline” others. This aligns with a personality profile of a pragmatic, moderately cooperative player who tolerates some level of free-riding, accepts unequal outcomes as part of the game, and relies primarily on an internal contribution norm rather than on dynamic, payoff-contingent responses.

text: The player is sensitive to others’ behavior but not highly punitive or retaliatory in their contributions. When others’ contributions and payoffs indicate occasional strong free-riding by some group members, the player sometimes reduces their own contribution and gains a relative payoff advantage, reflecting a willingness to benefit from others’ generosity and to adjust when inequality disfavors them. However, these adjustments are modest and infrequent; they do not mirror extreme shifts in others’ outcomes or abandon cooperation after observing others profit from low giving. When others’ contributions are generally high and payoffs more equal, the player is willing to sustain or even briefly increase their own contribution, despite the personal payoff disadvantage of over-contributing in such a setting. This indicates some tolerance for inequality and a mild cooperative preference that is not fully contingent on being treated “fairly” in each round. Because no punishment or reward mechanisms are available, there is no direct evidence of how they would spend resources to sanction or support others; their only observable “response” channel is adjusting contributions, which they use in a measured, non-extreme way.

text: This player observes that others often earn more than they do when they contribute fully, especially those who systematically contribute less. Nonetheless, they continue to contribute at or near the maximum in many subsequent rounds, indicating a relatively high tolerance for disadvantageous payoff comparisons. They do not escalate defection after seeing others profit from low contributions; instead, their rare free-riding or partial contribution episodes are brief, and they promptly return to cooperative levels. They do not have access to formal punishment or reward, so direct sanctioning cannot be observed. Behaviorally, they do not appear to engage in sustained informal “punishment” via prolonged low contributions. Even after others obtain conspicuously high payoffs while contributing little, this player’s response is limited and temporary. This suggests a personality inclined toward maintaining group efficiency over enforcing strict equity through retaliatory defection. At the same time, the existence of any low-contribution rounds shows that they are not indifferent to payoff structure: they sometimes exploit cooperative surroundings for high personal payoffs or at least reduce losses when cooperation feels one-sided. Overall, they seem to be a mostly cooperative, norm-maintaining type who notices payoff differences but only weakly converts those into sustained retaliatory behavior.

text: They are highly responsive to others’ contribution patterns but only indirectly to others’ payoffs, since there is no punishment or reward mechanism used in this game. When others maintain high contributions, they often drop their own to low levels and enjoy higher personal payoffs than most of the group, implying that they notice and act on payoff opportunities created by others’ cooperation. When contributions by some group members become more erratic or low, they are willing to increase their own giving to restore the size of the public pot and keep their earnings from collapsing. They tolerate substantial payoff inequality in their favor; there is no sign they try to align their own earnings with the group’s or “compensate” for others doing well or poorly. Because there is no active sanctioning or rewarding, and the player never has the option to directly change others’ payoffs, their reactions remain purely via their own contribution choices. In personality terms, this reflects a pragmatic, instrumental view of others: they treat group members primarily as part of the payoff environment, adjusting to their cooperation levels to sustain personally advantageous outcomes rather than to enforce or honor a fairness norm.

text: The player has full information about others’ contributions and payoffs, but their behavior suggests only weak behavioral updating in response. They observe recurring patterns: some players consistently contribute high amounts, others vary more or occasionally free-ride. Yet the focal player’s own contributions stay remarkably close to their initial level over time. They do not systematically increase contributions to support or “reward” high contributors, nor do they systematically reduce contributions to “punish” or discipline low contributors. When another player free-rides and earns a high payoff from the group’s contributions, the focal player does not respond with a lasting drop in their own contributions. Similarly, when some players bear a high cost by contributing a lot and receive relatively lower payoffs, the focal player does not reciprocate by moving closer to their level. Their one round of full free-riding coincides with others’ substantial contributions, yielding them a high personal payoff. However, because they return immediately to their previous cooperative baseline and do not enter a persistent exploitative mode, this seems more like an opportunistic deviation than a principled response to prior inequities. Overall, the player appears aware of payoffs but not highly outcome-reactive. They neither engage in strong fairness correction (seeking to align outcomes) nor in payoff-driven retaliation or reward. Their primary reference point seems to be their internal contribution rule rather than others’ moment-to-moment outcomes.

text: Others’ contributions are generally high and quite consistent, with several players repeatedly contributing substantially more than the standard free-rider benchmark. The focal player experiences multiple episodes where they contribute significantly less than others yet achieve higher payoffs than many of those more cooperative peers. They continue to employ this pattern after seeing such favorable outcomes, indicating that observing these payoff asymmetries reinforces their willingness to free-ride. At other times, after stretches of lower giving, they revert to high contributions while others remain cooperative. This implies they do not interpret others’ steady cooperation as an invitation to permanently defect; instead, they intermittently “reset” to high contributions, perhaps to preserve the public good level or to avoid feeling that they are always exploiting. However, there is no direct evidence of fairness-driven “repayment”; these higher contributions do not systematically follow being advantaged relative to others, and low contributions reappear even after renewed cooperation. Because their peers’ contributions rarely collapse in response to their under-giving, the player receives little negative feedback in the form of reduced group contributions. They appear to notice that the group is resiliently cooperative and exploit this by periodically lowering their own contributions to boost personal payoffs. The consistent coexistence of others’ high cooperation and the focal player’s intermittent low contributions, without visible adjustment toward full reciprocity, supports the view that they prioritize individual payoff over strict conditional cooperation. Punishment and reward are unavailable, so there is no behavioral evidence of how they would use or respond to direct sanctions or bonuses. Their observed behavior—continuing to under-contribute even when others stay generous—suggests they would be relatively insensitive to purely moral or reputational considerations in the absence of material enforcement, but this is low-confidence since no explicit social feedback is shown.

text: This player pays implicit attention to others’ outcomes but does not tightly condition their strategy on individual payoff or punishment histories. They see that most others consistently contribute and that some spend extremely large amounts on punishment; they nonetheless continue to experiment with free riding and only moderate their behavior gradually. They do not show strong conformism to the most aggressive punishers’ style; even in periods where others burn large sums to sanction defectors, they keep their own sanctions relatively small. This suggests a personality that is observant but not imitative in a simple way—they register that “the group punishes,” but they do not fully join the high-cost enforcement norm. When others incur heavy punishment or suffer sharp payoff reductions, the player does not visibly rush to defend or counterbalance these outcomes. Likewise, when some players escape punishment or earn high payoffs, they are willing to place themselves in that high-earning position by free riding, without apparent discomfort. Their reaction to others’ outcomes is pragmatic: they learn that cooperation is stable and that defection is punishable, and then they alternate between the two, rather than deeply committing to either pure cooperation or pure exploitation. In summary, this player behaves like a moderately opportunistic, norm-aware but not norm-faithful participant: they recognize cooperative and punitive structures, partially respond to them, but continue to test the boundaries between self-interest and group norms throughout the game.

text: This player is clearly attentive to the group’s outcomes and sanctioning patterns. They track who is cooperating and how much punishment others are dispensing, and they adjust mainly through contributions rather than through heavy punishment of their own. In highly cooperative, low-punishment phases—where everyone earns large, similar payoffs—they settle into stable full cooperation and virtually no sanctioning, implicitly endorsing the equilibrium. When they see chaotic or heavy punishment environments, they remain comparatively restrained and do not join in large-scale destructive sanctioning, indicating aversion to wasteful conflict and perhaps a preference for efficiency. They tolerate significant asymmetries in others’ punishment costs and payoffs without trying to balance them out via additional sanctions. Their limited, targeted punishment suggests they react more to clear norm violations or personal slights than to fine differences in earnings. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes mainly by choosing whether to maintain or withdraw cooperation, while leaving most of the “policing” and payoff reallocation to more aggressive players.

text: They appear largely unaffected by how others allocate payoff to punishment or by the large swings in other players’ earnings. During periods when some players burn huge amounts of coins on sanctions and create big negative or low payoffs for themselves or others, this player does not mirror that behavior or shift their own contributions. They also do not exploit or react to others’ misfortune or heavy self-inflicted losses. When others free-ride or gain more by contributing zero, they do not reduce their own contributions to restore payoff parity. When others are harshly punished, they do not join in or intervene. This indicates a weak sensitivity to relative payoffs and a low tendency to be emotionally or strategically pulled by visible inequality, conflict, or chaos. Their primary reference point seems to be an internal cooperative rule (“contribute fully”) rather than the fluctuating distribution of outcomes in the group. Overall, they behave as a stable, non-punishing full cooperator who largely ignores others’ sanctioning behavior and payoff swings, maintaining their strategy independently of immediate social feedback.

text: The player observes extreme patterns in others’ outcomes: some players invest huge amounts in punishment, some are heavily punished, some repeatedly defect, and yet the focal player’s choices remain steady. They do not appear to reward high cooperators with leniency because they are already not punishing anyone; likewise, they do not join in when others collectively punish defectors or “antisocial punishers.” Their contribution behavior is also mostly insensitive to the volatility in others’ payoffs and sanctioning costs. This indicates low reactivity to others’ payoff levels and sanctioning behavior; they attend enough to see who is punishing and who is being punished, but they do not let this drive their own strategy in a strong way. They modestly adjust at a few points (a small amount of punishment in a noisy environment, and a couple of rounds of zero contribution amid widespread non-contribution), which suggests some awareness and mild experimentation, but the dominant pattern is inertia toward cooperation and non-punishment. In personality terms, this is consistent with a stable, norm-driven cooperator who is tolerant of others’ volatility and conflict, avoids participating in punitive spirals, and continues to contribute even when others’ outcomes would justify egoistic or retaliatory reactions.

text: The player monitors others’ contribution and punishment outcomes and responds primarily to repeated, clear deviations from cooperation, not to incidental payoff differences. They are especially sensitive to a specific peer whose repeated low contributions lead to very high cumulative deductions imposed by many group members; the focal player joins in this “collective punishment” but at a moderate scale compared to the heaviest punishers. This points to a fairness norm focused on contribution behavior (cooperate vs defect) rather than on fine‑grained payoff equality. They do not react to others being punished per se: when cooperators sanction each other for reasons unknown, the focal player does not intervene with counter‑punishment or sympathy punishment, nor do they change targets to defend or pile on beyond their own norm‑enforcement target. They also ignore large punishment expenditures by some others that greatly reduce those others’ payoffs; such over‑punishing does not incite them to copy that intensity or to correct it. Their responses to others’ outcomes are thus fairly reserved: they use outcome information to identify consistent free‑riders and to confirm that the group is otherwise cooperative, but they neither micromanage others’ earnings nor chase status or dominance via payoffs. Overall, they behave as a principled cooperator and selective norm enforcer who is resilient to being punished and focused on maintaining high group contributions rather than optimizing relative payoffs.

text: The player pays implicit attention to others’ actions but reacts mainly to extreme deviations in contributions, not to the detailed payoff or punishment structure. They tolerate substantial punishment “wars” among other players without joining in and do not seem to adjust their own contribution in response to seeing others incur huge losses from punishing or being punished. Their only strong behavioral reaction to group outcomes is the single episode where the entire group coordinates on zero contribution; there they also contribute zero, aligning with the apparent group norm in that instance. Outside of that, their behavior is robust to fluctuations in others’ payoffs, punishments, and deductions. They also do not exploit temporarily weakened or heavily punished players; instead, they continue to fully cooperate even when some group members are effectively being driven to very low payoffs by others. This insensitivity to others’ short-term payoffs, coupled with sensitivity to stark contribution deviations, points to a norm-focused social orientation: they track who is cooperating versus free-riding, but do not micro-adjust behavior based on who is rich, poor, or aggressively punishing.

text: This player is highly attentive to others’ contribution choices and resulting punishment outcomes. They respond strongly when someone benefits from the public good while contributing less or nothing: such players receive substantial punishment from them, especially when defection is stark and repeated. The fact that they keep punishing certain low contributors over multiple episodes suggests they are tracking reputations and attempting to correct persistent norm violators. They do not appear to be influenced by others’ payoff levels alone; high or low payoffs among cooperators do not trigger punishment from them. What matters is whether observed outcomes stem from unfair contribution behavior. When the group converges to universal full contribution and payoffs are uniformly high, they cease punishing entirely, indicating satisfaction with the outcome distribution and the underlying behavior. They also coexist with other strong punishers who sometimes spend far more than they do. There is no evidence that they try to “free-ride on punishers” by reducing their own enforcement simply because others are punishing heavily; they continue to invest in punishment when they see violations, although somewhat less extremely than some peers. This supports an image of them as a consistent, norm-driven enforcer: they want outcomes where everyone cooperates and no one exploits the group, and they act—at personal cost—to push the group toward that state.

text: They visibly monitor others’ contributions and the distribution of punishment and payoffs, and they use this information to guide early‑game sanctions. When they see extreme free‑riding amid mostly full contributions, they are prepared to pay for punishment, consistent with an expectation that everyone should benefit proportionally from shared efforts. However, they do not adjust their own contributions downward in a sustained way in response to others’ free‑riding; instead, they prefer to keep their own behavior cooperative and use punishment selectively to push defectors into line. When other players (especially some “enforcer” types) spend large amounts on punishment, they do not mirror that intensity; they remain more moderate, indicating that they are not simply imitating the most punitive peers. They also do not appear to reward “heroes” who spend heavily to punish defectors, nor do they try to exploit the high cooperators by free‑riding on their efforts. Overall, they seem to interpret others’ outcomes mainly as signals about whether norms are being upheld, adjusting their own use of punishment and briefly testing defection, but ultimately reverting to stable high cooperation once the environment looks ordered and predictable.

text: This player responds very sensitively to others’ choices and payoffs in terms of norm compliance, but less so in terms of envy or payoff comparisons. They show strong behavioral reactions to visible free-riding: when one player repeatedly ends up with higher private payoffs due to not contributing, this focal player escalates severe punishment toward that individual. The pattern suggests they pay attention to who benefits from the public good while not bearing equal cost, and they are willing to spend heavily to change that person’s outcome. They also appear to monitor broader group behavior: when multiple players occasionally defect or when punishment activity is high across the group, their own punishment pattern sometimes becomes more intense or more widely distributed. Once the group converges on everyone contributing fully and punishment activity in general becomes modest or targeted by others, they withdraw from punishing and simply enjoy the high, equal payoffs. They do not show signs of punishing successful cooperators or equal earners, implying limited envy and a primary concern with fairness relative to contribution, not with absolute or relative income per se. When others spend heavily on punishment, they do not mirror that behavior by increasing their own punishment just because others are doing so; their heavy punishment is tied to perceived norm violations, not to matching peers’ aggression. Conversely, when others’ outcomes become uniformly high and symmetric in a cooperation regime, they stabilize into a peaceful, fully cooperative pattern, indicating satisfaction with that outcome and no desire to reintroduce sanctions. Overall, this player appears as a strong-cooperator, strong-enforcer type: they contribute reliably, tolerate personal punishment without clear retaliation, and react sharply when others undermine cooperation, especially persistent free-riders, but they are content to relax punishment once the group achieves stable full cooperation.

text: They are repeatedly exposed to dramatic and sometimes seemingly irrational punishment patterns around them: some players incur very large punishment costs, some players receive massive deductions, and certain free-riders are heavily targeted. Throughout, this focal player does not adjust their own behavior in a way that mirrors or amplifies these dynamics. They do not selectively join in punishing heavy free-riders when the rest of the group does so. They largely stand apart from the sanctioning “wars,” even when the targets are clear and norm violations are extreme. This implies that seeing others expend large amounts on punishment does not tempt them into coordinated norm enforcement or moralistic aggression. At the same time, they also do not reward or react positively (via reduced punishment or increased contributions) toward players who themselves spend heavily to enforce norms. Their contribution policy is almost invariant to others’ payoff outcomes and sanctioning decisions: whether someone loses a lot to punishment or spends heavily to police others has no clear effect on how this player contributes or punishes. In later phases, when the group converges on universal full contribution with minimal punishment, they stay with their existing cooperative pattern without trying to influence residual sanctioning between other players. They appear to treat others’ outcomes and sanction expenditures as background information rather than as signals that should change their own strategic stance. Overall, this defines a “steadfast cooperator, minimally punitive, non-retaliatory, and weakly responsive to third-party conflicts.” They prioritize playing their own cooperative rule, accept being occasionally punished or surrounded by punishment noise, and do not engage deeply with the punishment system as a tool for shaping others’ behavior.

text: They appear attentive to others’ outcomes and use them to update about norms and threats but not to micromanage their own actions. They observe when certain players are hit with extremely heavy punishment for under-contributing and subsequently maintain or resume full contributions, consistent with recognizing that the group is prepared to enforce cooperation strongly. They do not mimic the extreme punishment levels of the most aggressive enforcers. Even when others spend large amounts on punishment, they keep their own punishment expenditures moderate, suggesting they see the marginal benefit of additional punishment as diminishing or excessive. This indicates some sensitivity to efficiency: they participate in sanctioning but avoid the most wasteful extremes. They do not appear to condition their own contribution level on idiosyncratic payoff noise or small payoff differences across players; instead, they respond to clear structural patterns, such as everyone contributing versus one player repeatedly free-riding. When the whole group collapses to zero contribution for a round, they note the absence of gains and revert to full contribution once the cooperative pattern reappears, without trying to punish or reward anyone for that temporary collapse. In summary, they interpret others’ outcomes mainly as signals of the current social norm and enforcement environment. They adapt their own punishment and contribution in line with those perceived norms while maintaining a primarily cooperative, moderately enforcing persona.

text: They are exposed to a wide variety of others’ outcomes: heavy punishment campaigns against particular defectors, extreme overuse of punishment by some players, and occasional mild sanctions among cooperators. Throughout, they do not visibly alter their contribution or punishment strategy in reaction to shifts in others’ payoffs or sanctioning intensity, beyond the early exploratory phase. They do not appear to strategically ally with or avoid any specific player based on punishment behavior or payoff trajectories; instead, they maintain a uniform cooperative stance regardless of who is currently being hurt or who is spending large amounts on punishment. They also do not exploit opportunities created by others’ weakened positions (e.g., continuing to cooperate even when some players are heavily diminished by punishment and could be easy targets for exploitation). Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is muted and norm-stable: they seem to treat others’ payoffs and sanction expenditures as background information rather than as levers for their own strategic adjustments. This pattern is consistent with a player whose primary reference point is “what the group overall is doing in contributions,” rather than detailed payoff differentials or interpersonal conflicts.

text: This player observes large variations in others’ outcomes—extreme punishments, heavy punishment spending, negative or very low payoffs for some, and high payoffs for most cooperators. Their own behavior shows very limited sensitivity to these variations. They neither join the punishment waves against erratic, high‑spend punishers nor retaliate against those who target them or others. They also do not withdraw cooperation when they see full cooperators getting punished or when the group briefly collapses to zero contributions; their only defection coincides with universal defection, not with punishment patterns per se. When the environment becomes orderly and cooperative again, they simply continue their baseline cooperative behavior without trying to “correct” others via punishment or to exploit those who are weakened by previous sanctions. Even when they finally do punish the chronic defector, they do it once and then revert to non‑punishment, suggesting they do not continually adjust to others’ payoffs or chase equity across players over time. Overall, they interpret others’ outcomes as largely their own business unless a player persistently free rides on a cooperative group. Even then, their intervention is limited and late. This makes them a principled, cooperation‑first player: they focus on maintaining their own high contribution standard, tolerate noisy and even unfair punishment patterns, and use punishment only sparingly and selectively in response to clear, persistent exploitation rather than to every observable misalignment of payoffs.

text: They see detailed information about others’ payoffs, punishment spending, and deductions, and they do adjust behavior in light of these outcomes, but in a restrained way. They observe that massive, costly punishment campaigns are occurring among some other players; however, they neither join these extreme sanctioning sprees nor mirror their scale. Instead, they maintain a relatively low-cost punishment profile and emphasize securing high payoffs through continued cooperation. When they defect and experience large payoff losses due to others’ punishment, they react by returning to cooperation rather than escalating conflict. Similarly, when the group is fully cooperative and punishment is low or absent, they do not try to exploit the situation, except in a few isolated defection rounds that are quickly reversed. Their use of punishment after observing others’ outcomes suggests they understand that sanctions can shape incentives, but they are cautious about over-spending on it. They do not appear to systematically target high-earning players or low-earning victims; instead, their limited punishment is tied mainly to observable free-riding. This points to a player who cares more about maintaining a functioning cooperative environment than about redistributing payoffs or expressing envy. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes in a measured, norm-focused way, with an emphasis on sustaining group cooperation and their own steady earnings rather than engaging in payoff-destructive feuds.

text: The player is strongly responsive to the fact that others often earn less when they contribute and more when they defect, and they exploit that structure. Repeatedly, when the group is cooperative, their defection yields higher personal payoffs than those of contributors, and they maintain or return to defection rather than “correcting” this advantage. This suggests low concern for efficiency losses borne by others and low aversion to advantageous inequality. Their occasional cooperation when others also cooperate suggests some sensitivity to group patterns and perhaps a weak desire not to stand out entirely as a non-contributor when everyone else is giving. However, this is inconsistent and easily overridden by self-interest; they often continue to take the higher resulting payoff without reciprocating the group’s generosity in subsequent decisions. Because there is no punishment or reward mechanism in this game, we do not observe direct responses to being sanctioned or rewarded. However, they repeatedly observe outcome distributions where cooperators and defectors earn different payoffs and do not adjust toward more equitable patterns. This indicates that seeing others’ worse outcomes under cooperation does not trigger compensatory behavior. Their learning appears to be payoff-based rather than norm-based: outcomes inform them about which action is individually profitable, and they act accordingly, with only modest and unstable cooperative deviations. Their social preference profile thus appears as low altruism, low guilt or shame from profiting at others’ expense, and limited, context-dependent willingness to join cooperative episodes.

text: The player is clearly outcome-sensitive and observant, adjusting behavior in light of how much others contribute and how payoffs distribute. When they see that others’ cooperation reliably generates high public-good returns, they often choose to free-ride rather than matching those contributions, capturing high payoffs relative to cooperators. This indicates acceptance of advantageous inequality and low aversion to benefiting more than others when they contribute. At the same time, they do not ignore group performance. When overall contributions and payoffs drop, they sometimes respond by contributing fully, which improves both their own and the group’s returns. This suggests they attend to the mapping from collective contributions to personal payoff and are willing to cooperate when marginal benefits to themselves are high enough, but without strong concern for reducing others’ disadvantage. They do not have access to punishment or reward in this environment, so there is no direct evidence of sanctioning motives. Indirectly, their continued willingness to free-ride in the presence of high cooperators implies low sensitivity to the possibility of social disapproval or norm enforcement in comparable settings. In social-personality terms, they present as a calculating, payoff-focused agent who monitors others’ behavior and outcomes closely, willingly exploits cooperative environments, occasionally helps lift group outcomes when it remains profitable, and does not reliably adhere to reciprocity or fairness norms. Their learning and adaptation appear grounded in payoff comparison rather than in moral or relationship-based concerns.

text: The player clearly conditions on the general cooperation climate and associated payoffs, but in a coarse, group-level way rather than reacting to specific individuals. When others’ outcomes indicate a highly cooperative environment with strong public-good payoffs, the player is often willing to withhold their own contribution and benefit from the others’ effort. This yields them higher personal payoffs relative to those who consistently contribute. Conversely, when others’ outcomes reveal weaker group contributions and lower returns from the public good, the player is prepared to contribute, accepting a lower relative payoff than free-riders in that situation to help sustain or improve the group outcome. They do not appear to single out particular co-players as “targets” of reciprocity or retribution; their shifts in behavior correspond more to the overall level of group contribution and payoff than to any identifiable dyadic pattern. They also show no categorical withdrawal in response to the presence of persistent defectors: instead of fully joining the low contributors when some players under-contribute, they continue to mix contributions and defection. This suggests they are not strongly spiteful; they tolerate some free-riding in the group while still sometimes contributing themselves. Because explicit punishment and reward mechanisms are disabled in this setting, there is no direct evidence of how they would spend costly punishment or rewards. Indirectly, though, their willingness to keep contributing in environments that contain visible non-contributors implies they are not highly punitive or focused on enforcing strict equality of payoffs. They tolerate payoff asymmetries when it benefits them and also when it occasionally costs them, as long as the overall environment remains reasonably profitable. In summary, the player responds to others’ outcomes by adjusting their own contribution level at the group level rather than at the individual level, combining opportunistic free-riding during cooperative phases with selective support for the public good when cooperation appears fragile or declining.

text: The player observes others’ contributions and payoffs but shows only modest, irregular adaptation to them. When others achieve higher payoffs through free-riding, this does not trigger a persistent shift to defection. The occasional non-contribution episodes could be mild “tests” or short-lived responses to perceived unfairness, but because they quickly revert to contributing and do not sustain retaliation, any such response is weak and inconsistent. They do not exhibit clear conditional cooperation of the form “contribute when most others do, defect when many defect.” Instead, they contribute even in mixed or low-cooperation environments and continue doing so after being repeatedly out-earned by free-riders. This implies relatively low sensitivity to disadvantageous inequality and a limited tendency toward payoff-based revenge or mimicry. Since explicit punishment and reward tools are unavailable in this environment, direct sanctioning behavior is unobservable. Indirectly, the lack of persistent downward adjustment in contributions suggests they are not strongly outcome-reactive. They appear willing to bear recurring payoff losses relative to exploiters without escalating defection, which aligns with a tolerant, norm-stable social style. In summary, the player tracks others’ outcomes but largely stays with their own cooperative rule. Their reactions to others’ higher payoffs are muted: brief, opportunistic free-riding appears, but not systematic conditional defection or harsh payoff-driven updating.

text: The player sees detailed payoff information and others’ contribution choices but has no access to punishment or reward tools in this setup. Their responses thus appear only through subsequent contribution decisions. They show no behavioral sign of reciprocity in the sense of “rewarding” past cooperators with their own contributions or “sanctioning” defectors by withholding contributions when it would hurt those specific players. Instead, they continue to free-ride even on groups where many have just cooperated and earned less. They are evidently comfortable generating outcome inequalities in their favor. At the same time, they are not purely exploitative: they sometimes join in highly cooperative environments and take the same payoff as other contributors, suggesting they do value the large group benefit when it still provides them a high individual return. Their main reaction to others’ outcomes is instrumental: they use the observed profile of contributions and payoffs to decide whether they can safely exploit contributions or whether joining in is equally or more profitable. There is no clear sign of guilt, spite, or altruistic concern for the disadvantaged; their behavior remains focused on maximizing their own payoff given others’ visible tendencies. If they hold beliefs about deservingness or fairness, those beliefs do not constrain them from repeatedly free-riding on cooperators. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes as informational signals about profitable strategies rather than as moral cues.

text: The player frequently ends up with lower payoffs than free-riders in the same periods but does not systematically adjust in response. After being exploited, they continue to contribute fully. After achieving relatively high payoffs in cooperative environments, they also continue in the same way. This insensitivity to relative earnings indicates they do not strongly track or react to distributional advantage/disadvantage in others’ outcomes. They do not attempt to align their behavior with payoff-maximizing types, nor do they seem to punish by imitation (switching to free-riding after defectors gain more). Their single free-riding episode is not followed by sustained exploitation of cooperators, and it is not clearly tied to any particular pattern of others’ payoffs afterward. Whether that deviation reflects a deliberate experiment, a brief temptation to exploit, or noise is unknown; what is clear is that it does not establish a new pattern. In environments where group contributions surge, they do not “reward” that by changing their own behavior—because they are already at the cooperative ceiling. Conversely, when cooperation falls and defectors earn more, they do not retaliate or protect themselves by lowering their contributions. They accept others’ outcome differences as a given and persist in their chosen cooperative stance. Because punishment and reward mechanisms are disabled, there is no observable costly response to others’ earnings beyond contribution choices themselves. Based on those choices, the player appears tolerant of payoff inequality and exploitation, prioritizing adherence to a cooperative rule over reacting to others’ success at free-riding.

text: The player frequently observes that some others who contribute less (or nothing) in a round earn more than they do in that same round. Despite this, they never change their own action. This implies that relative disadvantage and visible free-riding do not trigger retaliatory behavior. They also do not appear to reward higher contributors in any active sense, because they do not have any extra levers besides their own contribution, and they never adjust their contribution contingent on seeing higher or lower group contributions. They show no detectable payoff-based adaptation: higher personal earnings in more cooperative group states and lower earnings in more selfish group states do not prompt them to modulate their behavior. They do not try to match the group’s average, punish low contributors by defecting, or “reward” cooperative episodes by escalating beyond maximum (which is impossible in this binary setting). Instead, they remain fixed at full contribution regardless of how outcomes are distributed among players. Their beliefs about others’ motives and future behavior are unknown, but operationally they act as if others’ actions and payoffs are largely irrelevant to their own choice rule. In type terms, this player functions as a pure, non-conditional cooperator: they pursue full cooperation as a standing policy, accept being exploited by free-riders, and ignore both the finite horizon and observable payoff inequalities when choosing their contribution.

text: The player sees detailed information about others’ contributions and payoffs and experiences many situations where defectors earn more than they do. However, immediately afterward they revert to full cooperation and then stay cooperative despite continued inequality. This implies that, while they notice payoff differences, they do not allow those differences to control their behavior for long. They show no sign of strategic matching such as “cooperate only if most others cooperate” or “defect when others defect.” Instead, they appear to treat others’ outcomes as background information rather than as a trigger for sustained behavioral change. They neither escalate cooperation in response to generous rounds (because they are already maxing out) nor punish low contributors by reducing their own contributions. There is no evidence of retaliatory motives based on observed payoffs. Because the environment lacks punishment and reward mechanisms, we cannot observe how they would use such tools. It remains unknown whether, if costly punishment or reward were available, they would enforce norms or compensate cooperators. All that can be inferred is that, in the absence of such levers, they accept outcome inequalities and continue to cooperate. In summary, they are outcome-aware but outcome-resistant: they register that defectors often do better, test defection once, and then recommit to a cooperative path, indicating that maintaining their contribution rule matters more to them than eliminating disadvantageous payoff gaps.

text: The player’s choices closely track not just others’ contributions but also the payoff structure those contributions create. At the same time, they also join in and contribute fully when group cooperation is high and the public good returns are strong. In those periods, they seem content to earn roughly similar payoffs to other contributors, suggesting that they value the high absolute returns generated by cooperation and do not require being individually best off in every round. This mix implies that their response to others’ outcomes is nuanced: they are attracted to high-payoff, cooperative states, but they also opportunistically shift to exploitation when the margin from freeriding becomes especially attractive. There is no direct evidence of emotional or moral reaction to inequality—only behavioral adaptation. They tolerate earning less than defectors in some cooperative phases, then later adopt the defector role themselves. This indicates relatively low aversion to payoff asymmetries per se; inequality is acceptable if it is in their favor, and occasionally accepted even when it is not, as long as the absolute level of earnings is high or cooperation seems worth preserving. Because punishment and reward are unavailable in this game, we do not observe any costly moves to change others’ payoffs. However, given their pattern, they appear unlikely to engage in strong altruistic or spiteful sanctioning even if it were available: they do not behave like a norm crusader but rather like someone primarily optimizing their own earnings in response to the existing contribution environment. In new PGGs, expect them to monitor others’ contributions and resulting payoffs closely and to alternate between joining cooperative clusters and exploiting them, rather than rigidly sticking to one moral stance.

text: The player is outcome-aware and appears to condition their own behavior on how others’ payoffs compare to theirs, but their response is indirect because there is no explicit punishment or reward tool in this game. They do not exhibit a stable pattern of “reciprocal fairness” (e.g., always matching the cooperative majority so that payoffs converge). At other times, after observing that non-contributors are doing well relative to contributors, they shift into a free-riding phase, which suggests that salient payoff asymmetries eventually push them toward self-serving action. They also do not show clear evidence of being strongly demotivated by the presence of chronic low contributors: they continue to contribute in several environments where non-cooperation is prevalent or payoffs to defectors are higher. In sum, the player pays attention to others’ contributions and resulting payoffs and adapts in a way that mixes opportunism with intermittent willingness to support the group. They are sensitive to payoff structures but do not enforce or follow a rigid equity norm; instead, they flexibly alternate roles between cooperator and beneficiary of others’ cooperation, without using explicit social sanctions (unavailable here) and without consistent, strong reciprocity in either direction.

text: The player monitors others’ contributions and outcomes and adapts behavior in a nuanced but not strictly consistent way. They also observes that some players persistently contribute little yet still earn high payoffs, and in response, they occasionally shift toward low contributions themselves, exploiting cooperative environments. Overall, this player’s type is best described as a “strategic conditional cooperator with opportunistic tendencies and an active but selective reward motive”: they cares about cooperation and sometimes invests in it, uses rewards as a norm-shaping tool despite personal cost, but remains willing to reduce contributions and exploit cooperative groups when circumstances appear favorable.

text: This player pays close implicit attention to who benefits or suffers each round. They repeatedly sanction those whose outcomes reflect very low contributions combined with little or no prior cost-bearing, especially when these players nevertheless receive substantial public-good payoffs. When a player with very low contributions is heavily sanctioned by many others, this player sometimes still adds more punishment later, suggesting they are not deterred by the fact that the target is already doing poorly; they continue to prioritize norm enforcement over concern for the target’s welfare. Conversely, they ignore high contributors’ high payoffs and do not attempt to balance outcomes via reward, indicating they do not aim at equality of payoffs but at punishing free-riding. They occasionally punish someone who contributes nothing while others are contributing a lot, even if that person’s current payoff is already poor due to sanctions, which is consistent with an anti-exploitation stance rather than outcome-based pity. Their own payoff remains relatively strong despite these costs, so they seem willing to trade off some individual earnings for a reputation (anonymous at the individual level, but still a behavioral stance) of being tough on defectors. Their adjustments in contribution after observing general drops in group giving suggest they monitor the overall cooperative climate and modestly adapt, but without abandoning their high-contribution identity. Overall, they act as a principled, sanction-focused cooperator who uses others’ outcomes—especially the combination of low contribution and high benefit—as cues for targeted punishment rather than for redistribution or compensation.

text: This player observes a complex social environment with heavy punishment activity, some players repeatedly sinking targets’ payoffs far below zero or well below the group, and some players spending substantial amounts on rewards. Despite this, their own behavioral levers (contribution, punishment, reward) remain relatively insulated from these outcome patterns. They see that some individuals are consistently hit with very large punishments, often after very low contributions, and that others invest heavily in both punishment and reward. Yet they neither join these efforts nor evidently adjust their contribution in a way that tracks specific individuals’ payoffs or sanction histories. Their changes in contribution appear to respond more to the broad level of group contributions than to who is being punished or rewarded and by how much. They also do not display opportunism toward victims of sanctions: they do not reduce contributions to free-ride on the fact that others are already bearing the cost of norm enforcement. Nor do they increase contributions when cooperation and positive rewards surge, beyond a moderate alignment with the general contribution climate. They seem to treat others’ payoff outcomes—especially the extreme negative ones—as information that does not require a personal, strategic response. Personality-wise, this suggests a relatively self-contained, low-reactivity style. They care about cooperating at a reasonable level but do not invest in managing others’ behavior and do not let others’ sanctioning choices dictate their own. Their type can be characterized as a “quiet cooperative individualist”: pro-social in contribution, but disengaged from the social politics of punishment and reward, and largely indifferent in action to the detailed payoff consequences others impose on one another.

text: This player appears to track not only contributions but also how others are treated via punishment and reward. When certain players are subject to repeated, extreme punishment and end up with very low or negative payoffs, the player does not join the punishment; instead, they sometimes respond by rewarding these same or similar low-status participants, indicating sympathy for “victims” of collective harshness or concern for fairness beyond strict contribution-based norms. They do not appear to reward those who spend heavily on punishment, nor do they directly reward heavy punishers even when they contribute fully, suggesting they do not valorize punitive enforcement as a valuable group service. Conversely, they occasionally reward consistent cooperators and those making solid contributions without engaging in extreme sanctioning, which implies they distinguish between “quiet cooperators” and “aggressive enforcers.” They do not visibly condition their own contributions on whether they personally receive rewards or deductions; their giving pattern stays pro-social regardless of personal treatment, suggesting relatively low reciprocity in the narrow, self-centered sense and a more principle-based orientation. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes in a way that softens extremes: they avoid adding to losses for low earners and sometimes channel rewards toward maintaining inclusion and recognizing cooperation, while declining to participate in or endorse severe sanction cascades.

text: This player pays attention to others’ payoffs and their punishment/reward activity and uses that information to adjust their enforcement choices. When they see players earning high incomes while contributing very little, they treat that as unjust free-riding and respond with punishment rather than increasing their own defection. When they see others suffering large punishment costs or deductions for low contributions, they often add to that pressure by continuing to punish the same low contributors, reinforcing group sanctions rather than undermining them. They also notice and positively respond to players who spend their own resources on cooperation-supporting actions. When a player combines solid contributions with spending on reward or measured punishment of free-riders, this player tends to direct rewards toward them, effectively endorsing and subsidizing their prosocial enforcement. In this sense, they not only punish defectors but also “reward the punishers” and other cooperators, amplifying the cooperative core of the group. They do not seem to be motivated by envy of high earners per se. High-payoff players who earn a lot through high contributions or prosocial enforcing are often rewarded or left alone, whereas low contributors who exploit the group to secure high payoffs are punished even at substantial cost to the focal player. This pattern suggests an inequality-averse but norm-conditioned perspective: unequal outcomes are acceptable if they stem from cooperative, norm-consistent behavior, but not if they result from exploitation. Their one large drop in contribution coincides with a context in which some players maintain cooperation while a few persist in low or erratic behavior despite repeated sanctions and rewards. The brief defection likely reflects frustration or experimentation with others’ responses, rather than a wholesale rejection of cooperation. When that low-contribution move triggers direct punishment against them, they do not retaliate. Instead, they immediately resume high contributions without punishing those who sanctioned them. This indicates that they interpret punishment of their own defection as legitimate norm enforcement rather than as a hostile act, and they adjust accordingly. In summary, this player is a “cooperative norm enforcer”: they respond to others’ outcomes and enforcement efforts in a way that supports a high-contribution equilibrium, accepts justified punishment (including when directed at them), and distinguishes between fair, contribution-based high payoffs and unfair, free-riding-based gains.

text: This player is repeatedly and heavily punished for their low contributions but does not visibly adapt their overall stance in a norm-following direction. After receiving large deductions and observing widespread use of rewards and punishments by others, they still continue to free-ride in most subsequent stages, only briefly matching the group’s cooperative norm once and otherwise remaining well below others’ typical contributions. They do not escalate conflict: they never punish back, never target individuals who impose high punishment costs or who free-ride themselves. They also do not join the cooperative “club” via rewards; they ignore the fact that others reward high contributors and that they sometimes receive small rewards. This pattern suggests a detached, payoff-focused type who is relatively unresponsive to reputational and relational cues: social approval, reciprocity, and fear of future sanctions do not strongly redirect their behavior. Learning is limited or highly selective: they may adjust contribution upward temporarily after extreme sanctions, but they do not convert this into a consistent cooperative strategy, and they remain unwilling to incur any cost to shape others’ behavior. Their stance toward others’ outcomes is thus largely observational and passive, not interactive or norm-enforcing.

text: This player is observant enough to adjust their own contribution pattern over time but does not react through the punishment/reward tools, even when others’ outcomes are extreme. They coexist with a group in which several players spend large sums punishing particular low contributors, and some players incur very large negative payoffs as a result. Despite this, the focal player neither joins these sanctioning coalitions nor opposes them via counter‑punishment or protective rewards. They seem willing to let others bear the cost of norm enforcement while continuing to focus on their own contribution decision. After experiencing severe punishment themselves, they change their contribution back to high levels but still refrain from using the sanctioning mechanisms, suggesting that their learning response operates only through their own contribution choices rather than through socially directed actions. They do not appear to condition contributions on who punishes or rewards whom, although they may be reacting to the aggregate environment of high contributions and strong third‑party punishment by reverting to cooperative contributions. Overall, they treat others’ payoffs, punish/reward costs, and deductions as background signals about the general climate rather than as cues for targeted interaction.

text: They pay attention to how others behave and how they are treated, and update their targeting accordingly. When a player with a history of very low contributions later matches or exceeds the group’s contribution norm, they relax their own sanctions toward that player, even if others continue to punish heavily. Conversely, when a new free-rider emerges, they begin punishing that player despite that player’s earlier cooperative history. They do not appear to punish those who already receive heavy sanctions from others purely because they are low in payoff; rather, their focus remains on contribution behavior relative to the group norm. There is no sign that they reward players who spend heavily on enforcing norms, nor that they adjust their behavior to curry favor with strong punishers or generous rewarders. Overall, they respond to outcomes by using others’ contributions and evident norm violations to refine who deserves punishment, while remaining largely indifferent to the distribution of rewards and to others’ individual payoffs except as signals of rule-breaking.

text: The player pays attention to others’ contributions, payoffs, and punishment decisions and updates their own behavior in response, but not in a simple mechanical way. When they see all players contributing nothing, they join the non-cooperative pattern rather than sacrificing to restart public good provision, indicating sensitivity to group norms and the perceived futility of unilateral cooperation. When they see mixed cooperation—some players contributing fully and others not—they sometimes align with the cooperators and sometimes with the defectors, suggesting that they are weighing both payoff asymmetries and recent punishment histories. They also observe how much others spend on punishment and how much is deducted from the group. In some cases, high punishment activity by others is followed by their own use of punishment, implying that they are willing to enter or intensify sanctioning conflicts when they perceive others as aggressive or unfair. They appear especially reactive to situations where some players earn notably higher payoffs at low personal cost; in such contexts, they are more likely to punish heavily or defect, consistent with aversion to being comparatively disadvantaged. However, they do not systematically protect high contributors with punishment or coordinate with them to discipline free-riders, so strong positive reciprocity toward prosocial players is not clearly present. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes with a combination of opportunism (matching low-contribution norms when lucrative) and norm assertion (costly punishment when payoff differences or prior sanctions are perceived as unjust), without settling into a stable cooperative coalition or a purely selfish stance.

text: They observe large payoff differences and others’ punishment expenditures but do not adjust their own contribution strategy in response, suggesting that others’ losses or efforts do not move them toward cooperation. They sometimes punish players who sacrifice to punish others, and at other times punish those who help create high public-good returns, indicating that they are not aligning with either cooperators or norm enforcers in a stable way. Their reactions to others’ outcomes look driven by a desire to maintain or enhance their own relative advantage, or by a loose, non-systematic frustration with how others play, rather than by a clear fairness benchmark. They are comfortable benefiting when others incur costs (through contributions or punishment) and do not reciprocate such sacrifices. Overall, they act as a consistently selfish contributor combined with a noisy or spiteful punisher, largely insensitive to others’ welfare or to the efficiency of group outcomes.

text: The player observes others experiencing large losses from punishment and large swings in payoff, yet does not adjust to imitate punishers, protect frequent targets, or systematically align with any subgroup. They do not step in to “help” those who are heavily punished (no compensating cooperation) and do not appear to exploit vulnerable, heavily sanctioned players with targeted defection or further punishment. Others’ costly sanctioning does not lead them to adopt a more norm-following or more self-protective strategy; they maintain their own oscillating contribution pattern and zero-punishment stance. This indicates a relatively low responsiveness to social signals encoded in others’ payoffs and sanctioning behavior. Their beliefs about others’ intentions and strategies are unknown, but behaviorally they treat others’ outcomes more as background information than as cues that directly guide their own choices.

text: The player pays close attention to others’ outcomes, as inferred from how their contribution and punishment decisions track others’ contribution patterns and payoff differences. They are especially reactive to cases in which another player earns much more while contributing less; these situations are precisely where they choose to punish, often at significant personal cost. This suggests they are sensitive to payoff asymmetries that they perceive as unfair. When everyone behaves similarly and earns similar payoffs, they withhold punishment entirely, consistent with a fairness-oriented rule: equal treatment is acceptable, whether everyone is cooperating or everyone is defecting. When at least one other player cooperates alongside them and one does not, they appear more willing to both cooperate and punish, as if attempting to support a coalition of cooperators and discipline defectors. When cooperative efforts do not lead to group-wide improvement, they drift into free-riding themselves and cease punishing, indicating a shift toward self-protection in response to persistent inequities or unresponsiveness. They do not appear to reward high contributors (no reward option is available, but they also do not refrain from profiting when others contribute and they do not), so their sensitivity to outcomes is asymmetric: they react mainly to perceived negative deviations (someone doing better while doing less) rather than positive deviations.

text: The player demonstrates a relatively muted behavioral response to others’ payoffs and sanctioning efforts. They repeatedly observe that others invest nontrivial amounts in rewarding, and that they personally receive positive rewards even when they defect, yet they do not mirror this behavior nor systematically adjust contributions to match who benefits or who pays. Their decisions do not track the distribution of rewards or relative payoffs in a transparent way: they neither “reward the rewarders” nor punish exploitative reward patterns by reducing contributions. Similarly, when others’ contributions and payoffs later become more uneven and some partners move toward defection, the player does not switch to a clear retaliatory strategy; they maintain cooperation at times even when this leaves them relatively worse off. Overall, they appear relatively insensitive to detailed outcome patterns and fine-grained fairness comparisons across players. Their main behavioral adjustments seem to be coarse (cooperate vs defect overall) rather than conditioned on who is gaining or sacrificing how much. This suggests low responsiveness to outcome-based social cues and a limited use of observed payoffs and reward expenditures to update beliefs or strategies.

text: This player attends to others’ contribution and payoff patterns and adjusts incentives over time. When all peers cooperate, they respond by offering substantial rewards, which is consistent with approval of mutual effort and a desire to support high contributors. When one player repeatedly free-rides while still earning high payoffs, the focal player does not immediately retaliate via defection but does scale back their own reward spending, indicating sensitivity to perceived exploitation and to the cost-effectiveness of further prosocial incentives. Their reactions are nuanced rather than purely tit-for-tat. They continue to contribute even while a free-rider benefits, but their reward behavior becomes more cautious and intermittent, suggesting they differentiate between “cooperation in the public good” (which they maintain for a while) and “extra costly generosity through rewards” (which they reduce when it seems underappreciated or strategically weak). They do not appear to reward players who neither contribute nor reward, nor do they try to offset payoff inequalities via extra transfers; there is no sign of strong inequality aversion expressed through compensatory rewards. In the final stage, when another player still contributes and another defects, the focal player aligns with the non-contributor and provides no rewards, implying that outcomes alone (others getting lower payoffs from still contributing) are no longer sufficient to elicit further generosity. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes by updating their willingness to spend on rewards and, only at the horizon, by changing their contribution level, reflecting a moderately patient but ultimately payoff-aware cooperative type.

text: This player monitors others’ contributions and outcomes but responds in a restrained, non-retaliatory manner. They do not reduce their own contributions in response to others earning high payoffs through free-riding, nor do they try to offset payoff inequalities via the reward mechanism. They sometimes provide reward when others are cooperative, but they do not systematically try to correct for unfair advantages or heavily reinforce those who already benefit. When others spend significantly on rewards or forgo them, the player does not mirror or sharply adjust behavior in the next opportunities; adaptation, if any, is very mild. They tolerate situations in which cooperative effort and rewards are unevenly distributed and do not attempt to rebalance payoffs. Overall, their reaction to others’ outcomes is stable and non-aggressive: they remain cooperative, apply modest positive reinforcement at times, and accept outcome disparities without visible adjustment in contribution strategy.

text: The player monitors others’ **contributions and payoffs** and adjusts primarily through reward and (eventually) their own contribution choice, rather than through any immediately reactive pattern to individual payoff differences. They show **tolerance of unequal outcomes**: at times others earn more than they do, partly through not spending on rewards themselves, yet the player continues to cooperate and sometimes even spends on rewards. This implies that modest disadvantage relative to others does not by itself trigger retaliation or withdrawal. The player seems particularly attuned to **collective contribution profiles**: - When all peers contribute at the high level, the player is both fully cooperative and more inclined to reward, consistent with reading high group contributions and strong outcomes as cues for supportive reinforcement. - When exactly one peer free-rides while the others cooperate, the player maintains full contributions but becomes more restrained or inconsistent in reward spending. This can be interpreted as a **guarded response to mixed group behavior**: they do not punish (not available), but they are less eager to sink additional cost into positive transfers when the social environment shows some free-riding. They do not exhibit clear **envy-driven behavior**: seeing others earn more—either by free-riding or by being rewarded more—doesn’t reliably cause them to cut their contributions in the ongoing middle rounds. Their eventual defection aligns with the end of the game rather than with any specific prior unfairness. They also do not display obvious **competitive motives**; there is no sign of attempts to reduce others’ earnings or to avoid helping high-earning cooperators. When the tool exists, their use of it is primarily to increase others’ payoffs, not to create relative advantage. In summary, the player **takes others’ contributions and outcomes into account at the group level**, supporting high-cooperation states and tolerating some unfairness, but aligns their ultimate decisions with game-theoretic incentives and horizon rather than with emotional reactions to specific payoff differences.

text: They consistently observe others’ outcomes and the extensive punishment activity in the group but maintain their own strategy with only minor adjustment on the punishment dimension. When others’ non-contribution causes substantial payoff losses for the whole group, they do not reduce their own contributions, indicating that decreased personal earnings and visible exploitation do not lead them to withdraw cooperation. At the same time, they are not drawn into the escalating punishment patterns exhibited by several other players; large punishment expenditures and heavy deductions by others do not tempt them to match that intensity. Instead, they remain cost-conscious and selective. Their rare use of punishment coincides with blatant free-riding, implying that they are sensitive to norm violations but try to address them with minimal expenditure. They appear robust to inequality in realized payoffs and to others’ aggressive sanctioning behavior, maintaining a calm, rule-consistent pattern rather than reacting strongly to short-term shocks. Their learning or adaptation is limited: they slightly adjust by using punishment once in response to clear defection, but they do not update their contribution behavior in response to others’ changing contributions or payoffs.
