text: This player closely tracks others’ behavior and outcomes but responds in a self-serving, asymmetric way. When others contribute heavily and earn solid payoffs, they do not respond with gratitude or matching cooperation. Instead, they continue to free-ride and often increase their absolute payoff by capitalizing on the cooperative environment. High group contributions seem to be a cue that it is safe to keep defecting rather than a signal to reciprocate. When a particular peer consistently contributes less and is heavily punished by multiple others, this player joins in or reinforces that punishment, even as the target’s payoffs become very low. The severe and repeated reduction of that peer’s income does not lead this player to moderate or re-evaluate their punishment. This is consistent with a willingness to pile on a disliked or norm-deviant target rather than a precise concern with proportional fairness. Their own experience of heavy punishment does not lead them to change their contribution behavior. After incurring substantial deductions that at times wipe out much of their income, they do not start contributing to avoid further punishment. Instead, they persist as a non-contributor and intermittently continue to punish others. This suggests that they interpret incoming punishment more as a cost of doing business or as hostility from others, not as a valid signal to adjust their behavior toward group norms. They also occasionally stop punishing after having been heavily sanctioned, but this shift is not accompanied by more cooperation. This pattern points to a flexible attitude toward the use of punishment (they can turn it up or down) but a rigid stance on free-riding. Their overriding objective remains maximizing personal gain from others’ contributions, even at the cost of enduring repeated punishment. In summary, this player is a “strategic anti-social free-rider with opportunistic punishment”: they exploit cooperative others at the contribution stage while selectively joining punitive coalitions against more visible defectors, are willing to endure or escalate costly punishment cycles, and do not internalize fairness or reciprocity norms from observing others’ outcomes.

text: This player pays attention to others’ outcomes but reacts selectively. They repeatedly see one player earning high payoffs while free riding and receiving heavy sanctions from multiple group members; instead of withdrawing cooperation in protest or joining in maximal punishment, they maintain full contributions and occasionally add modest punishment, implying they accept a division of labor where others do most of the costly norm enforcement. They do not appear to punish players who are heavily punished by others solely because those players are being targeted; they focus on the free rider’s contribution pattern, not on bandwagoning on someone already suffering payoffs losses. When others free ride in isolated instances, they do not noticeably adjust contributions downward in direct response, nor do they target these short-term defections with punishment, suggesting they are more concerned with systematic patterns than with one-off deviations. They tolerate being punished themselves when they defect and quickly revert to full cooperation rather than escalating conflict or trying to equalize payoffs via counter-punishment. This indicates that they interpret punishment as a legitimate response to defection and are willing to absorb personal losses to restore cooperative play. In summary, their reactions to others’ payoffs and sanctioning behavior align with a norm-supporting, relatively forgiving cooperator who attends to sustained contribution behavior and accepts some inequities as the cost of maintaining group-level cooperation.

text: This player appears relatively unmoved by others’ outcomes. They experience repeated, often severe punishment for free-riding yet do not adjust toward sustained cooperation. Their contribution behavior changes over time, but not in a way that clearly tracks the intensity or pattern of sanctions: they sometimes start contributing fully after being punished, but then later revert to free-riding even when punishment risk remains high. They do not respond symmetrically to others’ behavior. Cooperative co-players who consistently contribute and sometimes incur large punishment costs receive no support or reciprocal enforcement from them. Likewise, they do not single out chronic defectors for punishment, even though those defectors directly reduce the public good from which they benefit. This suggests that others’ payoffs, costs, and sanctioning efforts do not strongly enter into their utility except insofar as these affect their own payoff. Their tolerance for being a repeated punishment target, without retaliating or shifting permanently to high contributions, suggests either emotional detachment from interpersonal aspects of the game or a narrow focus on immediate expected payoff. They appear to accept that others will sometimes reduce their income as a cost of free-riding, and they simply continue optimizing within that environment rather than trying to reshape it via punishment. In summary, this player treats others’ outcomes—including large losses they cause or large costs others bear—mainly as background conditions, not as moral signals to which they must respond. Their behavioral “type” is a non-punishing, conditional-but-leaning-defector who adapts contributions somewhat to the cooperation level but does not internalize group-oriented fairness or enforcement norms.

text: This player pays implicit attention to others’ outcomes— both contributions and resulting payoffs— and adjusts their punishment use in line with whether someone is perceived to be benefiting “unfairly” from the group. When others with low or zero contributions still end up with strong payoffs relative to cooperators, they are more likely to spend to punish. When everyone’s payoffs are broadly aligned and high under mutual cooperation, they often refrain from punishing and focus on maintaining their own cooperative strategy. They do not appear to escalate contributions or withdraw cooperation in direct response to being punished; instead, they leave their contribution pattern essentially unchanged. This suggests that they interpret incoming punishment either as noise, as aimed at others, or as less important than their own commitment to cooperation. Their reaction to being punished is expressed (if at all) via changes in punishment behavior, not via reduced contributions. However, there is no strong, consistent pattern of “punish back when punished,” so explicit retaliation against punishers is uncertain or low-confidence. They seem relatively insensitive to short-term payoff comparisons in the contribution decision: even when others who contribute less sometimes end up with equal or higher payoffs due to punishment patterns or group composition, this does not cause them to defect in the next opportunities. Instead, they use the punishment mechanism as their main tool for addressing payoff imbalances and norm violations. In this sense, their response to observing others’ high earnings from low contributions is to try to correct it via sanctions rather than by personally free-riding. Socially, this behavior fits a type who sees themselves as a cooperative norm enforcer: they want a high-contribution, fair-outcome environment and are willing to expend resources to move the group toward that, while personally continuing to cooperate even in the face of some exploitation and occasional unjustified punishment. Their adaptation over time is limited; they show more consistency than learning in their core behaviors, maintaining high contributions and a readiness to punish defectors from start to finish.

text: This player pays attention to others’ contributions and outcomes and responds in a pattern consistent with strong aversion to free‑rider advantage. They continue to contribute fully even when defectors temporarily earn higher payoffs, indicating they do not immediately chase relative income; instead, they later deploy punishment very heavily against chronic non‑contributors, even to the point of driving a target’s payoff into strongly negative territory. This suggests they are sensitive to the perceived injustice of defectors doing well, and are willing to “redress” that by imposing large costs, even when it severely lowers their own payoff. They do not systematically reward high contributors, either through their own actions (no positive transfer mechanism is available) or through selective leniency; full contributors sometimes coexist with their harsh punishment of defectors in the same periods, and they never direct sanctions toward those who appear to cooperate. When they themselves are punished, they do not reduce their contributions nor obviously shift to self‑protective behavior; instead, they maintain cooperation and, in some cases, intensify punishment of defectors afterward. This indicates they do not interpret incoming punishment as a signal to change their cooperative strategy, and they may view it as noise or misdirected aggression rather than meaningful feedback. Their reactions over time show limited adaptation to the apparent inefficacy of punishment in fully reforming defectors; they persist in norm enforcement even without clear evidence of improvement. Their underlying type is best summarized as a steadfast cooperative norm enforcer: they prioritize maintaining and defending a high‑contribution standard, tolerate being personally worse off, and use severe sanctions in response to others’ exploitative outcomes rather than adjusting their own contribution behavior.

text: They track and respond to patterns of who contributes and who is heavily sanctioned, rather than to single outcomes. When a clear, repeated free rider is also being hit hard by others’ punishment, they still add their own smaller share, suggesting they view cooperation plus group-enforced punishment as a stable institutional pattern: everyone should contribute; noncontributors should be sanctioned, possibly harshly, even at substantial efficiency loss. They do not soften their stance when the free rider’s payoff becomes very low or negative; they continue to back further sanctions, which implies a willingness to tolerate or support severe downside for chronic defectors. At the same time, they protect cooperative structure: they do not join antisocial attacks on high contributors, and they maintain their own contributions even when they themselves are punished, indicating they separate “being targeted” from “what the norm requires.” Their own payoff can be reduced by punishment, but they do not visibly react by changing behavior, showing low responsiveness to short-term payoff fluctuations and high adherence to a stable strategy. They appear to infer that contributors who keep giving are “on their side” and leave them untouched, focusing attention on persistent low givers regardless of the complex punishment environment around them.

text: The player observes others’ contributions and payoffs, but their behavioral adjustments indicate primarily self-centered interpretation rather than empathetic or norm-enforcing reactions. When others earn less as a result of contributing more, the player does not systematically move to “reward” them by increasing to similar contribution levels in subsequent decisions. Conversely, when the player’s own payoff is comparatively high due to free-riding, they do not react by toning down the exploitation in a sustained way. They do adjust behavior after experiencing particularly high or low own payoffs in relation to the group, but these adjustments are short-lived and not clearly tied to specific others’ outcomes. For example, after enjoying strong free-rider gains, they sometimes try contributing more—possibly inferring that the environment is safely cooperative and worth “joining”—yet will later revert to taking advantage of others’ generosity once again. After experiencing poor returns from high contributions, they are quick to revert back to low or zero contributions, indicating that others’ failure to match their generosity reduces their willingness to cooperate. There is no evidence of targeted responses to specific individuals’ patterns, since the game structure only allows global contribution choices and no punishment/reward actions are available. More broadly, others’ outcomes function mainly as signals of the current cooperativeness and profitability of the group environment rather than as objects of moral concern. The player infers when the group is generous enough to support high payoffs and opportunistically chooses between joining or exploiting that generosity, without showing a stable commitment to aligning their behavior with others’ welfare.

text: Because no explicit punishment or reward is available, their “response” to others’ outcomes is only visible through contribution changes. The player appears **highly responsive to the success of others**, but in a self-serving way. When others earn high payoffs through heavy contributions, the player tends to reduce their own contribution, exploiting the strong group provision while personally saving their endowment. When cooperation erodes and others’ payoffs fall, the player is often willing to increase their contribution, effectively helping to rebuild the group benefit, but only to a point that still yields good personal returns. There is no evidence that they aim to equalize payoffs: they often accept situations where they personally earn more than the heavy contributors. There is also no visible pattern of “moralistic” behavior (e.g., voluntarily sacrificing to lift underperformers or to match high contributors); instead, their adjustments are best interpreted as **instrumental reactions to the payoff environment**, not empathetic reactions to others’ welfare. In sum, they monitor others’ outcomes and contributions and then choose between cooperation and free-riding according to expected personal gain, not according to fairness or reciprocity ideals.

text: The player has full visibility of others’ outcomes, and their behavior suggests they attend to them, but inference about detailed beliefs is necessarily low-confidence. When others earn very high payoffs due to lower contributions, this player at times imitates the low-contribution approach in subsequent decisions, consistent with payoff-based learning. Conversely, when the group’s contributions (and thus per-capita redistributions) are strong, they are willing to reciprocate with high contributions, even after previously free-riding. They do not display strong, consistent negative reciprocity: they do not permanently shift into low contributions upon observing apparent free-riders; instead, they cycle back to high contributions repeatedly, even after having seen that low contributors gain higher private payoffs. This suggests that envy or punitive motives toward successful free-riders are not decisive. Rather, they appear to view others’ outcomes mainly as information about which contribution patterns are profitable and sustainable. There is no systematic pattern of persistently punishing or rewarding any specific profile (e.g., consistent high contributors vs. consistent low contributors) through their own contributions, given the absence of direct sanctioning tools. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes in a flexible, payoff-oriented way, sometimes aligning with cooperative peers and sometimes exploiting them, without locking into a purely retaliatory or purely solidaristic stance.

text: The player appears payoff- and pattern-sensitive rather than person-specific: they see detailed information on everyone’s contributions and payoffs, yet their own behavior shifts in response to overall payoff structures and apparent levels of cooperation rather than targeting particular individuals (no targeting is possible here, but there is no sign they react to specific identities either). When cooperative environments generate strong payoffs for them despite some free-riding by others, they often continue to contribute heavily, suggesting that they tolerate some inequality as long as the cooperative “pie” is large. However, when they experience that personally contributing less yields them distinctly higher payoffs while many others still contribute more, they respond by lowering their own contributions on subsequent decisions. This is a classic conditional cooperation-cum-learning response: they update toward more self-serving behavior once they see that the system rewards low contributors disproportionately. They do not have access to direct punishment or reward, so their only “response” channel to others’ outcomes is their own contribution choice. They effectively use that channel: they withdraw cooperation after learning that generous behavior repeatedly leaves them behind relative to free-riders, and they sometimes re-engage in high contributions once the group’s cooperation level and payoffs rebound. This cycling indicates dynamic adaptation to observed group-level efficiency and payoff distributions, not a fixed trait of pure cooperation or pure defection. Overall, the type is: a high-baseline, efficiency-minded contributor who conditionally adjusts based on observed returns to cooperation, willing both to support a cooperative norm for many rounds and to opportunistically free-ride—especially as the horizon approaches or when they have just experienced that under-contributing produces strictly superior payoffs. Their beliefs likely include: “Group cooperation can make everyone rich, but if others keep exploiting, I should sometimes exploit too.” Exact beliefs and motives beyond this are unknown or low-confidence.

text: The player observes others’ contributions and payoffs and appears to condition behavior on aggregate outcomes more than on any individual’s performance; there are no punishment or reward actions, so direct sanctioning responses are absent by design. Still, their contribution shifts track the overall payoff climate: when group outcomes are high due to strong cooperation, the player sometimes reduces their own contribution while retaining high payoffs; when group outcomes deteriorate, they tend to maintain or return to moderate contributions rather than fully abandoning the public good. They tolerate considerable heterogeneity in others’ payoffs. They repeatedly remain in situations where low contributors earn more than high contributors without clearly retaliatory defection. Likewise, when they themselves gain relative advantage by free‑riding, they do not subsequently compensate with unusually high giving. This pattern suggests low sensitivity to inequality per se; they are more focused on their own payoff relative to the public-good level than on enforcing fairness across players. There is no evidence that they “chase” the highest earner’s behavior in a simple payoff‑matching sense. They do not systematically copy the apparent top earners’ contribution levels, nor do they converge toward the minimum contributions observed. Instead, they repeatedly default to their own intermediate rule, occasionally deviating to exploit very cooperative environments. This indicates a certain independence in strategy selection: they attend to the overall cooperative climate but do not slavishly imitate others. In terms of inferred beliefs, they seem to expect that enough others will keep contributing to sustain a profitable public good, even after observing free‑riding and payoff asymmetries. Their continued moderate giving in the presence of clear defectors suggests some baseline belief that cooperation will persist or rebound without needing sanctions. Overall, the player’s reaction to others’ outcomes is pragmatic rather than norm-enforcing: they adjust in a way that preserves their own earnings while maintaining some group benefit, showing tolerance for both exploitation and inequality as long as the public good remains profitable.

text: The player’s reactions to others’ outcomes are inferred from how they adjust contributions across rounds in relation to group behavior and payoffs, since there is no direct communication or sanctioning. They are clearly attentive to aggregate outcomes. After experiencing situations where others’ high contributions yield them especially high payoffs despite their own low input, they become willing to repeat or intensify low-contribution strategies, implying they notice and respond to the profitability of free‑riding on cooperators. This shows payoff‑sensitive learning rather than blind repetition. At the same time, when group contributions and shared payoffs appear to drop, they respond by raising their own contributions above their minimal level. This indicates that they value the public good enough to invest more when its provision seems threatened, even though they could remain strictly selfish. Their adjustments suggest a belief that their own contribution marginally helps maintain group benefits, and they are willing to shoulder some of that cost if the alternative looks like a poorer environment for everyone, including themselves. They do not show evidence of targeting particular individuals based on their payoffs; the game design only allows them to see others’ outcomes without any individualized response channel. Their adaptation is therefore aggregate rather than interpersonal: they respond to the overall pattern of contributions and payoffs, not to specific players’ fortunes. In personality terms, they appear pragmatic and opportunistic rather than strongly altruistic or strongly punitive. They are comfortable benefitting disproportionately when others are generous, but they also invest enough to keep the cooperative structure from collapsing. Their “social preference” profile is consistent with a self‑interested conditional cooperator: they respond to group outcomes in a way that aims to balance personal profit with the maintenance of a sufficiently cooperative environment.

text: The player appears attentive to others’ outcomes and uses them as information about what strategies are profitable. After instances where low contributors obtain higher payoffs than high contributors, the player later adopts more self-protective or exploitative contribution levels. Conversely, when cooperation is widespread and yields strong returns, they often move back toward high contributions. This indicates that they infer incentives from observed payoff ranks and adjust rather than ignoring outcome information. There is no punishment or reward mechanism in this game, so their reactions are purely through own-contribution choices. As such, we cannot infer any direct willingness to incur costs to sanction or reward others; that dimension remains unknown. Socially, their pattern suggests a pragmatic, somewhat opportunistic stance rather than rigid moralism. They do not consistently sacrifice to protect high cooperators nor to chase top earnings at all costs; instead, they aim for strong but not necessarily maximal relative payoffs. They tolerate the presence of both strong free-riders and strong cooperators and adjust their own strategy accordingly. Whether they emotionally resent free-riders or admire cooperators is unknown; all that is observable is an adaptive, outcome-sensitive adjustment process rather than a purely principled or purely exploitative style.

text: The player closely monitors others’ outcomes and adapts based on relative payoffs rather than absolute levels of the public good. When they can obtain higher or comparable payoffs while contributing less than most others, they readily choose low or zero contributions. When free-riding leaves them clearly behind high earners or when group cooperation shrinks enough that collective returns fall, they sometimes increase their own contribution to re-enter a more profitable cooperative regime. They treat others’ high outcomes as both a resource and a signal. When many peers earn strong payoffs by contributing generously, the player sometimes free-rides to capture the externality, and at other times matches the cooperative pattern enough to keep group returns high. This suggests a belief that there will usually be enough cooperators to sustain a beneficial public good level and that occasional defection will not collapse the system immediately. Conversely, when others’ payoffs deteriorate alongside declines in total contribution, the player appears to infer that pure defection may no longer be as profitable. In response, they sometimes increase their own contributions, seemingly to help restore a payoff-relevant level of cooperation, not to equalize payoffs across individuals. Because no punishment or reward is available, we cannot observe their willingness to sanction or compensate others. Their pattern of repeatedly exploiting cooperative peers and not maintaining consistently high contributions after observing others earn less strongly suggests low intrinsic concern for others’ disadvantage. They show no behavioral sign of voluntarily sacrificing payoff to protect or reward more cooperative or lower-earning players. However, they are not purely spiteful or anti-social; they are willing to contribute substantially when it serves their own expected earnings within the group context. Overall, the player responds to others’ outcomes in a calculative, payoff-maximizing way: others’ contributions and earnings are informational inputs into their own best-response strategy, not moral targets for correction or support.

text: The player observes that low contributors often earn more than high contributors in many periods, including instances where free riders substantially out-earn them. Despite this, they often continue to contribute highly. This indicates that immediate relative payoff disadvantage does not automatically trigger defection. They tolerate being out-earned when the group average contribution remains reasonably high, possibly valuing the maintenance of collective efficiency or disliking the idea of unilaterally undermining the group. However, when exploitation of cooperators becomes salient—or when group contributions drop and payoffs for high contributors look poor—they do sometimes cut back contributions, including down to very low levels. text: The player pays attention to how others are treated and adjusts in response. When they themselves are punished despite strong cooperation, they briefly adopt a non-contributory stance, indicating that unfair negative treatment undermines their willingness to contribute and may trigger experimental or defiant behavior. However, they do not mirror the punitive aggression of others; instead, they soon revert to high contributions and, at times, offer small rewards to those who are being punished heavily. This pattern suggests that seeing others incur extreme punishment for low contributions elicits a mild corrective reaction—rewarding them a bit—rather than joining the punitive majority. They do not appear to condition their own contributions strongly on specific individuals’ payoffs or histories; instead, they respond at a coarse level to the overall climate of punishment and cooperation. Their behavior is consistent with a type who values cooperative efficiency but is easily alienated by what they perceive as unfair targeting, and who sometimes uses rewards to counteract or soften disproportionate sanctions imposed by others.

text: They appear highly attentive to others’ contribution decisions and to the pattern of enforcement in the group, but less sensitive to detailed payoff outcomes. Their long-run punishment of one low contributor is consistent with tracking that player’s repeated deviations from the emergent norm, not with tracking who is richest or poorest. Their consistent reward of a particular cooperator/enforcer aligns with noticing that player bearing repeated enforcement costs. They do not adjust their own contribution in response to being punished once or in response to how much they themselves are being rewarded; their cooperation and enforcement choices seem largely independent of how much they personally gain or lose. There is no observable reaction to other players’ heavy punishment expenditures or to dramatic payoff swings; they neither imitate that intensity nor try to counter-punish. Overall, they respond to others’ actions (contributions, enforcement behavior) rather than to others’ final payoffs per se, and they maintain their own cooperative standard regardless of how the payoff landscape evolves.

text: The player pays attention to others’ contributions and outcomes and modifies their behavior in a nuanced but not aggressive way. When others spend heavily on punishment and create substantial payoff variation, the player does not react by escalating sanctions; instead, they typically maintain or revert to positive tools (reward) or remain neutral. They do not appear to target those who are themselves frequent punishers or heavy rewarders, nor do they visibly protect victims of severe punishment through compensatory rewards; their reward choices track contribution levels more than net payoff outcomes. They shift their own contribution level somewhat when aggregate cooperation drops, suggesting that group-level outcomes inform their personal cost–benefit balance, but they never pivot to pure self-interest. Overall, they respond to the social environment primarily by adjusting generosity and rewards rather than by outcome-based retaliation, positioning them as a stable, cooperative type who absorbs noise and aggression rather than amplifying it.

text: They observe others’ outcomes, including how much players spend on punishment and reward and who ends up with high or low payoffs, and they adjust behavior over time. The changes in their contributions and intermittent punishment choices roughly follow shifts in the group’s sanctioning climate: when other players intensify punishment or show strong norm-enforcement, this player experiments both with stronger cooperation and with occasional defection, and they briefly join in punishing some targets before withdrawing from active sanctioning again. They do not visibly respond via chat to being punished or rewarded, and there is no sign of reactive over-punishment or gratitude-based reward. Their main behavioral adjustments in response to others’ outcomes are shifts in their own contribution level and rare changes in whom they punish. Overall, they appear to process outcome information in a pragmatic, non-verbal way: they update their sense of which behaviors are safe or acceptable but do not overtly signal their interpretations. Their reaction profile fits a somewhat introspective, outcome-sensitive type who experiments within the available strategy space rather than a vocal leader or a purely myopic individualist.

text: They observe a highly punitive environment in which several players spend heavily on punishment, including anti‑social punishment against high contributors, and where some players accumulate large payoff swings. Their behavior indicates that they register these outcomes but respond only through their own contribution levels and selective rewards, not through counter‑punishment or public commentary. When they are themselves punished despite cooperative behavior, they do not escalate conflict. They neither retaliate nor reduce contributions in a simple tit‑for‑tat manner; instead, they adjust contributions and reward patterns in a more complex way, sometimes maintaining or restoring high cooperation after being targeted. They do not show obvious favoritism toward those who punish a lot or against those who are heavily punished; their rewards are not clearly aligned with the group’s punishment patterns. This suggests that they treat others’ punishment behavior as noisy or not worth reinforcing. In summary, they are robust to unfair outcomes and maintain a constructive orientation. They track who is distinctive in their behavior and sometimes reinforce them with rewards, but they refrain from amplifying the punitive dynamics around them.

text: The player experiences both rewards and punishments from others across the game, yet their own choices remain unchanged: they continue to contribute fully and never engage in sanctioning. They do not react to being rewarded by increasing cooperation (it is already maxed) or by reciprocating rewards; they also do not respond to being punished by lowering contributions or starting to punish back. Likewise, they ignore substantial sanction expenditures by others—both heavy punishers and heavy rewarders—showing no observable attempt to ally with, imitate, or counterbalance them. In effect, others’ payoff outcomes, including extreme cases of being heavily fined or generously rewarded, do not appear to alter this player’s strategy. Their learning or adaptation is minimal to nonexistent at the observable level: they seem committed to a fixed rule of behavior and treat the evolving payoff landscape more as background noise than as input for adjusting actions. This creates a profile of a steadfast, internally motivated cooperator who is largely insensitive to external incentives, reputational dynamics, and payoff feedback.

text: They pay attention to others’ contributions and to the intensity of punishment and reward in the group, and their strategy evolves in response. When others maintain very high contribution levels despite heavy punishment exchanges, they respond by holding or returning to moderate contributions and later by opportunistic defection, indicating they infer that the public good is robust to their own under-contribution. Observing extensive punishment against some low or inconsistent contributors seems to trigger two distinct reactions at different times: transient protective or compensatory rewards toward punished targets, and later a strong punitive action toward multiple low contributors when they judge free-riding to be more problematic. They appear largely indifferent to others’ exact payoffs and do not respond sympathetically to players who lose a lot through punishment, except for that brief early reward episode. Overall, they interpret the observed outcome environment as one where others are willing to sustain cooperation and do substantial norm enforcement; they exploit that stability at times, then selectively join in enforcement when they deem it necessary to curb what they see as excessive deviation.

text: The player pays close attention to others’ contribution levels, punishment costs, and deduction outcomes. They notice who contributes less, who gets punished, and who punishes whom. When a previously low contributor increases contributions, they respond with explicit positive reinforcement and thanks, suggesting a supportive orientation when others move toward the cooperative norm. They also quickly challenge implausible excuses about having “no coins,” referring to the visible payoff data and urging the player to “try harder,” indicating that they use outcome information to assess honesty. They are vocal about egregious misbehavior, criticizing the chronically low contributor and, later, their antisocial punishment of a high contributor. However, they remain behavior-focused rather than personally abusive; their comments, while morally charged (“bad rabbit”), are framed in terms of deservedness and fairness. They do not express envy toward higher-earning low contributors nor gloat over punished players; instead, they show a kind of moral bookkeeping where outcomes serve as evidence for who should be encouraged, who should be condemned, and when harsh punishment becomes warranted.

text: They appear behaviorally indifferent, at least in their own decisions, to others’ payoff swings, punishment costs, and being heavily deducted. Their implicit stance is to decouple their own cooperative behavior from others’ gains or losses once the broad cooperative norm is in place. They notice enough to align with the declared “everyone contributes fully” pattern and to state their own behavior once, but they do not visibly let others’ payoffs or sanctioning choices influence their own strategy. Overall, they fit a “quiet high cooperator who abstains from punishment and largely ignores outcome drama” type.

text: They observe a wide range of outcomes: high cooperative payoffs, extreme punishment expenditures by some, and large income losses for a deviant player. They respond once in chat to group calls to punish a low contributor, indicating that others’ observable low effort plus coordinated punitive intent can move their behavior. Conversely, when another player receives very large cumulative punishment, they do not add to the pile, suggesting they avoid overkill and perhaps consider further sanctions as redundant. Their lack of visible response to antisocial punishment against a high contributor implies either detachment from justice concerns beyond clear free riding, or a preference not to escalate social conflict. They appear more attuned to contribution levels and announced intentions than to detailed payoff numbers per se.

They also acknowledge and explain their own past punishment decisions to the group, signaling transparency and a desire to maintain group understanding of why outcomes look the way they do. text: The player gives no explicit verbal reaction to payoff distributions, free-riding, or the presence of mixed contribution levels. Behaviorally, they show zero retaliation or adaptation in response to others’ outcomes: even when low contributors and clear free-riders earn higher payoffs, this player does not reduce their own contributions. They effectively tolerate exploitation without protest or adjustment. This suggests one of the following: (a) they prioritize social efficiency and group gains over relative payoffs; (b) they follow a simple unconditional rule and ignore outcome feedback; or (c) they notice the inequity but choose not to react (emotional state unknown). Because they never use chat, there is no evidence of resentment, moralizing, or attempts to shame defectors; their “response” is to continue full cooperation regardless of who benefits more or less.

text: They attend to others’ behavior, especially persistent low contributors, and interpret this in payoff terms. They do not display envy or strong resentment about others earning more in any given round, and they never threaten or shame others. They show no desire to track or punish particular individuals, and there is no sign of competitive drive to “win” the most coins; their choices seem more about balancing decent earnings with being part of the cooperative majority. text: The player has full visibility of others’ contributions and payoffs but shows little overt emotional reactivity to them. Even when specific players repeatedly give zero or very low amounts and consequently earn higher payoffs in some rounds, the player does not complain, shame, or attempt to correct them through argument. Their only explicit interpretation of others’ behavior concerns who is “real” versus “fake,” based on chat engagement rather than payoff patterns. They do not comment on fairness of payoffs or relative earnings, and they never suggest changing their own behavior in response to observed free-riding. This indicates a relatively low emotional sensitivity to outcome inequalities and a focus on maintaining their own cooperation strategy regardless of others’ gains. With no punishment or reward tools available, they also make no attempt to substitute social sanctioning for material incentives; they simply continue contributing and keep the conversation light. Their stable high cooperation in the face of visible free-riding and unequal payoffs suggests that outcome differences do not strongly undermine their willingness to contribute.

text: They see others’ payoffs and contribution patterns but do not react strongly to inequality or exploitation. Instead, they align verbally with those advocating trust and higher contributions, while treating the presence of low contributors almost as a background fact of the game. They seem to accept that a few players will earn extra at the group’s expense and still continue contributing generously, implying a relatively high tolerance for being modestly disadvantaged as long as the group overall does well and the social atmosphere remains pleasant. They do not explicitly comment on others’ payoffs in detail, nor do they differentiate their behavior based on individual others’ exact earnings. Their behavioral changes (when they reduce contribution) do not correlate clearly to any explicit stated resentment; the transcript supports only that they are aware of free-riding but not that they are emotionally reactive to it. text: They observe everyone’s contributions and payoffs but never comment on them. They repeatedly experience situations where more cooperative players receive lower payoffs than themselves, yet they do not voice concern, guilt, or praise. This silence, combined with their persistent under-contribution, implies low behavioral concern for distributive fairness or for aligning with expressed group norms, at least within this anonymous, low-stakes setting. They witness strong altruism from many others and also blatant free-riding from at least one consistently low contributor. Unlike the actively cooperative players who discuss strategy, trust, and mutual benefit, this player remains entirely quiet, neither condemning nor supporting either side. Their behavior suggests that others’ outcomes primarily function as information to exploit (i.e., reassurance that high group cooperation continues) rather than as moral or emotional signals that would alter their strategy. In summary, when others earn less as a result of being more generous, this player continues their pattern without adjustment toward fairness; when others keep earning well despite high contributions, they feel safe to reduce their own contribution further. Any emotional reaction to others’ payoffs is unknown; behaviorally, they act as a detached opportunist who monitors but does not socially engage with outcomes.

text: They notice that certain players systematically keep more coins and thereby earn more each round. They explicitly remark that these players gain extra coins relative to the more cooperative group. Yet they downplay its importance, explicitly characterizing the extra earnings as not a big issue. This indicates low envy and relatively high tolerance for moderate inequality, as long as overall payoffs remain high for everyone. They do not attempt to punish or ostracize freeriders via harsh language. Instead, they frame possible hidden bonuses as an explanation for these behaviors and then dismiss the relative advantage as minor. Their cooperative behavior persists despite visible differences in payoffs. They do not adjust contributions in a sharply tit-for-tat manner in response to specific others’ outcomes. When others defect or partially defect, they sometimes lower their own contributions, but not in a direct retaliatory way, and they also return to full cooperation afterward. This suggests that they track group-level behavior and incentives at a coarse level rather than closely conditioning on particular individuals’ outcomes. They express an expectation that everyone will end up with large totals, and they appear satisfied with that, even if some individuals (the consistent freeriders) end with more. Their orientation is “as long as we all do fine, relative differences are acceptable,” rather than strict equality or strong reciprocity.

text: They see others’ payoffs and the obvious advantage enjoyed by persistent low contributors, but there is no evidence that they react with anger, calls for punishment, or explicit resentment. They accept, at least behaviorally, that some individuals will systematically free-ride, but they do not let this fully derail their own willingness to return to high contributions when group conditions feel favorable. text: They clearly observe that others’ payoffs are somewhat lower than theirs when they free‑ride, and that many others are contributing much more while earning slightly less each round. Despite this, they show no explicit emotional or verbal reaction to these outcome differences and no attempt to mitigate them through higher later contributions. Even when other players explicitly highlight that certain avatars (including this player) consistently keep all their coins, the focal player neither denies nor justifies the behavior, and does not compensate by increasing contributions afterward in any sustained way. They are mentioned by others as gaining more per round and as a source of mild frustration, but they do not engage with those comments. This suggests low expressed concern about relative fairness or about being perceived as unfair in the absence of direct penalties. Their behavior indicates that the visibility of others’ contributions and payoffs is not sufficient to trigger guilt, shame, or a desire to rebalance outcomes, at least not in a way that materially affects their choices. Because there is no punishment or reward mechanism, we cannot observe how they would react to explicit cost‑imposing actions by peers; their response to others’ outcomes is limited to silent observation and continued self‑interested play. text: The player observes others’ outcomes and contributions but does not verbally react to specific payoffs. They do not complain about being exploited when they contribute fully in cooperative rounds, and they do not express guilt or moral concern when they free-ride on high-contributing others. Their chat never references precise earnings, nor do they compare their payoff to others’. Their behavioral responses, however, indicate that they are tracking group behavior at a coarse level. They free-ride in contexts where others are contributing a lot, implying that they recognize those as profitable exploitation opportunities. They also match cooperative norms in rounds where chat strongly emphasizes “everyone contribute fully,” suggesting that they internalize group cues about what others are likely to do, even if they do not discuss payoffs directly. When another player speculates about who might be “not real,” the focal player echoes this meta-suspicion rather than shifting contributions reactively toward or against specific players. They do not appear to adjust behavior in a targeted way toward identified low contributors; instead, their shifts are broad (cooperate vs. defect at the group level) instead of individually directed. The emphasis in their comments on the eventual bonus and time cost, combined with the absence of moral language about fairness or exploitation, suggests that they evaluate outcomes primarily in aggregate (total earnings vs. time spent) rather than as relational fairness outcomes between self and others. In summary, the player is attentive enough to others’ behavior to exploit or align with group-level patterns, but they remain affectively neutral and non-confrontational about payoff differences. Their responses to others’ outcomes are implicit and strategic rather than explicit or emotionally charged.

text: The player sees that certain others repeatedly earn higher payoffs by contributing little or nothing, and they explicitly acknowledge this pattern with a humorous remark about “role play” and a later plea to habitual low contributors framed as a narrative appeal rather than a moral accusation. text: This player observes others spending substantial amounts on reward and earning very diverse payoffs, yet their own behavior shows no reactive use of the mechanism and only a coarse, payoff-driven adjustment in contributions. They do not imitate generous rewarders, do not retaliate against or compensate for skewed reward distributions, and do not appear to systematically favor or avoid any subgroup of players based on how those players are treated by others. Despite receiving rewards in multiple instances, they do not change by adding reciprocal rewards, and their contribution pattern remains an alternation between very low and very high levels rather than a move toward stable cooperation. This suggests that the main “signal” they extract from others’ outcomes is that high contributions can coexist with high personal payoffs due to strong group cooperation, encouraging them to continue toggling between exploiting that cooperation and sometimes joining it. Their social orientation is instrumental and relatively detached: they attend to the aggregate benefits created by others’ contributions but remain largely indifferent to the fairness of reward flows or to the sacrifices others make to incentivize cooperation.

text: This player appears responsive to the environment, but in a relatively restrained and payoff-conscious way. They repeatedly witness some players spending very large amounts on rewards, sometimes driving their own payoffs extremely low, and others contributing little or nothing while still earning decent payoffs. Despite this, they do not copy the extreme rewarding strategy or retaliate against low contributors (punishment is unavailable). Instead, they continue to mix high contributions with moderate or zero reward spending, suggesting that they interpret extreme rewarders as outliers rather than as a norm to follow. When many others contribute generously and overall outcomes for cooperators are strong, this player tends to maintain or raise their own contributions and may sometimes accompany that with some reward spending. This indicates a belief that cooperation can be sustained and is worth supporting, but they still guard against over-investing in rewards. When they see noisy or uneven outcomes—such as free-riders doing well, high contributors sometimes not being especially favored, or reward-spenders suffering low or negative payoffs—they adjust by reducing their own contribution or reducing reward use, rather than exiting cooperation entirely. This points to a learning pattern where they update cautiously: they remain cooperative but avoid strategies that lead to extreme payoff volatility. They do not visibly chase the highest individual payoff patterns (e.g., pure free-riding with no rewards), suggesting they are not purely payoff-maximizing in a narrow sense. Nor do they emulate the most “moralistic” or self-sacrificing cooperators. Their persona is that of a balanced, norm-aware participant: supportive of cooperation, mildly reinforcing of others’ good behavior, but consistently attentive to their own earnings and unwilling to incur large costs solely to shape others’ outcomes.

text: The player pays attention to others’ contributions and reward expenditures and adapts rather than reacting emotionally or punitively. When some others spend extreme amounts on reward and take large payoff losses, the player does not imitate this behavior; they continue to spend little on reward. This implies that they recognize generous over-rewarding as costly and do not internalize it as a norm to follow. They also do not appear to react directly to any single peer’s payoff or cost pattern. For instance, when some peers occasionally earn very high or very low payoffs due to their reward behavior, the player does not sharply adjust in the next round to “help” or “exploit” those specific outcomes. text: They pay close attention to others’ outcomes and integrate this into their social and strategic views. When others are heavily punished (e.g., frog, cow), they sympathize and respond constructively rather than ignoring it. They compliment or help those players via reward or by encouraging them to retaliate and giving them “a little boost.” This shows sensitivity to others’ disadvantage and a willingness to redistribute from themselves to restore perceived fairness. They accept being rewarded without gloating; when thanked by another player, they respond briefly but positively, indicating they interpret reward as a social bond rather than a license to free-ride. They do not appear envious of other cooperators doing marginally better; they share their own cumulative coins transparently and engage lightly in that comparison without changing behavior. They also update beliefs based on observed outcomes: they join the group’s emerging belief that the bird is likely a bot or researcher, react to how many coins the bird accumulates, and adjust from passivity to active punishment once the extent of the bird’s advantage is salient and the group shifts to “destroy the bird” coordination. However, these belief updates do not make them reduce their public contributions; they remain steadfastly cooperative even when perceiving the game as somewhat unfair or exploitative. They prioritize fairness and group cohesion over narrow self-interest, and they adapt their use of punishment and reward in response to others’ outcomes while keeping their own contribution level constant.

text: They are repeatedly punished when they contribute substantially less than the norm, and later are targeted again after group members explicitly recall that “owl did well” while not contributing fully. Despite this, they never respond in chat and never punish back. Their observable adjustments are limited to changing contribution levels in later rounds. Early on, after a single under-contribution that attracts heavy punishment, they quickly revert to maximum contributions for a long stretch, which keeps them out of the crosshairs. Later, once the group is more distracted by the parrot and by pay discussions, they again contribute much less than others, get punished, and then revert to higher contributions in subsequent rounds. This looks like risk-managed opportunism: test the limits, accept some sanctions as a cost of skimming, and then blend back into the cooperative majority. They clearly notice that punishment is anonymous and that many others are willing to spend a lot on enforcement, but they never join in; instead, they use this structure to their advantage by free-riding both on contributions and on punishment. They show no evidence of caring about fairness in outcomes beyond their own payoff, and they tolerate being disliked or labeled “sus” as long as their own earnings remain comparatively high. In summary, this player in new PGG environments behaves as a largely cooperative, silent optimizer who: - usually contributes at or near the norm to secure group benefits, - occasionally under-contributes to privately skim when it seems safe, - completely abstains from all forms of costly social enforcement, - accepts being punished and socially distrusted without retaliation or explanation, - and treats others’ outcomes and narratives as background conditions rather than moral concerns.

Overall, this player type in new PGGs is best characterized as: silent, strongly but not rigidly cooperative; highly averse to spending on punishment or reward; tolerant of outcome inequality; and willing to opportunistically free-ride when reputational and punitive risks appear minimal.

text: They consistently notice and mentally track who is being deducted and rewarded. They identify themselves as a punishment target as soon as others ask, and later report that the parrot has hit another player. They also note disappearing chat messages and engage in the meta-conversation about possible bots and researchers, so they clearly monitor both outcomes and process. Despite this awareness, their behavioral response is extremely muted: they do not change their contribution level, do not start punishing, and do not change who they support. When they themselves are deducted, they respond with a light, reflective comment rather than anger or switching to defection. When another high contributor complains about being unfairly hit, they empathize briefly (“same man”) but again change nothing about their own play. They occasionally voice structural concerns—asking when the game ends, acknowledging the low hourly pay, and noticing censorship-like effects—yet they keep following the contribution norm fully until the very end. This suggests they weigh fairness and personal payoff, but once committed to the cooperative rule they do not let negative shocks or doubts shift their behavior. They also do not appear to be especially status- or rank-focused. Others share their accumulated coin totals; this player shares theirs once (when asked implicitly by the social context around totals) and then moves on. They do not attempt to catch up, exploit, or “punish” those doing unusually well. Altogether, they respond cognitively to others’ outcomes—tracking, interpreting, sometimes commenting—but not behaviorally in terms of strategy changes. They are a stable, norm-compliant cooperator who watches others’ justice dramas from the sidelines and keeps playing “their game” regardless.

text: They pay close attention to others’ payoffs and to how punishment and reward flows shape those outcomes. This fuels their willingness to support renewed punishment after a lull. At the same time, they show sympathy for fellow participants who are harmed or targeted unfairly, and they explicitly point out when game pay and bonus conversion rates seem low, indicating they map in-game coins to real money and care about distributive fairness beyond simple in-game scores. They appear to update their beliefs about the deviant from “maybe researcher/bot” to “high earner exploiting a glitch or mechanism” and adjust their punishment stance accordingly. When others begin to feel punished or exploited by the platform (e.g., comments about underpayment, long duration), the player voices agreement but does not pivot to self-serving behavior; instead they maintain full contributions and only modest, targeted punishment. Overall, their responses to others’ outcomes show they are fairness-oriented, attentive to relative payoffs, and willing to adapt enforcement behavior when outcome patterns reveal persistent exploitation, while still preserving a cooperative baseline.

text: This player observes others’ contributions, payoffs, and punishment patterns and adjusts their behavior modestly rather than dramatically. They do not appear to condition their actions strongly on who gains or loses from punishment, nor do they attempt to exploit those who have been heavily sanctioned. There is no evidence that they target perceived “winners” or “losers,” likely because they never punish at all. They also do not seem to sharply withdraw cooperation after observing harsh or possibly antisocial punishment patterns from others, suggesting a level of resilience: the presence of punitive behavior in the group does not push them into full defection. Their adjustments in contribution suggest that they are aware of the cooperative climate—responding to whether others appear more or less aligned—but they react smoothly rather than with abrupt shifts. They likely infer that some group members are enforcing or disrupting norms, but they continue to focus on a middle-ground contribution strategy rather than actively reshaping outcomes through punishment or extreme cooperation. text: They appear insensitive, at least behaviorally, to variations in others’ payoffs, contribution choices, or reward expenditures. text: They observe a wide range of others’ outcomes: some players contribute high and also spend on rewards, others contribute low and receive no social sanction, and some earn very high payoffs through minimal contributions plus received rewards or through saving both contributions and reward costs. Specifically, as they see persistent patterns where certain players free-ride or under-contribute yet still earn strong payoffs (often because others reward liberally), they reduce their own cooperative level but do not completely withdraw. This suggests they are sensitive to the fairness of the outcome distribution and are unwilling to indefinitely subsidize what looks like an inefficient or inequitable system. However, they stop short of punitive behavior: they do not use rewards to counteract inequality (e.g., by selectively boosting only generous players), nor do they have access to punishment, so they simply adjust their own provision rate. They also do not chase the highest possible payoff by mimicking the most selfish strategies. When others drop to very low contributions and still do well, the player responds only partially; they preserve some level of contribution that keeps them aligned with a minimum cooperation standard. text: They pay attention to others’ contributions, reward spending, and payoffs, but they do not appear to respond strongly to group-level outcomes. Conversely, when they themselves end with lower payoffs in contribution-heavy, reward-heavy rounds, they do not appear to shift toward consistent defection only; they continue to use a mixed approach. text: They pay evident attention to others’ contribution levels, payoff outcomes, and reward expenditures, and they adapt their own behavior accordingly. As they observe some players persistently contributing very little yet earning high payoffs, and others spending heavily on reward in ways that may look excessive or inefficient, they increasingly shift toward lower contributions and reduced or zero reward spending, while enjoying higher round payoffs themselves. They do not appear motivated by spite or inequality aversion in a punitive sense: they never use reward in a way that directly reduces others’ payoffs, and they tolerate persistent high earners who contribute very little without trying to offset that via negative actions (which are mechanically unavailable) or by targeted withholding of reward toward those specific individuals (they simply scale down reward overall). Their reaction to others’ high payoffs when they are free-riding is to join the more self-interested strategy rather than to sacrifice income to discipline them indirectly. At the same time, they remain responsive to cooperative signals: when many peers return to higher contributions and reward patterns after a low-cooperation spell, the focal player sometimes raises their own contributions again. This reflects a learning, adaptive style focused on tracking the net cooperative climate: they upshift both cooperation and reward when the group looks cohesive and the time horizon is still meaningful, and downshift when they infer that others’ outcomes and behaviors signal instability, exploitation, or imminent end-game defection. Overall, this player type is a “conditional altruistic strategist”: initially generous and willing to pay to support cooperation and reciprocate reward, but increasingly self-protective and opportunistic as group behavior becomes noisier and as the end of the game approaches.

text: This player pays evident attention to others’ contributions, rewards, and payoffs, and updates their behavior in coarse but meaningful ways. When the group’s cooperative performance is relatively strong and there are clear high contributors, they tend to remain in their cooperative, all-in contribution mode and actively reward those others. They do not appear deterred simply because some individuals earn higher payoffs than they do; as long as those players are contributing and/or rewarding, they remain willing to support them. The main behavioral shift triggered by others’ outcomes seems to come when there is a combination of substantial free-riding and relatively low or uneven use of rewards toward high cooperators. In such environments, they sometimes switch to zero contribution and suspend reward giving. This indicates sensitivity to perceived unfairness or lack of mutual support: when they see that contributing and rewarding does not consistently produce a fair or mutually reinforcing structure, they retreat to a self-protective stance. They do not ever use negative instruments (punishment is absent here, but they also do not use “withholding reward from high cooperators” as a clear retributive signal; instead, they usually continue to reward high contributors until they globally withdraw). Their response to problematic others’ behavior is more of a global disengagement than targeted retaliation: rather than isolating specific defectors, they occasionally abandon cooperative efforts altogether. They also do not chase high-payoff defectors by attempting to match their exploitative strategy permanently. Their defections are episodic rather than fully imitative of low contributors. This suggests a preference structure where fairness and norm-conformity matter, but they are not fixated on eliminating every payoff disadvantage. They accept some inequity so long as the overall environment feels broadly cooperative and appreciative. Overall, their reaction to others’ outcomes is best described as: “norm-sensitive coarse adjuster.” They sustain cooperation and reward as long as a recognizably cooperative cluster exists and is visibly supported; when that support appears too weak or inconsistent, they temporarily prioritize earnings by suspending both contribution and reward.

text: They track others’ contributions and outcomes and respond in ways that prioritize supporting high contributors and visible cooperators. text: They consistently see that others are spending heavily on rewards, sometimes at substantial personal cost, and that low contributors often earn high payoffs relative to them. text: The player closely tracks that others sometimes incur substantial costs by contributing while the player benefits disproportionately. Instead, they maintain non-contribution, indicating that equality per se does not motivate them toward greater provision of the public good. Their behavior suggests that others’ outcomes matter primarily as an input to understanding what they can profitably free ride on, not as a target to equalize or as a basis for building mutual cooperation. text: This player **pays attention to others’ outcomes and contributions and adapts in a flexible, self-serving but exploratory way**. When they see that at least one peer repeatedly contributes regardless of others, they sometimes switch to pure free-riding, capitalizing on that person’s cooperative behavior. At other times, after witnessing that others are not contributing and that they themselves have been earning comparatively less due to cooperating, they still return to contributing in later rounds. This suggests they are not driven purely by envy or negative reciprocity; they do not rigidly punish low contributors through permanent defection. Instead, their response is **probabilistic and experimental**: they occasionally re-offer cooperation even after being exploited, perhaps testing whether group norms can shift. They also accept synchronized full defection patterns when all players withhold contributions and enjoy the equal, risk-free payoff in those moments, showing they are comfortable with a low-cooperation, low-variance equilibrium when that appears to be the group’s default. There is no evidence of strong emotional reactivity such as persistent retaliation, moralistic punishment, or strict norm enforcement, especially since no explicit punishment or reward mechanisms exist here. Behavior suggests a **cool, instrumental stance** toward others’ outcomes: they observe who contributes, who doesn’t, and who gets what, and then adjust in ways that sometimes exploit, sometimes probe for cooperation, without locking into a stable fairness-driven rule. Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is **adaptive, opportunistic, and only weakly guided by fairness or reciprocity norms**.

Because no explicit punishment or reward tools are available, their only observable “response” channel is their own contribution. Within that constraint, their behavior shows mixed motives: they sometimes reinforce cooperative environments by contributing despite personal cost, and sometimes reinforce non-cooperative environments by matching others’ free-riding, shifting flexibly between the two rather than systematically tracking or correcting payoff imbalances.

They value cooperation but are not willing to bear additional second-order costs to police it. They also do not react when some players earn more by free-riding or by being rewarded; there is no visible resentment, complaint, or attempt to “correct” outcomes through changing contributions or using rewards. Their only explicit reaction to others’ behavior is to support the idea that everyone maximizing contributions increases group returns. Beyond that, they treat others’ payoffs and reward costs as background information, not as inputs to conditional strategies. Emotionally, they appear even-keeled and not easily provoked by inequality or short-term inefficiencies; they stay on their chosen cooperative path regardless of how outcomes are distributed.

text: They observe that others sometimes spend substantial amounts on rewards, but they neither imitate this nor comment on it. They do not react negatively to under-contributors who profit in some rounds, nor do they express resentment or attempts to shame them in chat. When the group converges to full cooperation and everyone’s payoffs peak, they respond with short positive messages that acknowledge joint success, indicating satisfaction with collectively high outcomes rather than concern about minor distributional details. Their silence regarding others who incur personal costs to reward suggests that they treat such behavior as optional and non-normative rather than as something to praise or reciprocate. Overall, they appear outcome-focused at the group level (high total and equal payoffs) and relatively insensitive, at least verbally and behaviorally, to the finer-grained differences created by the reward stage.

text: They pay attention to others’ outcomes indirectly via noticing contribution patterns and inferring confusion or trolling. At first, seeing lower contributions, they question others’ rationality or authenticity (calling them bots or not smart). Once it becomes apparent that others were misinterpreting the interface, they shift to an instructive stance, explaining that the default setting already corresponds to full contribution and that changing it harms everyone. They do not react materially to others’ use of rewards—either to those who spend heavily to reward others or to those who do not. Their own behavior remains stable: no retaliatory reduction in contributions toward under-contributors, no reciprocal rewarding of reward-givers, and no attempts to sanction. Their primary response channel to others’ payoff-relevant behavior is communicative, not strategic adjustment. Overall, they interpret outcome discrepancies as coordination or understanding problems rather than as intentional exploitation, and once those are resolved, they are satisfied with the uniform high-payoff equilibrium.

text: The player clearly observes others’ outcomes and contributions (their own behavior changes early on in ways that only make sense if they process that information), but they do not react through chat, contribution cuts, or rewards in a targeted way. When others contribute less, the player sometimes ends up with a notably lower payoff than low contributors, yet they largely maintain high contributions rather than retaliating or trying to discipline defectors via rewards. The brief early instance of free-riding occurs even while others are advocating full contribution and then following through; this is more consistent with a short exploratory test of the payoff structure than with a response to unfair treatment. After that, they continue to cooperate even when a minority free-rides, suggesting they place more weight on the majority behavior and expected group benefit than on strict payoff fairness relative to each individual. They do not acknowledge or mirror others’ reward spending decisions, and they do not appear to adjust their behavior based on who is being rewarded or how much anyone spends. Overall, they respond to the aggregate pattern of high group cooperation by settling into stable, high contributions, but they refrain from using outcome information to fine-tune or personalize their strategy beyond that.

text: They observe others’ contribution patterns and infer types from behavior (e.g., calling uncooperative or inconsistent players bots or trolls, and later correcting that once others explain confusion). When others’ misunderstandings lead to low contributions and lower group payoffs, they react through explanation and coordination rather than retaliation or contribution cuts. They explicitly point out when someone is effectively contributing negatively or making a mistake, and they frame that in an instructive, not accusatory, way. They do not comment directly on who is rewarding whom, and they do not adjust their own actions in response to reward flows. When the group successfully achieves everyone contributing maximally and outcomes become high and uniform, they celebrate the shared payoff and the speed/ease of rounds, reinforcing the cooperative norm socially. They show mild concern that later parts of the experiment might try to induce backstabbing, which suggests they are attuned to how payoff structures can change social dynamics, but in this game they continue to act in a trust-based, group-benefit-oriented way.

text: They observe others’ contributions and the use of rewards but do not comment on specific individuals’ payoffs or spending. They do not praise or criticize those who reward, nor those who deviate in contribution. When some players under-contribute or spend heavily on rewards, the focal player maintains their cooperative pattern and silence regarding those choices. This suggests a non-confrontational, low-monitoring stance: they track outcomes well enough to keep contributing efficiently but avoid moralizing or policing others. Receiving modest reward once does not change their subsequent behavior; they do not shift to favoring that mechanism or to reciprocating. Their only clear behavioral reaction to group outcomes is the brief, modest reduction in contribution when group contributions fragment, followed by a rapid return to full cooperation once full-group coordination is reestablished. This implies that their primary feedback channel is coarse—“is the group generally coordinating on the efficient norm or not?”—rather than a fine-grained response to individual-level outcomes.

text: The player observes that non-contributors systematically attain higher individual payoffs when others contribute, yet they do not adjust their own behavior in response. They neither reduce cooperation when freeriders benefit more, nor increase cooperation beyond the already-maximal level when others contribute more. Their response to others’ outcomes is thus behaviorally inert: they maintain full cooperation regardless of who gains or loses. Since no explicit punishment or reward mechanisms are available, there is no direct evidence about how they would allocate costly sanctions or rewards. However, from their unwavering cooperative stance in the face of repeated exploitation, it is reasonable (though still uncertain) to infer that they are either strongly inequality-tolerant when inequality arises from under-contribution, or that they focus on the absolute level of group welfare rather than its distribution. They appear not to use others’ higher payoffs as a trigger for strategic retaliation via defection. There is no sign of reactive learning from outcome patterns. Even when low group contributions reduce everyone’s earnings, they do not respond by protecting themselves through defection. This suggests a player who either discounts relative payoffs and outcome-based reciprocity, or who does not infer that deviating themselves would improve their own earnings. Their observable type is an outcome-insensitive, norm-driven cooperator: others’ payoffs, whether fair or unfair, do not induce behavioral change.

text: The player repeatedly observes that many others free ride and obtain higher payoffs, yet they do not alter their own cooperative behavior in response. This indicates a high tolerance for unequal outcomes and exploitation, or alternatively a deliberate choice to ignore such inequities. They do not engage in responsive strategies such as “retaliatory defection” or “protecting themselves” once it becomes clear that some group members systematically benefit more by contributing nothing. Because punishment and reward are not available in this environment, there is no direct behavioral evidence of how they would spend costly points to sanction or reward others. However, their willingness to keep cooperating despite persistent free riding suggests they are unlikely to favor harsh, payoff-correcting responses; they appear more focused on sustaining contribution than on disciplining defectors or selectively incentivizing cooperators. Their behavior provides no indication of tracking specific individuals’ outcomes for reciprocation or targeted response; they treat the group as a whole and maintain their cooperative stance regardless of how advantageous the situation is for particular others. Overall, they react to others’ higher or lower earnings with behavioral inertia: they register these outcomes enough to see that exploitation is possible but do not let it redirect their own strategy.

text: This player is highly responsive to the structure of others’ outcomes but only in a self-serving direction. After seeing that non-contributors are systematically earning more than contributors, they align themselves with the better-paid group and remain there. Others’ lower payoffs do not trigger any compensatory behavior; instead, they exploit the payoff asymmetry. They appear to infer that other players either accept, tolerate, or fail to react to unfairness. Observing that cooperators keep contributing despite earning less, they come to believe that they can continue to free-ride without material consequence. Their learning is fast and one-sided: they update toward “defecting pays and is safe” and never backtrack. Because explicit punishment and reward mechanisms are absent, they never have to confront direct social disapproval or material sanctions. Their behavior suggests they do not internally substitute moral or fairness concerns for these absent mechanisms; they remain indifferent to the fact that their choices reliably worsen others’ relative outcomes. They exhibit no indirect reciprocity: they do not “reward” high contributors by joining them, nor do they “punish” low contributors by stepping up their own effort. Overall, they respond to others’ payoffs as information about how much they themselves can safely defect, not as a cue to coordinate, reciprocate, or correct inequalities.
