text: The player repeatedly observes that some others obtain higher payoffs by contributing nothing or less, yet they continue to contribute fully for most of the game. This indicates a muted immediate reaction to payoff disadvantage: they do not quickly mimic successful free-riders, nor do they appear to condition their own choice tightly on who benefited most in the prior outcome. They show a willingness to keep cooperating even when low contributors consistently earn more, suggesting that inequity aversion (if present) is initially overridden by their cooperative or norm-following motives. However, the eventual choice to free-ride after many such experiences shows that inequity and repeated observation of others’ advantageous outcomes do matter over time. Their late defection plausibly reflects accumulated frustration or learning that the group will not converge to high, symmetric contributions, pushing them to protect their own payoff at least once. Because no punishment or reward is available in this environment, we cannot observe any explicit sanctioning or compensating behavior (unknown). There is also no direct evidence of targeting specific peers, since their contribution choice affects everyone symmetrically. However, their sustained full cooperation in the presence of visible, repeated free-riding indicates a relatively forgiving or patient social style: they do not immediately retaliate against others’ non-cooperation by switching off their own. In sum, when others gain from free-riding, this player initially responds by staying cooperative and absorbing the disadvantage, signaling a strong internal cooperation norm and social tolerance. Only after repeated exposure to such outcomes do they temporarily align their action with the dominant self-interested pattern, indicating that observed payoff structures eventually influence their behavior, but only with delay and only modestly in this setting.

text: The player repeatedly observes that others’ contributions generate substantial group returns and that contributors often earn less than non-contributors, including themselves. Nevertheless, they largely maintain their free-riding strategy. This suggests they respond to others’ outcomes primarily as confirmation that exploiting cooperative behavior is profitable rather than as a prompt to join in. They do not escalate contributions in response to high cooperative participation by others; if anything, their behavior indicates an expectation that others will continue to contribute even without reciprocal effort. When cooperative levels fluctuate, they do not systematically adjust to stabilize group welfare; instead, they continue to prioritize their own payoff, indicating low responsiveness to efficiency losses borne by the group. There is no mechanism for explicit punishment or reward in this game, so their disposition toward spending resources to sanction or support others is unknown. However, given their reluctance to incur any cost to help others through contributions, it is low-confidence but plausible that they would also be reluctant to bear personal cost to intervene via punishment or reward in similar settings. The player appears relatively unaffected by inequality in payoffs. When they personally benefit more than cooperators, they continue with the same free-riding pattern rather than attempting to reduce disparities. When some others shift toward more self-interested play, the focal player does not react with either “retaliatory” cooperation breakdown (because they are already mostly non-cooperative) or compensatory generosity. Overall, they come across as an individually focused, outcome-oriented type who tracks others’ outcomes mainly to confirm that their own strategy is working, not to align with social norms or fairness.

text: The player regularly observes that many others contribute while they do not, yet they persist in free-riding. This implies that seeing others obtain lower payoffs as a consequence of contributing does not induce them to adjust toward more cooperative behavior. They appear indifferent to the fact that cooperators often earn less than they do, and they do not attempt to “correct” this through voluntary contributions. Because no punishment or reward options exist, there is no direct behavioral evidence of how they would spend resources to alter others’ payoffs. However, from their unwavering non-contribution despite clear group provision, it is reasonable (though low confidence) to infer that they are unlikely to incur personal costs to help norm-enforce cooperation or to compensate others. They seem to accept and exploit a setting where others’ contributions raise the group return and their own payoff without imposing any obligation to reciprocate. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes primarily by maintaining a strategy that preserves their relative advantage: they continue to free-ride even when this means others repeatedly bear the cost of generating the public good. Any emotional or moral reactions remain unknown, but behaviorally they act as a payoff-focused self-interested defector who does not adjust in response to observed inequality or collective losses.

text: The player consistently observes others earning less than them when they free-ride and still continues to free-ride, indicating low behavioral sensitivity to others’ disadvantaged outcomes. They do not react to lower payoffs for high contributors by increasing their own contributions in a sustained way. When others’ payoffs decline along with decreasing group contributions, they do not step in to stabilize the group outcome; instead, they maintain or return to non-contribution. In environments without explicit punishment or reward, they do not spontaneously “self-discipline” by contributing more after observing that cooperators are systematically worse off than non-contributors. This suggests that their internal response to others’ outcomes is not driven by concerns about compensating cooperators or correcting payoff imbalances. Their occasional contributions do not reliably coincide with moments when others are especially disadvantaged, so there is low evidence that they are motivated by altruistic concern for those doing poorly. Instead, their behavior fits a belief that others will continue to contribute enough to keep the public good profitable, allowing them to largely maintain a free-riding stance. They act as if they expect at least a core of persistent cooperators, and they exploit this expectation rather than reciprocating. Unknowns: Their emotional or moral reaction to others earning less is not observable; we only see that it does not translate into a consistent behavioral adjustment. Their exact beliefs about how long cooperation persists, or whether they feel any guilt or justification for free-riding, remain unknown.

text: This player closely attends to group outcomes, as evidenced by the timing of their one switch to free-riding after observing recurrent patterns of others earning more while giving less. Their behavior implies that they notice when non-contributors systematically secure higher payoffs than contributors, and this does influence them, but only transiently. Their reaction to free-riders is measured and limited. Rather than retaliating in a sustained way or fully joining the low-contribution camp, they perform a single “test” of the free-rider strategy, then abandon it. This suggests a learning and comparison process: they explore what it feels like and what payoff it yields, then decide not to adopt it as a new norm. Without access to punishment or reward, their only lever is to adjust their own contribution, and they choose not to use that lever as a tool for sustained sanctioning or pressure. They show resilience in the face of unfair payoff structures. Even as some others repeatedly earn more by contributing less, this player does not escalate defection or attempt to “match” the low contributors over time. Instead, they revert to pro-social behavior, indicating that their cooperative identity or belief about “the right thing to do” outweighs reactive responses to others’ higher payoffs. Their beliefs about others are indirectly inferred: they seem to assume that enough others will keep contributing to make cooperation worthwhile, despite visible and persistent free-riding. They do not visibly downgrade their expectations about the group in a lasting way. They likely view cooperation as a default or desired standard, and interpret others’ higher payoffs from defection as problematic but not sufficient reason to abandon that standard. Overall, this player responds to others’ outcomes with brief experimentation and mild adjustment, but they do not engage in ongoing payoff-matching or tit-for-tat. They are best characterized as a mostly principled cooperator who is aware of and momentarily tempted by exploitative payoffs, yet ultimately remains anchored in a cooperative, efficiency-oriented approach.

text: The player repeatedly observes that others who contribute more often end up with lower payoffs than themselves, yet they continue to free-ride in most rounds. This indicates that they respond more to the payoff gradient (defection is individually advantageous) than to any norm of reciprocity or mutual support. They do not move toward higher or more stable contributions even when the group’s cooperative level is relatively high for multiple consecutive rounds. They do not appear to condition their behavior on specific individuals’ outcomes; their pattern does not track any one peer’s shifts. Instead, they seem to respond at most to the overall payoff structure: defection yields higher individual returns given the group’s contributions. When they do choose to contribute, they do not follow this with a new cooperative streak despite others still bearing costs, which suggests that any inequality they observe does not strongly motivate them to correct it. There is no discernible pattern of “rewarding” cooperative phases with sustained cooperation or “punishing” group-wide defection by withdrawing contributions; their own payoff remains the central reference point. With no explicit punishment or reward mechanisms available, their reaction to others’ outcomes manifests only through their own contribution decisions, and those decisions show minimal pro-social adjustment. They treat others’ lower payoffs relative to theirs as acceptable collateral of the game structure rather than as a signal to change course. Their beliefs about others’ behavior are not directly observable, but their persistence in free-riding despite ongoing contributions from many peers is consistent with expecting that others will continue to contribute enough to make defection profitable.

text: This player repeatedly observes that non-contributors receive higher payoffs than contributors, sometimes by a substantial margin. Despite this, they do not systematically switch to free riding, indicating a relatively muted payoff-reactivity. They do notice and briefly imitate the profitable strategy of not contributing once, which shows they are sensitive to relative earnings and can adapt behavior in that direction. However, their rapid return to cooperation suggests that material payoff differences alone do not override their underlying cooperative stance. They do not have access to punishment or reward tools in this setting, so their response to others’ outcomes is expressed only through their own contribution decisions. They never engage in sustained retaliatory defection against low contributors, nor do they escalate defection in response to persistent inequality. This implies a low tendency toward payoff-based retribution and a relatively high tolerance for being taken advantage of. Their pattern is consistent with a player who tracks that free riding is individually lucrative but continues to prioritize maintaining a cooperative norm. They seem to accept that some group members will earn more and still choose to contribute, implying that they value group efficiency or adherence to a cooperation norm more than strictly maximizing their own or correcting others’ payoffs. Their learning is therefore conservative: they register others’ higher earnings, briefly test the exploitative strategy, but ultimately default back to cooperative behavior rather than re-optimizing around others’ superior payoffs.

text: The player’s cooperation is insensitive to others’ payoffs and contribution patterns. As others alternate between relatively cooperative and more free-riding profiles, including periods where free riders earn strictly more than contributors, the player does not adjust. They neither reduce their contributions to avoid being exploited nor appear to reward cooperative phases by increasing from a previously lower level; their behavior is flat. They observe that some players systematically benefit from contributing nothing while still receiving substantial returns from the group. Despite this, they do not respond by imitating the profitable strategy or by withdrawing their own support. This suggests that either they do not prioritize payoff comparison, or they do but choose not to act on it. There is no evidence of emotional reciprocity (retaliation, “teaching a lesson,” or strategic forgiveness) because they never change their action in response to others’ outcomes. Because there is no punishment or reward mechanism available in this environment, we cannot observe how they would allocate costly sanctions or bonuses; their latent willingness to punish defectors or reward cooperators is unknown. All that can be inferred is that, when the only lever they have is their own contribution, they do not use it as a contingent response to others’ earnings or behavior. Their type is best characterized as a steadfast, non-conditional cooperator whose choices are effectively independent of others’ outcomes.

text: They consistently maintain full cooperation despite observing clear payoffs from free-riding and substantial heterogeneity in others’ reward spending. When others earn high payoffs by contributing nothing, they do not retaliate or reduce their own contributions. When some players incur large personal costs to reward the group, they do not visibly “match” these efforts, either by upping their own rewards or by adjusting contributions (which are already maximal). The presence of highly generous rewarders does not induce them into a competitive generosity spiral; they stay with low or zero reward spending. This suggests a relatively detached stance toward others’ payoff outcomes: they note them (it is visible) but do not allow them to reshape their own core behavior. There is no evidence of envy, revenge, or payoff-based conditionality. Their response profile is best characterized as stoic and rule-driven: others’ gains, losses, or sacrifices have minimal observable impact on their own contribution and reward decisions. Whether they internally approve, disapprove, or simply ignore others’ payoffs remains unknown.

text: They closely observe others’ outcomes but respond primarily through their own contributions and one-time reward burst, not through direct commentary. They show no explicit emotional reaction to others sacrificing a lot of coins to reward the group, and they do not verbally praise or criticize heavy rewarders or non-rewarders. However, their eventual symmetric reward move suggests that, at some level, they register the sustained cooperation and perhaps feel a delayed need to reciprocate in aggregate. When others defect on contributions, they do not adjust their own contributions downward or punish via withholding future rewards; they keep cooperating. This indicates a relatively high tolerance for short-term unfairness and a focus on preserving high group payoffs rather than equalizing earnings. They do not react negatively when they themselves are called “greedy,” and they do not seem sensitive to reputation attacks in chat; there is no sign that social disapproval drives their choices. Spikes in others’ reward spending do not trigger matching behavior or retaliation; they continue their own low-reward strategy. They treat others’ costly generosity as something to accept rather than something that obligates direct response. In summary, they are outcome-aware but behaviorally self-contained: they notice what others do and earn, but modulate their own actions mostly according to their own payoff calculus and occasional, broad-brush reciprocity rather than responsive tit-for-tat.

text: They observe that some others occasionally defect and that some peers spend heavily to reward the group, but they do not materially adjust their own economic behavior in response. When another player free-rides, they maintain full contribution and refrain from retaliatory measures (which in this design would have to be indirect, since punishment is unavailable). Their comments suggest awareness of greed and acknowledgment of uneven contribution, but this awareness does not translate into strategic countermeasures. When others spend substantial amounts on reward, including generous “gifting” toward them, they do not reciprocate with their own rewards; instead, they continue to follow their existing contribution-only strategy. They do engage verbally with group dynamics (e.g., playfully referencing a player who alternates between cooperating and defecting, and joining the group’s ongoing commentary on that player), which shows social awareness of who is doing what. However, their behavioral response is inertial: they hold cooperation constant, ignore reward as a tool, and seem content to let others’ costly reward behavior be their choice. In sum, they are responsive at the conversational level but behaviorally steadfast, neither punishing nor rewarding through the available mechanism, and relying on the baseline structure of the PGG and their own principled cooperation rather than outcome-contingent adjustments.

text: They pay attention to others’ contribution behavior and occasionally comment on it, but they do not explicitly reference others’ specific payoffs or reward expenditures. Their reaction to a consistently low contributor is to label that behavior as greedy in chat, indicating a negative normative judgment of free-riding—at least when performed by someone else. When later singled out by another player for not contributing, their response is not to correct course or justify rationally, but to respond playfully and embrace their avatar identity, effectively downplaying the seriousness of the accusation. They do not publicly acknowledge the substantial reward costs borne by certain group members, nor do they express gratitude or concern for players who spend heavily on rewarding others. Despite being a net recipient of rewards over the game, they offer no explicit verbal thanks or reciprocal reward, suggesting that others’ generosity does not substantially shift their strategy. Overall, they notice and comment on conspicuous deviations from cooperation when it is someone else defecting, but they treat both others’ heavy spending on rewards and the group’s tolerance of their own free-riding as exploitable features rather than signals that warrant changing their own behavior.

text: They monitor and understand others’ contributions and, indirectly, payoff patterns, but their behavioral response is muted and forgiving. When one peer clearly free-rides while others cooperate, they react verbally by nudging that player to contribute but make no visible attempt to retaliate economically, either by reducing their own contributions or by manipulating rewards. They do not escalate when the same player or another peer continues to occasionally deviate; their own strategy remains unchanged. They also witness some players spending substantial amounts on rewards—sometimes in large, costly bursts directed toward the group—without imitating that intensity or commenting on it. This indicates that they notice prosocial excesses and free-riding alike but maintain a steady, cooperative course rather than adapting aggressively. Their few remarks about game length and “forever” reflect mild fatigue or bemusement, not strategic anxiety; uncertainty about horizon does not alter their behavior. They accept being rewarded by others without explicit expressions of gratitude or attempts to reciprocate directly to those benefactors. Overall, they interpret others’ outcomes primarily as confirmation that the cooperative equilibrium is working and worth sustaining, rather than as signals for strategic adjustment; they are a resilient, steady cooperator who prefers social nudges and personal consistency over payoff-contingent reactions.

text: They show strong behavioral tolerance for outcome asymmetries. They continue contributing fully even when certain others earn more by not contributing, and even when some players repeatedly burn their own payoffs to reward everyone else. There is no sign that they adjust contributions or rewards in response to others’ payoff patterns, whether those patterns favor free-riders, heavy rewarders, or consistent cooperators. They do not comment on others’ payoffs, reward expenditures, or emerging inequalities in the chat. They also do not attempt to reward or offset particularly costly prosocial acts by others, suggesting limited sensitivity to the magnitude of others’ sacrifices. Instead, they maintain a steady cooperative baseline regardless of whether others are exploiting, supporting, or over-rewarding the group. Their implicit belief about others appears to be that group cooperation is either stable enough or sufficiently “free money” that fine-tuning based on others’ individual outcomes is unnecessary. Learning from others’ outcomes is essentially absent; their behavior appears rule-based rather than outcome-contingent. They thus present as a robust, non-retaliatory cooperator who is largely indifferent—behaviorally and communicatively—to distributional nuances and to how others use the reward mechanism.

text: The player monitors others’ contributions and payoffs and occasionally comments on deviations but does not fundamentally change their own long-run cooperative stance as a direct reaction to individuals’ earnings or reward usage. They notice and publicly call out a specific group member’s non-contribution behavior, highlighting that this member is benefiting more while not putting in. Yet, despite recognizing this free-riding, they largely continue to contribute fully in subsequent rounds when that member defects, implying that the player tolerates some inequity when the group outcome remains strong. This indicates a willingness to prioritize group efficiency over strict equality, at least in the short to medium term. They also pay attention to how long the game is lasting and how much effort others are expending (including notable high reward spending by some peers), but they do not explicitly respond to those high-cost generosity signals. Their own contribution and reward decisions do not visibly escalate in response to others’ large reward expenditures; they accept being a net beneficiary of others’ generosity without compensating behavior. This suggests limited reciprocity toward costly prosocial acts directed at the group, and a comfort with asymmetric benefit if it does not provoke social sanction. Receiving rewards does not appear to significantly alter their strategy: when they are rewarded after cooperating, they continue cooperating; when they are not rewarded after cooperating, they also continue cooperating in many cases. When they free-ride and are not punished or singled out, their willingness to occasionally defect persists. Thus their behavior is only weakly contingent on immediate payoff differentials; they seem more guided by a loose plan (cooperate most of the time, defect opportunistically) than by fine-grained reactions to each round’s outcome. Overall, they are observant and mildly responsive in speech to others’ outcomes but relatively stable in their own mixed strategy, allowing them to benefit from both the cooperative norm and occasional self-serving deviations.

text: They observe others’ contributions and payoffs but do not materially adjust their own contribution level in response; defectors and free-riders do not lead them to reduce cooperation. Likewise, they do not switch to rewarding or withholding rewards in a clearly contingent way based on who previously rewarded them or how much others spent. They sometimes receive rewards but do not visibly react by changing either their reward spending or contribution behavior, which remains uniformly cooperative. When others keep their full endowment while the rest cooperate, they continue to contribute fully and, in some periods, still end up rewarding the entire group, including previous freeriders, suggesting they are not focused on correcting inequality through their own actions. They show no punitive instincts; they neither retaliate against those who benefit more nor withhold group-wide generosity to discipline them. Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is characterized by steadfast cooperative behavior and occasional, non-selective generosity, rather than by strategic adaptation to others’ payoffs or attempts to restore fairness through differential treatment.

text: They pay attention to outcome information but react in a measured and analytic way, with very little emotional volatility. When others contribute less or behave unexpectedly, they respond by reframing the situation as still profitable and downplaying the damage, emphasizing that everyone remains “in profit” so long as the overall cooperation level is high. When deduction patterns are confusing or apparently misaligned with contributions, they quickly suggest alternative explanations such as system-driven zeros or lag, and they generalize this into a hypothesis that the study may be deliberately manipulating contributions or deductions to test trust and conflict. They show no signs of anger or calls for retaliation, even when high contributors are punished or when they themselves lose coins, and they do not join in shaming of specific low contributors. Instead, they maintain the message that the game remains advantageous and that trusting one another is still the rational path. They show interest in the payoff structure and conversion to real money, doing back-of-the-envelope reasoning about how coins translate into currency and speculating about total earnings, but this curiosity does not lead them to squeeze others or adjust their behavior in a predatory way. Overall, they interpret others’ outcomes through a cooperative, slightly experimentalist lens: anomalies are data points or design quirks rather than moral offenses, and the primary response is to keep the group coordinated and focused on maximizing the shared surplus rather than to redistribute payoffs via punishment.

text: They appear largely unreactive to the detailed pattern of who is punished and how much. While many group members discuss deductions, accuse likely punishers, or express outrage or sympathy, this player does not comment on specific punishment events and never adjusts their contribution strategy in response to shocks like unfair or noisy sanctions. They also do not adjust their own contribution, punishment, or reward decisions in response to observed exploitation or altruism. This indicates a non-reactive stance toward others’ outcome profiles: they hold their own cooperative rule constant and appear indifferent, or at least behaviorally unresponsive, to the evolving distribution of payoffs. Whether they experience fairness concerns internally is unknown; externally, their behavior remains calm, consistent, and non-confrontational.

text: The player sees that others sometimes gain higher payoffs by not contributing and that some players spend substantial amounts repeatedly on rewards. They also see that this spending reduces the rewarders’ own payoffs. Despite this, they do not visibly react—no chat comments, no shifts in contributions, no move to punish free-riders, and no decision to start rewarding strong cooperators. They do not join in when another player suggests a group punishment norm against non-contributors, even though they initially verbally support full cooperation. Their behavior suggests a certain detachment from outcome-based social control: they maintain their own cooperative choice irrespective of whether others profit more or less, or whether others are “overpaying” to reward. They neither exploit the rewarders’ generosity by changing behavior (they are already maximally cooperative) nor support them by sharing the cost of rewards. Their sensitivity to distributional fairness at the payoff level appears low in terms of action: they tolerate outcome inequalities without behavioral adjustment, focusing solely on their own rule of full contribution.

text: They are attentive to others’ behavior and outcomes in a qualitative sense but rarely comment on specific payoffs or costs. When another player is spending heavily on rewards, they respond with a supportive remark highlighting the kindness of that behavior rather than its inefficiency. This indicates they value generosity and moral framing over strict payoff optimization in social commentary. They do not protest or criticize when one group member repeatedly free rides and earns higher payoffs; instead, they quietly continue to cooperate and do not adjust contributions downward in retaliation. They also do not react publicly to the large sacrifices made by the generous reward-giver, beyond a single positive comment and one small reward. Overall, they notice others’ efforts and deviations but maintain a non-confrontational, affirming stance, prioritizing group harmony and prosocial norms over explicit enforcement or payoff-based critique.

text: They pay attention to payoff-relevant mechanics and others’ spending. They question the value of deduction early, implying sensitivity to net payoff effects. Later, after observing a teammate repeatedly spend on rewards, they openly advise against continuing that behavior on grounds that it does not improve one’s own payoff, indicating that they monitor others’ costs and resulting payoffs enough to judge efficiency. They also casually remark on others’ behavior (e.g., joking about another’s pattern or name), but do not express annoyance or moral condemnation when others free-ride or overspend on rewards. Instead, they provide gentle, rationale-based guidance (e.g., “you don’t gain anything from it sadly”). Overall, they notice others’ outcomes and choices and respond with soft influence rather than moralistic or punitive reactions.

text: This player pays attention to others’ contributions and payoffs and adjusts reward patterns accordingly, but not their own contributions. When cooperative players earn less (due to rewarding others heavily), they often direct extra rewards toward those players, effectively compensating them for their costly prosocial behavior. This suggests some concern for supporting “overburdened cooperators” rather than for pure payoff equality across the whole group. They seem especially responsive to peers who combine high contributions with heavy reward spending. When such players end up with reduced net payoffs relative to free-riders, this player frequently uses their own reward budget to boost those peers, thereby endorsing and encouraging that costly prosocial role. They treat high-cost cooperators as key assets whose position in the group should be protected. They do not, however, punish low contributors (no punishment is available) nor do they visibly withdraw rewards from consistent free-riders in a strong way; instead, they simply do not reward them. The main “negative” response to low contribution is omission of reward, not any active retaliatory stance. Thus, they rely on positive reinforcement and selective support rather than deterrence. Even when the group’s aggregate cooperation drops and others’ payoffs fall, they largely maintain their own low contribution while sometimes continuing to reward certain cooperators. This shows that their behavioral adjustments to others’ outcomes are channeled mostly through the reward stage. They appear to believe that supporting a cooperative core—via targeted rewards to high-contribution, high-reward players—is sufficient to keep the system beneficial for them, even while they themselves continue to free-ride on the public good. In personality terms (inferred with low confidence), they resemble a “reward-focused opportunistic cooperator”: attentive to who helps the group, willing to spend to recognize them, but reluctant to personally bear the primary cost of contribution.

text: This player observes that several others incur significant costs in the reward stage and that some peers’ payoffs are visibly reduced by their generous rewarding. They nevertheless do not materially adjust toward more prosocial reward or more stable high contribution. Even when others’ outcomes clearly show consistent generosity and cooperation, the player continues to alternate between cooperating and free-riding and often refrains from rewarding. They do not appear to punish or deter high spenders by withholding future contributions specifically in response to others’ reward behavior; instead, their contribution pattern is only loosely connected to others’ observed outcomes. Likewise, they do not seem to protect or bolster those whose payoffs suffer because they are rewarding a lot; there is no robust evidence of “support the supporter” behavior. Overall, the player treats others’ outcomes more as contextual information than as normative signals requiring adjustment. Others’ high costs and generosity do not strongly reshape their own strategy. This suggests a personality type that is relatively resistant to social pressure, focused on personal earnings, and only weakly motivated by equity or reciprocity concerns in response to what happens to others.

text: They observe that some low contributors consistently earn high payoffs, sometimes higher than the fully cooperative players, and that several others invest significant amounts in rewarding or selectively supporting cooperators. Despite this, they do not adjust strategy: they neither punish (not available), nor withhold cooperation, nor shift to rewarding to counterbalance payoff inequalities. They also show no signs of reacting to others’ rising or falling payoff trajectories—whether others are being drained by heavy rewarding or enriched by free‑riding does not alter their own actions. This points toward a type who prioritizes maintaining their cooperative principle over responding to outcome‑based fairness or strategic concerns. They appear largely outcome‑insensitive in practice: others’ costs, rewards, and final payoffs are observed but not used to condition their own behavior. Overall, they embody a “principled unconditional cooperator who ignores the reward channel,” sustaining full cooperation regardless of group dynamics or personal treatment.

text: The player is highly attentive to others’ contributions and outcomes in the aggregate, as shown by how their rewards track cooperative effort and broader group payoff patterns. When others maintain high contributions, they often invest heavily in rewarding them, even at clear personal cost, implicitly acknowledging that cooperative others are generating high group payoffs (including their own). They do not respond punitively to low contributors; instead, they simply exclude them from reward. Their reactions to others’ payoffs are thus asymmetric: high payoffs tied to high contributions are positively reinforced, while low or moderate payoffs from low contributions are ignored rather than punished. They show little sensitivity to the fact that some cooperators already receive substantial rewards from others; they still reward these same highly cooperative players, which suggests they prioritize reinforcing “good” contributors over smoothing payoff inequalities. At the same time, they do not react defensively when others spend heavily on reward while they remain unrewarded and richer in some periods; there is no sign of retaliation, contribution changes, or reward withdrawal driven by envy or perceived unfairness. Their overarching pattern is: observe who contributes, support those contributors with reward, but preserve their own non-contributing stance regardless of how generous or costly others’ actions become. Overall, this player acts as a “selfish meta-supporter of cooperation”: they free-ride on the public good while actively and repeatedly subsidizing and encouraging cooperators, seemingly to keep cooperation alive and personally profitable, without engaging in reciprocity or altering their own contribution level. Their learning and adaptation appear limited; once this pattern is established early, they maintain it with only minor fluctuations in how intensely they reward.

text: This player clearly observes that some group members invest heavily in both contributions and reward, while others free-ride in different ways. Their own actions show that they understand who cooperates and who spends costly reward: a few others consistently reward contributors, one member persistently withholds contributions while spending on reward, and some switch between strategies. Despite these visible differences in others’ payoffs and social investments, this player does not condition their own behavior in a socially responsive way. They do not reward high contributors, do not punish (not available), and do not adopt the contribution norms of the more cooperative sub-group. They respond to the *aggregate* outcome more than to individual roles: when the public pot is large and many others are contributing, they sometimes join in; when the pot is sustain­able without them, they often withhold. They ignore the fact that some individuals sacrifice their own payoffs via reward to maintain cooperation. They also tolerate strong asymmetries: others often end up with lower immediate payoffs because they spend on reward or contribute more, while this player’s payoff remains relatively strong due to low costs and incoming rewards. There is no sign of them adjusting behavior to “even things out” or to relieve heavily burdened players. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes primarily as information about expected returns from the public fund, not as moral or relational signals. They are attentive to the strategic environment but not socially engaged in supporting or reinforcing others’ costly prosocial efforts.

text: The player monitors others’ contribution patterns and aggregate outcomes and uses rewards to shape the environment rather than changing their own contribution level. They observe persistent free-riding by some players who accumulate high payoffs while contributing nothing or very little; nevertheless, they continue to give full contributions and do not redirect rewards toward these high-payoff non-contributors. High payoff alone does not attract their support; visible cooperative effort does. They also see that some peers bear large reward costs, sometimes to the point of markedly reducing their own payoffs. The focal player does not imitate this extreme level of expenditure, but they consistently adopt a “middle-high” reward-spending profile, indicating that they are willing to share the burden of sustaining cooperation without competing to be the most generous at all costs. Their behavior suggests they interpret others’ reward spending as a positive signal of cooperative intent and reliability, which reinforces their own willingness to keep contributing and rewarding. They do not appear to adjust contributions downward when others’ payoffs from free-riding exceed theirs; envy or inequality aversion in the sense of reducing cooperation is not evident. Their main response channel is to keep rewarding cooperators, sometimes more intensely when contribution by others is low, suggesting they see themselves as responsible for helping keep cooperation alive in the group rather than reacting defensively to exploitation. Overall, they behave as a robust, norm-driven cooperator and active pro-social rewarder, moderately reciprocal but strongly anchored in a personal standard of full contribution and conditional rewarding of cooperative behavior.

text: They clearly notice group outcomes and align themselves with the cooperative majority, but they rarely comment on specific payoffs, punishments, or rewards. They respond positively to good collective outcomes (“nice” after a high‑contribution round, affectionate symbols during group celebration) and laugh along with the group when a known defector is punished, without personally piling on. They do not express moral judgment or anger; their only explicit reaction to harsh sanctions is shared amusement, not condemnation or sympathy. They never call out low contributors, never push for stronger sanctions, and do not publicly praise high contributors by name. They focus more on meta‑aspects (duration, procedure) and on maintaining a friendly atmosphere than on micro‑managing the payoff landscape. Overall, they accept the emergent norm‑enforcement pattern run by others, benefit from it, and stay quietly cooperative and sociable without taking an active role in policing or rewarding.

text: They pay attention to how others behave and occasionally react, but in a light, non-enforcing way. When a player defects or gets heavily punished, they express amusement or mild “oof” sympathy rather than outrage or moralizing. They join in gentle teasing of a problematic player but stop short of endorsing more punitive escalation. They explicitly highlight that many people contributing full amounts is “extra monies” and that their collective strategy is working, reinforcing the group’s focus on stable, high payoffs. They do not appear to track individual histories for strategic purposes; their comments are about group-level patterns (everyone putting in maximum, bonuses accumulating, good group performance) rather than targeted reputational judgments. Overall, they interpret others’ outcomes mainly as confirmation that the cooperative strategy is effective and that the group is enjoyable to play with, not as triggers for corrective action. Overall type label: “Friendly Unconditional Cooperator, Non-Enforcer” They prioritize stable, full cooperation, value group harmony and speed/simplicity, avoid both punishment and reward mechanisms, and communicate in a warm, humorous, consensus-supporting style.

text: The player watches a wide range of outcomes—costly punishment cascades, heavy use of reward by some, free-rider windfalls followed by sanctions, and extreme spending by a few “altruistic” or erratic participants—without visibly reacting in chat or changing their core contribution strategy. Their single early punishment act coincides with a prior low contributor who had just been sanctioned, implying that they are sensitive to patterns of deviation and willing to “pile on” enforcement in defense of the cooperative scheme. Their later three-person punishment act aligns with a period of complex reward and punishment that distorts simple cooperative payoffs; this suggests they attend to payoff structure and perceive overactive or misaligned use of secondary mechanisms as problematic or unfair. However, they do not begin rewarding after seeing others earn large gains via reward, and they do not copy hyper-punitive or hyper-rewarding styles. They learn primarily one lesson from others’ actions: free‑riding draws real and substantial sanctions, making it unprofitable; consequently, they stick with full contributions. Overall, they respond more through quiet adjustment of their own behavior (ending defection and using rare norm-enforcing punishment) than through active experimentation with the broader incentive tools others are using.

text: They pay attention to others’ outcomes and the group’s enforcement dynamics but keep their own behavior simple. They acknowledge and lightly praise cooperative behavior (e.g., early positive comments about others “coming in clutch” or about “max time”) and verbally endorse the idea that coordination yields higher payoffs. When others are punished heavily for defection, they do not express sympathy or outrage; they mostly ignore the drama and keep focusing on max contributions and finishing quickly. They recognize the existence of bonus payments and discuss them in chat, aligning with others’ focus on collective payoff and time-efficiency. When the group debates mutual reward schemes to exploit the fact that rewards are net-positive, they verbally signal willingness to participate but fall back to the safer, simpler equilibrium of just maxing contributions without extra mechanisms, especially once others express concerns about trust and complexity. When some players are punished multiple times or spend heavily on rewards/punishments (with visible large payoff losses), they do not adjust their strategy to exploit or compensate those players. Their response to others’ costly behavior is essentially: maintain cooperation, avoid using costly mechanisms themselves, and keep the game moving. Overall, they act as a stable, non-enforcing, non-manipulative cooperator who recognizes group-level incentives but prioritizes low-friction, straightforward play over fine-tuned optimization.

text: They pay some attention to others’ outcomes and behavior, but their reactions are restrained. They make lighthearted comments when someone behaves ideologically (“let’s not be tories”), and later, when a known defector is punished heavily, they publicly state distrust of that player, aligning verbally with enforcement without spending coins themselves. When others discuss punishments, rewards, and losing points, they do not moralize strongly or escalate conflict; they let others discuss penalties and simply continue to cooperate. They participate in meta‑discussion about the study’s design and bonus structure, and even proactively share the completion code for the benefit of the group, indicating concern for others’ real‑world payoffs. Overall, they appear attentive to fairness and group earnings but prefer soft social signals (agreement, distrust statements, sharing helpful information) over directly manipulating others’ in‑game payoffs.

text: They pay attention to others’ contributions, punishments, and rewards and reference global patterns and bonuses more than individual micro-outcomes. They notice and verbally reinforce the fact that everyone is contributing maximum amounts, highlighting “big bonus” potential and celebrating reaching threshold totals. Their focus is on collective success and bonus payments, not on tracking who is slightly ahead or behind. When one player defects early by contributing nothing, they accept and join the group’s harsh punishment episode. Their subsequent comments emphasize that non-cooperation hurts the defector more and that there is bonus money tied to points, implicitly reinforcing that defection is both irrational and socially discouraged. When some players later use reward and punishment heavily and distort payoffs (others ending rounds with very low or very high net points), they do not comment on those extremes, nor do they intervene with counter-punishment or compensatory reward. Their response is effectively “stay the course”: continue contributing max and not get involved in complex payoff engineering. They do not express hostility toward any individual, including defectors, but they do emphasize that the group gets a bonus according to accumulated points, implicitly valuing cooperative outcomes and long-term earnings. Their reactions to others’ outcomes thus show: (1) endorsement of punishing blatant free-riding when the group is aligned, (2) indifference to small, noisy deviations, and (3) an overarching orientation toward stable cooperation and shared monetary benefit.

text: They pay attention to the distribution of punishments and rewards and to how others’ payoffs are affected, but their reactions are mostly implicit and expressed via their own reward choices or light commentary rather than explicit judgments. They do not join in the joking “pile‑on” against low contributors, nor do they verbally condemn harsh punishments; instead, they quietly counterbalance by rewarding a heavily punished participant substantially and giving smaller boosts to steady cooperators. This indicates concern for fairness and aversion to excessive inequality, even when it costs them. They also support the group’s focus on maximizing shared bonus earnings and on avoiding coordination schemes (like universal rewarding) that create exploitable asymmetries or slow the game down. When others worry about timing, crashing, or study design, they respond with humor and reassurance, reinforcing group cohesion rather than anxiety. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes in a calm, prosocial, and efficiency‑oriented way: maintain full cooperation, correct perceived over‑punishment with positive transfers, avoid drama, and keep the group moving.

text: They attend to and interpret others’ payoffs and sanction flows, and occasionally comment in a way that reveals a norm-enforcement mindset. They correctly perceive which avatar had previously defected (“green bird”) and bring that history up later, suggesting they track reputations across rounds. Their own punishing choices align with this tracking: they hit known low contributors even when others are also punishing them, implying they deliberately reinforce the cost of deviation. When the group is discussing multipliers, bonuses, and the possibility of reward schemes, they show awareness of how the structure might be exploited but favor simple, robust full cooperation over more fragile side-arrangements. They refrain from punishing or rewarding once outcomes stabilize at near-perfect equality, indicating sensitivity to the idea that further sanctioning would be unnecessary or inefficient in that environment. Overall, they interpret others’ outcomes through a lens of “are they upholding or breaking the group-efficient, fair pattern?” and act accordingly, even at personal monetary cost. Overall type: a principled, high-cost norm enforcer and unconditional cooperator who uses both punishment and reward to stabilize group-wide full contribution, respects group welfare over individual payoff, and maintains a calm, lightly reflective social presence.

text: The player clearly tracks others’ actions but responds mostly through contribution stability and occasional sanctions rather than chat. They observe and react to: (a) deviations from full contribution, which trigger punishment; (b) restoration of cooperation by former deviants, after which they stop punishing and even later reward; and (c) large punishment and reward expenditures by others, which they do not imitate. They do not join episodes of heavy rewarding or punishing that are not directly about first‑order contribution, indicating that they are not drawn into cycles of escalating enforcement or generosity. They also do not adjust their own contribution level downward in response to others’ earning more through strategic rewards or free‑riding; their cooperative norm is robust against local inequality. There is no evidence of envy or of opportunistic mimicry of free‑riders once those free‑riders receive high net payoffs through others’ actions. Instead, they continue with a simple rule: contribute fully, punish clear defectors, occasionally reward clear cooperators, and ignore the rest. This suggests a principled, rule‑based type who focuses on contribution norms rather than on fine‑tuning individual payoffs.

text: They do not comment directly on others’ payoffs, punishments, or rewards in a way that shows moral judgment or empathy. They ignore episodes where others spend heavily on punishing or rewarding, and they do not praise or criticize extreme behaviors (for example, players devoting many coins to reward/punish in mid-game). They show no interest in punishing dominant free-riders elsewhere, nor in celebrating players who use reward altruistically. Their only explicit discussion of outcomes is at the meta-level: they emphasize that participants have “already been paid,” casting the in-game bonuses as less critical. In the reward-strategy debate, they focus on expected marginal gain per person and the complexity of fair allocation, treating others’ potential outcomes in a calculative rather than emotional way. They seem largely indifferent to the fine-grained distribution of payoffs across players, as long as group cooperation remains high enough to keep returns good. Their primary reference group is “we” at the level of everyone contributing 20, but when it comes to sanctions and extra mechanisms, they step back and treat them as optional and somewhat unimportant add-ons rather than central moral concerns. Overall type label: “Pragmatic Cooperator with Opportunistic Deviations, Non-Punishing and Non-Rewarding” They typically cooperate by contributing the maximum, mainly because the group coordinates on that and the payoffs are solid. They occasionally defect when they judge that the personal downside is tolerable, especially after reminding themselves and others that core payment is already secured. They avoid both punishment and reward entirely, treating those mechanisms as not worth the personal cost, and respond to sanctions with practical acceptance rather than anger, while maintaining a lightly rebellious, humorous communication style.

text: They attend closely to others’ behaviors and outcomes, especially when someone defects or is heavily punished. They comment jokingly but disapprovingly about specific low‑contribution players, reinforcing the message that such behavior is “bad” and socially noted. When a known defector is slammed with large deductions, they laugh and playfully admonish the target, yet also emphasize that continuing such behavior will “hurt” that player more, reflecting an understanding of the payoff consequences and a desire for them to adapt. They join in group talk about bonus structures and show clear interest in collective payoff maximization, emphasizing strategies that are both safe and quick. When a risky mutual reward idea comes up, they publicly express distrust in the previously untrustworthy player and advocate sticking with the simple max‑contribute strategy, indicating sensitivity to exploitation risk and coordination failure. Overall, they interpret outcomes in terms of fairness, group benefit, and bonus optimization, and they use both chat and occasional punishment to steer the group back toward stable, efficient full cooperation.

text: They observe a wide range of others’ outcomes, including heavy punishment of free riders, costly reward experiments, and later, fine‑tuned sanctions targeting a specific unreliable cooperator. They almost never comment directly on specific payoff patterns or target choices. Their single explicit response related to mechanisms is to the proposal of a mutual reward scheme: they frame their rejection in terms of effort and time rather than fairness or trust. They do not publicly pressure defectors, applaud punishers, or criticize those using the mechanisms in idiosyncratic ways. Instead, they quietly stick with max contributions and endorse the simple cooperative “just max out” approach in line with others’ comments. They also do not exploit knowledge of others’ outcomes to improve their private payoff beyond the one occasion of contributing nothing (which could be exploratory, convenience‑driven, or simply opportunistic; motivation is unknown). After that, they ignore the possibility of targeted free‑riding or strategic reward/punishment based on observed histories. In sum, they monitor the environment enough to agree with the group’s high‑cooperation equilibrium and to understand that extra mechanisms exist, but they choose not to respond to individual outcomes with tailored strategic behavior. Their dominant reaction to others’ outcomes is to maintain stable, simple cooperation and favor fast, low‑effort play.

text: They pay attention to others’ outcomes and the effect of reward and punishment, and they comment on them in ways that reveal both norm concerns and payoff thinking. When others free‑ride or get heavily punished, they react with light joking mixed with normative framing (e.g., references to collective responsibility and loss of bonus). When a chronic deviator is hit with strong sanctions, they do not defend that player; instead, they emphasize the monetary consequences of such behavior (damage to the bonus), aligning more with the punishers’ perspective than with the punished. They show sensitivity to group‑level earnings: they talk about the conversion of points to money, express interest in total bonus size, and support strategies that maximize shared payoffs with minimal complexity. When someone notes extreme sanctioning of a defector, they do not question whether the punishment is excessive; instead they focus on the impact on the defector’s own outcomes and on the group’s incentives. They also engage with meta‑outcomes—how the experimenters might interpret the group’s cooperative behavior and the role of chat. They speculate that coordination and consistent cooperation are likely part of what the researchers want to observe. This indicates a higher‑order perspective: they view payoffs and outcomes not only as individual gains but as data in a study about social behavior. In sum, this player is a “non‑punitive, mostly unconditional cooperator with occasional generosity and mild opportunism,” oriented toward group efficiency, light norm enforcement via talk, and simple, stable strategies rather than complex enforcement regimes.

text: They clearly monitor others’ contributions and the group’s payoff structure. Their early and persistent max contributions align with the emerging cooperative majority. They notice and conceptually endorse the heavy punishment directed at the main defector and later personally contribute a punishment unit to that same target, suggesting approval of strong sanctions for undermining group benefits or dismissing the bonus. When a cooperator is punished, they occasionally respond with a reward, signaling concern for fairness and group cohesion. Their chat suggestion to “bully” a problematic player off to increase the multiplier shows that they not only track the payoff mechanics (fewer active players increasing per‑capita returns) but are willing to consider group-level exclusionary tactics to protect earnings. They are sensitive to time and efficiency, echoing the group’s desire to “speedrun” and to ignore more complex reward-schemes that could slow progress. They do not react emotionally to others’ heavy spending on punishment or reward, nor do they criticize these choices; instead, they quietly keep maximizing contributions and selectively align with majority sentiment against clear outliers. Overall, their type is: a steadfast, efficiency‑minded cooperator who strongly adheres to a simple “all contribute max” norm, rarely uses incentives, but eventually participates in norm enforcement against salient defectors, all while keeping communication minimal, pragmatic, and occasionally supportive of strategic group pressure.

text: They pay close attention to who contributes what and to how punishment affects payoffs for defectors. They openly note when a defector “redeems” themselves and when a frequently punished player ends up worse off than if they had simply cooperated, interpreting this as evidence that defection is dominated by cooperation once sanctions are in play. They also notice the heavy punishment inflicted on a repeatedly non-cooperating player and respond with a mixture of humor and gentle warning rather than piling on, effectively reinforcing the norm without cruelty. They comment explicitly that deviating again will hurt that player more, showing that they mentally link punishment and future payoffs as a learning mechanism. When others suggest complex reward schemes or meta-strategies, they evaluate them in terms of who would benefit if someone defects from the plan and steer the group back to the simple, robust full-contribution strategy. They are also attuned to structural aspects such as bonus thresholds and anticipated total earnings, and they reflect on whether the group’s high cooperation “breaks” the intended experimental dynamics, indicating awareness of the study’s design and incentives.

text: The player exhibits tolerance of inequality and limited behavioral retaliation. They continue to contribute generously even when low-contributing or zero-contributing peers earn substantially higher payoffs than them. This implies a relatively low propensity to respond to payoff disadvantage with sharp defection. Their small downward adjustment in contribution appears to respond to the combination of others’ low contributions and their resulting high payoffs, but the response is measured rather than punitive. They do not mirror the lowest contributors’ behavior, nor do they try to equalize payoffs by drastically reducing their own cooperation. Instead, they nudge their behavior toward more self-protection while still allowing free-riders to benefit from the public good. Because no explicit punishment or reward mechanisms are available, their only observable “response” channel is their own contribution level. They use this channel weakly and conservatively: they signal some dissatisfaction with persistent free-riding via a partial reduction in generosity, but they stop short of a full breakdown in cooperation. Their behavior suggests they may interpret others’ free-riding as disappointing but not sufficient reason to completely abandon their cooperative norm. Their beliefs about others’ intentions or types are unknown, but their actions are consistent with viewing cooperation as a value in itself, not solely as a reciprocated exchange.

text: The player observes pronounced free-riding by some others and corresponding payoff advantages for those low contributors, yet continues to contribute at a high level. They do make a modest downward adjustment after seeing clear one-sided exploitation, which indicates they notice and care about others’ behavior to some degree, but they stop well short of matching selfish play. Even when others’ higher payoffs make their own relative disadvantage salient, they do not retaliate or attempt to equalize outcomes through strategic withholding. In an environment without punishment or reward tools, they respond more by reaffirming their cooperative norm than by trying to discipline or mirror others’ strategies. Overall, they appear tolerant of inequality and exploitation, prioritizing their own commitment to cooperation over reactive responses to others’ payoffs.

text: The player appears attentive to others’ outcomes and responsive to observed payoff asymmetries. When low-contributing individuals receive comparatively high payoffs, the focal player later adopts a similar low-contribution, payoff-maximizing approach. This suggests that they interpret such payoff structures as a cue that free-riding is both feasible and normatively “permitted” in practice, even if not desirable. They do not have access to direct punishment or reward tools in this setting, so their primary means of responding to others is through their own contribution choices. Their shift away from high cooperation functions as an indirect sanction: instead of continuing to subsidize others, they withdraw support and align their behavior with the more self-interested strategies they have observed being rewarded. Their adaptation over time indicates that they update beliefs about group behavior based on outcome patterns rather than stubbornly holding to an initial strategy. They seem neither naïvely trusting nor permanently resentful; rather, they calibrate their level of cooperation to the realized incentive environment. Emotional states are unknown, but behaviorally they demonstrate a pragmatic, outcome-sensitive stance: if others benefit from contributing less, they are willing to emulate that approach, especially once future cooperation is less valuable.

text: The player is clearly sensitive to the relationship between others’ contributions and payoffs. text: The player is sensitive to how others’ contributions and payoffs evolve and updates behavior accordingly, but they do not display any direct enforcement tendencies because neither punishment nor reward is available. They seem to interpret uniform high contributions as a trustworthy environment in which full cooperation is worth sustaining. However, when others’ contributions create chances for profitable free-riding—such as when several players contribute a lot while at least one is already defecting or low-contributing—the focal player sometimes sharply reduces their own contribution. In these cases, they accept unequal outcomes where they come out ahead of others, suggesting they are comfortable benefiting from others’ cooperative efforts when there is no mechanism to sanction them. They appear to notice which roles (high contributor vs. low contributor) earn better payoffs in a given configuration and then emulate the more profitable role in subsequent decisions. They do not consistently retaliate against specific low contributors by dropping contributions whenever someone defects; instead, they respond to the overall pattern of group behavior and payoff opportunities. If, despite defections, the group as a whole still provides a substantial public fund, the player is willing to free-ride. If the overall level of contributions drops too far, they sometimes return to higher contributions when it seems that everyone is again moving toward cooperation and high payoffs. Because direct punishment and reward are absent, we cannot infer any willingness to incur personal costs to change others’ behavior; their only “sanction” is to withhold their own contribution when cooperation does not serve their self-interest. text: This player tracks others’ outcomes and responds to payoff patterns primarily by adjusting their own contribution level. They cannot use punishment or reward tools here, so their only lever is their own future behavior. When others all receive similar payoffs under high contributions, they are comfortable sustaining or returning to high contributions even if they could personally do better by free-riding. This suggests they value states where everyone earns well and relatively equally. They appear willing to accept being slightly behind some players in such cooperative states, indicating tolerance for minor advantage by others if cooperation is strong overall. When they see clear free-riding that produces a large relative gain for a low contributor, they respond by lowering their own contribution in subsequent play. This reduces the exploitable surplus and serves as an implicit form of deterrence. They do not persist with full cooperation in the face of repeated opportunism by others; instead they move toward partial contribution or full defection, thereby narrowing payoff gaps. They also sometimes exploit situations where other players continue to contribute generously despite mixed behavior in the group, choosing low or zero contributions and capturing higher payoffs themselves. In these cases they do not show compensatory “guilt” behavior afterward; instead, they revert to cooperative or experimental levels depending on how the group evolves. This indicates that their response to others’ high contributions is conditional rather than altruistic: high contributions from others are appreciated and often reciprocated, but only when combined with a generally cooperative environment or when it seems likely to produce sustained mutual benefit. The player does not exhibit clear evidence of punitive or rewarding intent beyond these contribution adjustments; because no explicit punishment or reward mechanism is available, their response to others’ outcomes remains limited to strategic adaptation. Their overarching pattern is to use their own contribution as a flexible, indirect signal and control mechanism: they increase it to support and test cooperation when payoffs look mutually beneficial, and decrease it to protect themselves and curb others’ gains when payoffs become too unequal or suggest exploitation.

text: The player’s responses to others’ outcomes are entirely behavioral, via changing their own future contributions, since no explicit punishment or reward tools are available in this setting. When all players earn similar, high payoffs, the player reinforces that state by continuing to contribute fully. They appear content when payoffs are equal and large, and their subsequent behavior supports the continuation of that pattern rather than exploiting it. This suggests they positively value mutual high outcomes and see them as stable and desirable. When others achieve higher payoffs while contributing less, the player reacts by cutting their own contribution, sometimes to zero. This is consistent with a negative reaction to being the “sucker”: they do not tolerate persistent situations where less cooperative players end up better off. Instead of directly sanctioning others (which is impossible here), they implicitly retaliate by withholding contributions so that exploiters cannot keep enjoying disproportionate benefits. Their responses to mixed group outcomes show nuance. If one player free-rides while the rest contribute high and overall payoffs remain reasonably high, they may still contribute a lot themselves. This implies a belief that a stable cooperative majority can survive a limited number of free-riders, or that the long-run gains from cooperation can outweigh short-run inequality, up to a point. However, if this imbalance persists or leads to them being relatively worse off, they later reduce their contributions. The player does not show evidence of targeting specific individuals or tracking individual histories beyond what is needed to form a general impression of “the group being cooperative” versus “the group being exploitable.” They react to the distribution of contributions and resulting payoffs at the group level, not in an individually strategic manner (e.g., they do not consistently reward specific high contributors with higher own contributions, nor do they selectively “punish” particular low contributors through tailored behavior). Their adaptation is therefore coarse-grained: group-contingent rather than partner-contingent. Emotionally or attitudinally (inferred from behavior only, with low confidence), they seem to dislike feeling taken advantage of and to value reciprocity in aggregate: they “reward” cooperative outcomes by staying cooperative and “punish” exploitative outcome patterns by becoming more self-interested. However, they do not appear vindictive or locked into defection; once the group returns to a more equal, cooperative pattern, they reliably resume high contributions, indicating that their responses to others’ outcomes are reversible and oriented toward restoring mutual cooperation rather than permanently defecting.

In mixed-contribution settings, when their own payoff falls clearly below that of free-riders or minimal contributors, they often respond by adjusting their contributions downward in later rounds, but these responses are calibrated rather than extreme. They also occasionally take advantage of situations where others are already contributing strongly, capturing a significantly higher payoff than the rest by contributing nothing. text: The player is clearly attentive to others’ actions and outcomes, as shown by how their own contributions shift after they receive large deductions or after others discuss coordinated punishment aimed at them. When heavy punishment against them spikes, they typically respond by increasing contributions toward or up to the group’s full-contribution norm, which then reduces or stops the deductions they face. When punishment pressure relaxes or chat attention moves away, they often drift back toward lower or zero contributions. This pattern indicates that they treat others’ punishment expenditures and the resulting payoff consequences as a feedback signal: they implicitly recognize that deviating from the contribution norm makes them a target and that providing more can restore more favorable treatment. They do not visibly adjust or react to how others are punished or rewarded among themselves (e.g., when other low contributors are targeted), beyond the indirect effect that those events have on group norms and threats directed at them. They do not comment on or try to influence others’ punishment choices. Overall, their main response to others’ outcomes is instrumental: they track how their own payoff changes as others spend on punishing them and then adapt contributions to balance exploitation against expected sanctions.

text: They appear largely indifferent at the behavioral level to how payoffs and punishment are distributed among others. Even when one player gains more by free riding or when others spend heavily on punishment, they do not adjust by either defecting or significantly increasing their own punishment spending. Their contributions remain stable and high despite observing that under-contributors are sometimes left unpunished and sometimes harshly punished, and despite witnessing periods in which free riders temporarily earn more. They do not join in when the group sharply escalates deductions against a known low contributor; they instead maintain their typical cooperative and mostly non-punitive pattern. This indicates a focus on their own rule (“contribute fully”) rather than responsive payoff-based strategizing. They show no visible reactive behavior to others’ individual outcomes—no opportunistic free riding when they see others punished, no retaliatory moves, and no apparent envy-driven responses when someone earns more. Their type is best characterized as a principled, steady cooperator who notes others’ outcomes but does not systematically condition their own behavior on those results.

text: This player shows no explicit emotional response to payoff disparities, even when they personally benefit from others’ higher contributions. There is no evidence of guilt, apology, anger, or moral judgment related to who earns more or less. They tolerate situations where others earn less because they cooperated more, yet do not protest when free-riders earn more. This indicates a pragmatic rather than moralistic framing of outcomes: differences in payoff appear acceptable as a consequence of individual choices. They do not call out defectors when others start free-riding, nor do they praise cooperators explicitly. Instead, they make a neutral comment about individual choice, emphasizing autonomy over sanction or approval. However, once others voice a preference for universal cooperation, they align rhetorically with that position and advocate that everyone contribute, implying that they prefer coordinated, symmetric outcomes once a cooperative standard seems realistically achievable. In summary, they accept exploitation by and of others as part of the game structure, avoid direct confrontation about unfair outcomes, and only move toward explicit pro-cooperation messaging after norms and patterns are visible and when it is consistent with their own current behavior. Their responses suggest a calculative, norm-responsive type who does not moralize outcomes but adapts to them strategically.

text: They track that some players are consistently contributing while others free-ride and adjust their own behavior with this in mind. When they themselves exploit a cooperative round, they immediately signal awareness that the situation is unfair or “messed up,” which indirectly acknowledges the payoff asymmetry between contributors and non-contributors. This reaction suggests they are sensitive to distributional fairness and reputational concerns, even when they personally benefit from the unfair outcome. When later observing that a few others continue to defect while most cooperate, they do not retaliate by reducing their own contributions. Instead, they publicly reaffirm that universal cooperation would be best, supporting the cooperative equilibrium despite persistent free-riding by specific players. They show no evidence of holding grudges or attempting to punish via reduced contributions; instead, they appear to accept some inequality in outcomes as long as the group’s overall payoff remains high. They seem more focused on reinforcing the cooperative ideal than on directly responding to specific individuals’ higher payoffs from defection.

text: The player appears tolerant of free-riding and does not visibly adjust behavior in response to payoff asymmetries. When defectors earn more, they do not express anger, call out specific individuals, or threaten to change their own contribution level. Instead, they double down on emphasizing that collective contribution increases everyone’s payoff, including their own. Their reaction to others’ higher earnings from defection is to restate the cooperative efficiency norm, indicating a focus on long-run or group-level outcomes over short-run equity enforcement. They welcome others’ verbal commitments to contribute and align with those statements, but they do not condition their own strategy on whether those commitments are actually fulfilled. With no punishment or reward tools available, they rely solely on appeals to mutual gain rather than on any form of sanctioning or selective reciprocity. Their behavioral profile suggests a stable, norm-driven cooperator who remains cooperative even in the face of exploitation and uses outcomes primarily as opportunities to reassert the benefits of full-group cooperation.

text: The player systematically benefits from others’ higher contributions and appears comfortable with payoff asymmetries that favor themselves. When others’ outcomes are worse because they contributed while the focal player did not, there is no observable adjustment toward more fairness-oriented behavior in subsequent choices; instead, the player continues to defect. They do not comment on others’ lower payoffs, do not express guilt, and do not attempt to restore balance through later contributions. They also do not react verbally when other players express that the situation is “messed up” or when disappointment about non-contributors is voiced, implying either indifference to social evaluation or a preference to remain silent rather than defend or explain their strategy. There is no sign that they derive disutility from others earning less, nor that they reward high cooperators; both punishment and reward are behaviorally absent (aside from the intrinsic effect of their own contribution decisions). Overall, they treat others’ outcomes primarily as an opportunity to improve their own payoff by exploiting persistent cooperation, rather than as feedback prompting reciprocal or norm-following behavior.
