text: The player is consistently insensitive in their actions to others’ realized payoffs. They continue free riding when contributors earn substantially less than them and also when contributors occasionally outperform them. No observable pattern indicates that seeing others “win” or “lose” relative to them changes their subsequent contribution choices. When cooperative others effectively subsidize the public good and earn lower payoffs, the player does not adjust toward contributing out of guilt, fairness, or concern about group welfare. They appear comfortable with repeated payoff asymmetries that benefit them. This suggests weak or absent inequity aversion in the direction of advantageous inequality. When their own payoff temporarily falls below that of others—most prominently when they themselves contribute while some others do not—they immediately revert back to non-contribution. This indicates an aversion to being the “sucker,” but this reaction is expressed only by stopping cooperation, not by attempting to discipline defectors (which is impossible here) or trying to coordinate more cooperation. Across a wide range of group contribution levels and group payoff distributions, their response is effectively invariant: they stay with the individually dominant free-riding strategy. They do not seem to infer or rely on any cooperative norms from the outcome patterns; even when most others behave cooperatively, they continue to exploit that cooperation rather than join it. Given that punishment and reward are not available, they cannot directly act on any desire to sanction or reward others; however, their contribution pattern suggests that even if such tools were available, their primary concern would remain avoiding personal disadvantage rather than reducing others’ advantages. In new environments with similar payoffs, they are likely to keep free riding regardless of how others are doing, and only change behavior if their own payoff is clearly and repeatedly harmed by doing so.

text: Since no punishment or reward actions occur in this environment, there is no direct evidence of how this player uses such tools. However, their contribution behavior in the face of others’ outcomes provides indirect clues. They repeatedly continue full cooperation even when others’ strategies give those others strictly higher payoffs than the focal player. They do not shift to free-riding after episodes where non-contributors earn much more than they do, nor do they appear to react to temporary improvements in group cooperation by relaxing their contributions. This suggests they are relatively insensitive to unequal payoff distributions and do not strongly prioritize correcting disadvantageous inequality through their own strategic adjustments. When others’ outcomes improve due to higher overall contributions, the player does not respond by exploiting the cooperative environment; instead, they maintain their cooperative stance, indicating that they do not opportunistically chase high relative payoffs when conditions are favorable. They also do not appear to condition on the identity of cooperators or defectors; there is no observable pattern of adjusting behavior based on which specific peers gain or lose. Their implicit response to others’ outcomes is thus characterized by persistence rather than calibration: they keep pursuing the cooperative course regardless of being exploited or rewarded by the group dynamic. Without explicit punishment/reward data, their likely stance toward such mechanisms is unknown, but their tolerance of being exploited and their lack of reactive adjustment make them more consistent with a “lenient,” non-sanctioning cooperative type than with a norm-enforcing or retributive one.

text: This player consistently uses others’ payoffs as a guide for best‑response behavior but never has access to (nor uses) punishment or reward tools, so all “response” is purely via changing their own contribution. When others who contribute heavily end up with lower payoffs than free riders, the player tends to choose defection in subsequent decisions, reinforcing a strategy that mimics the better‑off types. Conversely, when contributing yields them a payoff that is competitive with or better than what defectors get—typically when many others are also cooperating—they become more willing to cooperate again, at least temporarily. This shows they track relative, not absolute, outcomes: the key comparison is “how am I doing versus those who didn’t contribute?” They do not appear motivated to protect or reward consistent cooperators; instead they systematically exploit them. Even when some peers repeatedly sacrifice and end up clearly worse off, the player does not shift toward supporting them; rather, they keep their own contribution at zero while those cooperators accumulate lower payoffs. This suggests little concern for equity or for “helping the helpers.” They also do not appear to be deterred or morally moved by seeing a single player repeatedly harmed by others’ free riding. When one peer contributes alone and is repeatedly left with much less than everyone else, the focal player still free rides in many of those instances, confirming that empathy or guilt, if present, is not strong enough to override payoff considerations. Overall, this player uses others’ outcomes to refine a self‑interested best‑response rule: copy the payoff‑dominant behavior pattern and avoid being on the exploited side. There is no evidence of altruistic punishment, positive reciprocity, or norm enforcement—only adaptive, outcome‑based calibration of a primarily selfish strategy.

text: This player is highly sensitive to payoff structures and others’ outcomes, but they do not have any tools to directly modify those outcomes (no punishment or reward in this environment). Their responses therefore show up as indirect, via their own subsequent contribution choices. They repeatedly observe situations where some peers earn substantially less because they contribute while others free ride and profit more. They are willing to occupy both roles over time: sometimes joining the lower-payoff contributor group, sometimes joining the higher-payoff free riders. This indicates that they do not hold a rigid preference for always being on the advantaged side, but they are clearly comfortable taking that advantaged position when the opportunity presents itself. There is no evidence of strict reciprocity in response to others’ payoffs. They continue to cooperate in settings where non-contributors earn more than contributors, which suggests they do not insist that others’ outcomes be “fair” before they contribute again. Instead, they seem to accept persistent inequality and keep using contribution as a tool to maintain a reasonably high group return that they can periodically exploit. Their behavior does not show clear punishment-by-exit or “grim trigger” reactions: they do not switch permanently to defection after seeing unfavorable distributions. Nor do they attempt to “reward” high contributors by staying in a consistently cooperative stance. Instead, they oscillate between roles, implying that others’ outcomes inform them about the feasibility of ongoing cooperation (i.e., whether enough contributors remain to make the game worthwhile), but do not trigger strong moralistic or retributive responses. They appear goal-oriented toward maximizing their own cumulative payoff under a realistic expectation that some players will chronically exploit the group. In response, they adopt a mixed pattern: contributing enough to keep the public good from collapsing when many others are also contributing, and free riding enough to capture extra surplus. Their reaction to others’ earnings is thus instrumental rather than emotional: they adapt their strategy to the overall level of group cooperation, not to the precise fairness of each payoff profile.

text: They monitor others’ contributions and punishment outcomes closely and use this information to update their social stance. When someone is punished very severely, they react with brief commentary that indicates approval of the deterrent effect rather than sympathy for the punished. They seem to enjoy the dramatic aspect of “destroying” a persistent defector, interpreting extreme group sanctions as appropriate for someone who keeps testing the norm. They also recognize and lightly joke about the experimental nature of the environment and about the possibility of bots, which shows they contextualize outcomes as part of a broader structure rather than solely personal conflicts. Overall, they side with norm-abiding, high-contribution players and treat punishment-heavy outcomes for defectors as justified and even satisfying, provided they reinforce long-run full cooperation.

text: They pay attention to others’ contributions and to the pattern of punishment and outcomes, and they use this information to reinforce norms. They do not criticize heavy punishers; instead, they often align with them verbally and behaviorally by adding their own smaller punishments. They appear comfortable with escalating collective punishment against a perceived “bot” or incorrigible player and reframe these extreme outcomes as part of the experiment rather than as unfair. They do not express sympathy for punished low contributors, and they show no sign of wanting to reduce punishment once a target’s payoff is already very low; as long as someone keeps deviating, further punishment seems acceptable or even entertaining. They also use others’ outcomes to update beliefs (e.g., interpreting irrational persistence as evidence of a bot or experimental manipulation), but this does not alter their own cooperative or punitive strategy.

When partners’ contributions move upward or show more balance, the player often responds by increasing their own contribution again. The player thus reacts to improved group cooperation with renewed generosity, rather than “holding a grudge” for earlier exploitation. The player does not show an outcome-maximizing, purely self-interested pattern (they do not converge to minimal contribution despite repeated opportunities to earn more by free-riding). Because there is no punishment or reward mechanism in this environment, there are no observed explicit costs spent on sanctioning or rewarding others. The player’s only lever of response to others’ outcomes is their own contribution level, which they use as an implicit signal and self-protection device: contributions decrease after instances of exploitation and increase when mutual cooperation looks more promising.
